During a Lean-Agile transformation, a consultant lists the guiding principles of SAFe for the leadership team. One item on the list is actually contrary to SAFe thinking. Which option is NOT a SAFe principle?

correct.text: Maximize the utilization of every team member to keep everyone fully busy and improve efficiency
correct.why: SAFe explicitly cautions against high utilization because it lengthens queues and slows flow, so this is not a SAFe principle.

correct.text: Keep all team members loaded to full capacity so no one is ever idle during an iteration
correct.why: Driving everyone to full utilization contradicts SAFe's guidance to manage queues and optimize flow, so it is not a principle.

correct.text: Push resource utilization as high as possible to extract maximum output from the team
correct.why: SAFe favors flow and an economic view over utilization, making maximum-utilization the option that is not a SAFe principle.

distractors.text: Apply cadence and synchronize planning across teams and domains so dependencies stay aligned
distractors.trap: May look unfamiliar, but this is an actual SAFe principle about alignment across teams and functions.

distractors.text: Unlock the intrinsic motivation of knowledge workers by minimizing constraints and building autonomy
distractors.trap: Seems soft or people-focused, leading some to wrongly exclude it even though it is a SAFe principle.

distractors.text: Decentralize decision making so that frequent, time-critical choices are made closer to the work
distractors.trap: Can appear too broad to be formal guidance, but it is a core SAFe principle.

distractors.text: Take an economic view when sequencing work and making trade-off decisions about cost and value
distractors.trap: May be overlooked because it sounds strategic rather than agile, but it is a foundational SAFe principle.

distractors.text: Assume variability and preserve options throughout the development effort rather than committing early
distractors.trap: Sounds like generic advice, yet it is a genuine SAFe principle about set-based design and deferred commitment.

distractors.text: Build incrementally with fast, integrated learning cycles that run continuously across the entire value stream.
distractors.trap: Looks like a paraphrase, but this is an authentic SAFe principle about incremental delivery and learning.

distractors.text: Base milestones on objective evaluation of working systems rather than relying on phase-gate documents
distractors.trap: Easy to dismiss as too specific, but it is a real SAFe principle about milestone evaluation.

distractors.text: Visualize and limit the work in process, reduce batch sizes, and actively manage queue lengths to keep the flow healthy.
distractors.trap: Reads like a Kanban tip, yet it is a core SAFe principle for keeping flow healthy.

explanation: SAFe treats high utilization as harmful to flow, so maximizing every member's utilization is the statement that is NOT a SAFe principle, while the others are genuine principles.

topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: A project manager is preparing a funding request for a manufacturing firm's warehouse automation initiative. Several team leads have offered rough top-down figures, but the organization wants a budget that reflects real delivery capacity because engineers are splitting time across multiple active efforts. What is the best way for the project manager to develop the cost estimate?

An IT project manager must submit a budget for a data-center migration. Managers have provided quick ballpark numbers, yet leadership wants an estimate that reflects how much time the shared platform engineers can truly devote, since they are assigned to several projects at once. How should the project manager build the estimate?

A project manager at a hospital is funding a new clinical-records rollout. Department heads have given high-level cost guesses, but the sponsor insists the budget mirror actual delivery capacity because the analysts are stretched across parallel programs. What is the best approach to develop the cost estimate?

correct.text: Build the estimate from individual task estimates provided by the people doing the work, adjusting for each contributor's real availability and workload
correct.why: Bottom-up estimating informed by the team's current capacity produces the most reliable cost view for planning.

correct.text: Aggregate detailed task-level estimates from the team and factor in how much time each person can commit given their competing assignments
correct.why: Rolling up capacity-adjusted task estimates reflects real delivery ability rather than optimistic top-down guesses.

correct.text: Develop a bottom-up cost estimate grounded in the team's own task estimates and their current capacity
correct.why: Estimating from the actual work and available capacity yields the accuracy the organization is asking for.

distractors.text: Use analogous estimating drawn from similar past projects to produce the budget figure and submit it to leadership ahead of the funding review deadline.
distractors.trap: Chooses speed over accuracy and ignores the current team workload differences that materially affect cost.

distractors.text: Set the budget to match the portfolio funding ceiling already allocated, then ask the delivery teams to trim and align their planned scope to fit within that fixed amount.
distractors.trap: Starts from a financial target rather than estimating the work actually needed to deliver the required outcomes.

distractors.text: Ask each functional manager for a single rolled-up estimate covering their whole department, and then combine those departmental totals into the overall project budget figure.
distractors.trap: Aggregates high-level departmental guesses instead of developing estimates from the actual tasks and the assigned contributors.

distractors.text: Estimate the highest-risk work packages in detail and apply a standard percentage uplift across the rest of the remaining work.
distractors.trap: Uses partial detail and generic padding rather than comprehensive task-based estimating.

distractors.text: Base the budget on the team's velocity from the last initiative and roll it forward without reviewing how their current assignments have changed.
distractors.trap: Misapplies agile concepts by assuming historical throughput remains valid despite changed workload and context.

distractors.text: Have the project sponsor set the total figure from the strategic value of the initiative and then ask the delivery team to fit the required outcome within that predetermined number.
distractors.trap: Anchors the budget to a desired investment level instead of the effort the work genuinely requires.

distractors.text: Average the rough top-down figures the team leads already provided and then add a fixed contingency percentage across the total to cover the remaining uncertainty in the plan.
distractors.trap: Blends rough guesses and blanket padding rather than estimating from real tasks and available capacity.

distractors.text: Use parametric estimating by multiplying an industry-standard unit cost across the full measured scope of the automation initiative to produce the budget figure.
distractors.trap: Relies on a generic cost model that ignores the team's specific and unevenly loaded delivery capacity.

explanation: When accuracy and real delivery capacity matter, a bottom-up estimate built from task-level input and adjusted for each contributor's availability is the most reliable basis for the budget.

prompts: After contract signature, a vendor delivers a milestone that meets contract terms but does not meet the customer's actual need. What is the MOST appropriate response?

A supplier submits a deliverable that fully satisfies the written statement of work, yet it does not solve the business problem the customer actually had. The contract is already in force. What should the project manager do next?

During procurement execution, a contractor hands over work that conforms to every clause in the signed agreement but falls short of what the organization truly needs. How should the project manager respond?

correct.text: Accept the deliverable per the contract terms, then engage the vendor to close the gap, potentially through a formal change order
correct.why: Contractual conformance and value-to-business can differ; the gap is addressed through change orders or new procurement, not by breaching the contract.

correct.text: Formally accept what conforms to the contract and open a change request with the vendor to address the unmet business need
correct.why: The signed contract has been met, so the remaining value gap is handled through the change-control and procurement process.

correct.text: Honor the existing contract by accepting the milestone, then pursue a change order or new procurement to obtain the missing value
correct.why: Accepting conforming work and separately negotiating the gap respects the agreement while still pursuing the needed outcome.

distractors.text: Begin a new procurement for a supplier that can meet the underlying business need and plan an orderly transition off the current contract
distractors.trap: Abandoning a contract the vendor has satisfied is disproportionate and ignores the change-order path.

distractors.text: Accept the deliverable as contractually complete and continue with the remaining project work without opening any change with the vendor
distractors.trap: Silently accepting an unmet need abandons a responsibility the project manager should own.

distractors.text: Decline to accept the milestone and withhold payment until the vendor delivers something that meets the actual business need
distractors.trap: Declining conforming work sidesteps the contract and a responsibility the PM should manage properly.

distractors.text: Withhold acceptance and require the vendor to rework the milestone at no additional cost until it satisfies the underlying business need
distractors.trap: Requires free rework beyond the agreed scope, which the contract does not entitle the buyer to demand.

distractors.text: Escalate to the legal department to determine whether the contract can be voided for failing to deliver the intended business value
distractors.trap: Treats a value gap as a legal breach even though the vendor met the terms that were actually agreed.

distractors.text: Reopen the full contract for renegotiation, shifting the acceptance basis from the written specifications toward the broader business needs
distractors.trap: A full renegotiation is disproportionate when a targeted change order can address the specific gap.

explanation: Because conformance to contract and value to the business can diverge, the PM accepts the conforming deliverable and addresses the remaining need through a change order or new procurement rather than breaching the agreement.

prompts: A pharmaceutical company is preparing a new packaging line for a time-sensitive product launch. A regulatory milestone was moved forward, and leadership asks the project manager to recover 10 business days without reducing scope. After reviewing the schedule network, the project manager finds that several validation activities currently planned in sequence could begin once partial outputs from equipment setup are available, though this would require tighter coordination and increase rework risk. What should the project manager do next?

A construction project manager is told to pull a fixed inspection date two weeks earlier without cutting scope. Studying the schedule network, the PM sees that some finishing activities now planned in sequence could start once partial structural work is complete, though overlapping them would demand closer coordination and raise the chance of rework. What should the PM do next?

An aerospace program manager must recover 10 business days after a certification date was advanced, and scope cannot change. Reviewing the network diagram, the PM notices several test activities scheduled back-to-back could overlap once early hardware outputs exist, at the cost of tighter coordination and more rework risk. What is the best next step?

correct.text: Evaluate the affected dependencies and risks, then revise the schedule to overlap the appropriate activities with stakeholder agreement and monitoring controls
correct.why: This applies schedule compression by fast tracking only after confirming logical relationships and managing the added risk.

correct.text: Confirm the logical relationships and rework exposure, then fast track by overlapping the suitable activities under tighter coordination and controls
correct.why: Analyzing the network first ensures the overlap is feasible before compressing the schedule without cutting scope.

correct.text: Analyze which activities can safely run in parallel and adjust the schedule to overlap them while actively managing the increased risk
correct.why: Fast tracking is appropriate here, but only after validating dependencies and putting risk controls in place.

distractors.text: Add extra resources to the critical path activities to shorten their durations and recover the requested date
distractors.trap: Reaches for crashing before checking whether logic-based overlap can recover the time more cheaply.

distractors.text: Approve overtime for the team while keeping the existing activity sequence and network dependencies unchanged
distractors.trap: Treats schedule pressure as a staffing matter without first optimizing the activity logic.

distractors.text: Update and re-baseline the schedule to the advanced milestone date first, then work with each team lead to redistribute the ten business days across their activities.
distractors.trap: Re-baselines to the new date before analyzing whether the compression is actually feasible.

distractors.text: Move the lower-priority deliverables out of the current release and defer them to a later project phase, holding the advanced milestone date with a reduced project scope.
distractors.trap: Reduces the project scope rather than compressing the schedule while holding the agreed scope constant.

distractors.text: Escalate the sponsor's recovery request to the PMO and place it on the agenda for the next scheduled governance review before altering the current project schedule at this stage
distractors.trap: Routes the request through governance review before first analyzing the viable schedule options the PM can already assess.

distractors.text: Crash the critical path by assigning additional engineers and contract staff across the remaining validation tasks to buy back the ten business days more quickly
distractors.trap: Jumps to broad crashing before checking whether targeted, logic-based overlap can recover the time.

distractors.text: Bring in an outside scheduling consultant to review the schedule network independently before the project manager decides how to recover the ten requested business days
distractors.trap: Adds delay and overhead when the PM already has the network detail needed to analyze the overlap.

distractors.text: Negotiate with the regulator to move the advanced milestone back toward its original date, preserving the current activity sequence without any schedule compression at all
distractors.trap: Sidesteps the compression request from leadership instead of first analyzing the viable schedule options.

explanation: The right next step is to analyze dependencies and risk, then fast track by overlapping suitable activities with stakeholder buy-in and monitoring, compressing the schedule without cutting scope.

prompts: In an agile project, why should a developer suggest a new requirement to the product owner instead of implementing it immediately?
During a Scrum project, why is it better for a tester who spots a useful new feature to raise it with the product owner rather than building it right away?
On an agile team, a designer thinks of an enhancement mid-sprint. Why should they take the idea to the product owner instead of adding it themselves?

correct.text: It ensures the product owner can evaluate the requirement's fit with project goals and prioritize it accordingly
correct.why: The product owner is accountable for ordering work based on value, goals, and priorities rather than allowing unreviewed implementation.

correct.text: It lets the product owner weigh the idea's value against other backlog items before any work begins
correct.why: Backlog ordering is a value trade-off that belongs to the product owner.

correct.text: It keeps backlog ordering with the accountable product owner rather than an individual developer
correct.why: Prioritization authority rests with the product owner, not the team member who raised the idea.

distractors.text: It makes it considerably more likely that the newly suggested requirement will be scheduled and then delivered in the very next iteration.
distractors.trap: Assumes that raising a requirement commits the team to near-term delivery rather than to backlog evaluation by the owner.

distractors.text: It prevents the developer from wasting time building tasks that may later turn out to be unnecessary or of low value to the product.
distractors.trap: Focuses narrowly on developer efficiency and ignores product ownership, value assessment, and backlog prioritization.

distractors.text: It lets the developer proceed based on their own judgment of the requirement's value.
distractors.trap: Contradicts agile product ownership by implying unilateral scope decisions by the developer.

distractors.text: It gives requirements raised by technical experts priority over other backlog items.
distractors.trap: Assumes technical expertise overrides business value and backlog governance.

distractors.text: It allows the Scrum Master to slot the requirement into the current sprint so the team can begin refining and estimating it during the next daily standup
distractors.trap: The Scrum Master facilitates but does not own backlog priority; ordering is the product owner's responsibility.

distractors.text: It guarantees the requirement will be recorded in the scope baseline and formally approved through the integrated change control process before work starts
distractors.trap: This applies heavyweight predictive change control to an adaptive backlog where reprioritization is expected and lightweight.

distractors.text: It shifts responsibility for the requirement onto the product owner so the development team cannot be blamed if the feature later proves unnecessary
distractors.trap: Frames the handoff as blame-avoidance rather than value-based prioritization by the accountable owner.

distractors.text: It creates a documented entry in the requirements traceability matrix so auditors can later confirm the feature was formally requested and authorized
distractors.trap: Traceability governance is not the reason an agile developer routes an idea to the product owner.

explanation: New ideas go to the product owner because that role is accountable for evaluating value and ordering the backlog, not because raising an idea commits the team to build it.

prompts: A project manager is leading a hospital equipment rollout across multiple clinics. Midway through deployment, site technicians report that every device activation now requires a new cybersecurity handshake introduced by the enterprise architecture group. Installations are backing up, but no one is sure whether the real problem is the approval path, the technical sequence, or how the new control is being applied in the field. What should the project manager do first?

A project manager overseeing a regional smart-meter installation finds that a newly imposed utility authentication step is slowing every meter activation, and no one can say whether the approval workflow, the technical steps, or the way crews apply the step is to blame. What should the manager do first?

Midway through a bank's branch-wide POS terminal upgrade, a new fraud-check step from the security team is stalling activations, but the source of the backlog is unclear. What is the project manager's best first move?

correct.text: Meet with the technology leaders and site technicians to understand how the control is being applied and pinpoint the source of the delay
correct.why: The project manager should first understand the implementation details and root cause so the response addresses the real issue rather than a symptom.

correct.text: Bring the technicians and the architecture group together to trace where activations are actually stalling before acting
correct.why: Locating the true bottleneck first prevents fixing the wrong thing.

correct.text: Investigate the approval path, technical sequence, and field application to isolate the true bottleneck first
correct.why: Identifying which of the three candidate causes is responsible drives an effective response.

distractors.text: Escalate the matter to the executive sponsor and request a formal exception to the new cybersecurity control for all of the affected device activations.
distractors.trap: Assumes the control itself is the cause before validating what is actually slowing the activations.

distractors.text: Compress the remaining schedule by bringing on additional field technicians to recover the installation time already lost.
distractors.trap: Treats the slowdown as a capacity problem without confirming what is actually constraining progress.

distractors.text: Ask the enterprise architecture group to temporarily relax the new authentication step for the field crews while the installations catch up.
distractors.trap: Adjusts the control before confirming it is the actual source of the backlog.

distractors.text: Update the project risk register and extend the projected rollout dates across all of the remaining clinic sites to reflect the emerging delay.
distractors.trap: Records and re-baselines the impact before investigating the issue and exploring response options.

distractors.text: Hold the enterprise architecture group accountable for the delays and require them to redesign the authentication protocol for the rollout.
distractors.trap: Jumps to blame and a fixed solution without first understanding how the process is functioning in practice.

distractors.text: Draft a formal change request to remove the cybersecurity handshake from the device activation workflow and route it through the enterprise change control board.
distractors.trap: Pushes a specific solution through governance before the actual cause of the backlog has been confirmed.

distractors.text: Reassign the most experienced installers to the slowest-performing clinics to lift overall activation throughput.
distractors.trap: Reshuffling resources treats a symptom and defers diagnosing the real constraint that is stalling activations.

distractors.text: Notify each clinic that its go-live date will slip and begin negotiating revised acceptance milestones with the various site leadership teams.
distractors.trap: Managing the optics of slippage skips the step of investigating and resolving the underlying delay.

distractors.text: Ask the quality and compliance team to run a formal root-cause analysis of the activation process and report back on the main contributing factors.
distractors.trap: Delegating to a formal analysis exercise is heavier and slower than the PM directly locating the bottleneck with those involved.

explanation: When the cause of a slowdown is genuinely unclear, the project manager should first investigate how the new control is being applied to identify the root cause before selecting any response.

topic: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/Methods and Practices
prompts: Which of the following is the best reason to use a burndown chart: to predict when project work will be completed, to create a project charter, to establish a backlog for project deliverables, or to determine how much risk is still associated with the project?
On an agile team, what is the main purpose of maintaining a burndown chart during a sprint?
A Scrum team reviews its burndown chart each day. What is the chart primarily meant to tell them?

correct.text: To predict when the project's remaining work will be completed
correct.why: A burndown chart tracks remaining work over time so the team can forecast likely completion based on current progress.

correct.text: To forecast a likely finish date from the team's current burn rate
correct.why: The slope of remaining work extrapolates to an expected completion point.

correct.text: To show how much work is left and project when it will run out
correct.why: Remaining effort trending to zero indicates the anticipated end of the work.

distractors.text: To formally create the project charter that authorizes the project and names its manager
distractors.trap: Confuses an agile progress-tracking chart with a project initiation artifact.

distractors.text: To establish a backlog for project deliverables
distractors.trap: Confuses backlog creation and prioritization with monitoring progress against remaining work.

distractors.text: To determine how much risk is still associated with the project
distractors.trap: Assumes burndown charts are risk-analysis tools rather than work-progress forecasting tools.

distractors.text: To assign individual team members to the specific sprint tasks that they will each own
distractors.trap: Mistakes a visual progress metric for a staffing and allocation mechanism.

distractors.text: To capture the full list of prioritized product features the team has committed to deliver over the entire release
distractors.trap: Describes the product or release backlog, not the progress-versus-remaining-work view a burndown chart provides.

distractors.text: To record how much of the budget has been spent compared with the value of the work completed to date
distractors.trap: That is earned value cost tracking, which a burndown chart of remaining effort does not perform.

distractors.text: To schedule which team member is responsible for each task during the upcoming iteration planning session
distractors.trap: Assignment of ownership happens in planning, not in a chart meant to forecast completion of remaining work.

distractors.text: To measure the total number of defects discovered during testing so quality trends can be reported to stakeholders
distractors.trap: Defect trends are shown on quality charts, not on a burndown chart that plots remaining work.

explanation: A burndown chart plots remaining work against time so the team can forecast when the work will most likely be completed.

topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: A project manager is leading a global ERP deployment and wants to forecast total cost at completion using earned value data. The project has a budget at completion of 900,000. Actual cost to date is 360,000, planned value is 500,000, and earned value is 400,000. The project manager expects the current schedule efficiency to continue for the remaining work. What is the EAC based on applying SPI to the remaining work?

A project manager running a hospital billing-system implementation has a BAC of 900,000, actual cost of 360,000, planned value of 500,000, and earned value of 400,000, and expects current schedule efficiency to continue. What is the EAC when SPI is applied to the remaining work?

A telecom network-upgrade project shows BAC 900,000, AC 360,000, PV 500,000, and EV 400,000, with the present schedule performance assumed to hold. Compute the EAC by applying SPI to the work that remains.

correct.text: Compute SPI as EV divided by PV, which is 0.8, then apply EAC equals AC plus BAC minus EV divided by SPI to get 985,000
correct.why: This correctly applies schedule performance only to the remaining work: 360,000 + (900,000 - 400,000)/0.8 = 985,000.

correct.text: Divide the remaining work of BAC minus EV by the 0.8 SPI and add the actual cost, yielding 985,000
correct.why: The remaining-work forecast (500,000/0.8) plus AC gives the same 985,000.

correct.text: Carry the current 0.8 schedule performance into the open work using EAC equals AC plus open work over SPI, giving 985,000
correct.why: Only the unfinished work is inflated by the schedule factor, producing 985,000.

distractors.text: Use the cost-based formula EAC equals BAC divided by CPI, which gives 810,000, treating the overrun as a cost-efficiency problem
distractors.trap: It uses CPI-based forecasting even though the question explicitly says to apply SPI to the remaining work.

distractors.text: Add AC to BAC minus EV with no efficiency factor, giving 860,000, assuming the remaining work proceeds exactly as originally planned
distractors.trap: It ignores the schedule performance factor and assumes future work will be perfectly on plan.

distractors.text: Divide the entire BAC by the SPI of 0.8 to get 1,125,000, applying the schedule factor across all project work
distractors.trap: It applies SPI to the whole project instead of only to the remaining work.

distractors.text: Calculate EAC as AC plus BAC minus EV divided by both CPI and SPI together, producing 1,012,500 for the forecast
distractors.trap: It uses a combined performance formula that the scenario does not support.

distractors.text: Subtract AC from BAC and divide that result by the SPI to arrive at 675,000, then report that figure to leadership as the project's estimate at completion.
distractors.trap: Confuses estimate-to-complete logic with estimate-at-completion and omits the proper treatment of actual cost already spent.

distractors.text: Treat BAC itself as the estimate at completion because the approved baseline budget already represents the intended total, reporting 900,000 with no performance adjustment
distractors.trap: It ignores current schedule performance entirely and simply restates the original budget.

distractors.text: Add the schedule variance of negative 100,000 to the BAC to adjust the forecast, arriving at an EAC of 1,000,000 for the project
distractors.trap: It folds a one-time variance into the forecast instead of applying the SPI ratio to the remaining work.

distractors.text: Multiply the remaining work of 500,000 by the SPI of 0.8 and add the actual cost, producing 760,000 as the estimate at completion
distractors.trap: It multiplies remaining work by SPI instead of dividing, understating the forecast.

explanation: When schedule efficiency is expected to continue, EAC equals AC plus (BAC minus EV) divided by SPI, applying the 0.8 factor to only the remaining work: 985,000.

prompts: A project manager is preparing the approach for handling uncertainty on a large data center migration before detailed risks are collected. Which statement correctly describes what belongs in the risk management plan?

Early in a hospital IT rollout, a project manager is defining how the team will deal with uncertainty before any specific risks have been logged. What content properly belongs in the risk management plan?
A project manager on a new product development effort is drafting the risk approach during planning, ahead of gathering individual risks. Which item correctly belongs in the risk management plan?

correct.text: The methodology, roles, and procedures the team will use to identify, analyze, and respond to risks
correct.why: The risk management plan defines the methodology, roles, procedures, and categories for how risk work will be done.

correct.text: The overall approach, categories, and thresholds that will govern how risk work is carried out
correct.why: The plan sets the approach, categories, and thresholds that govern risk activities rather than listing specific risks.

correct.text: The defined process and responsibilities for how the team will manage risk throughout the project
correct.why: It documents the process and responsibilities for managing risk, not the individual risks themselves.

distractors.text: A running, continuously updated list of each identified risk with its assigned owner and current status
distractors.trap: Confuses the risk management plan with the risk register, which stores the individual risks.

distractors.text: The approved workaround that was applied to a risk event which has already occurred on this project
distractors.trap: Mistakes issue response information for planning guidance on how risk will be managed.

distractors.text: The final distribution of the contingency reserve allocated across every work package in the schedule
distractors.trap: Assumes the plan contains finalized detailed allocations instead of the strategy for reserves and thresholds.

distractors.text: The detailed lessons learned from prior projects about defects that were found during system testing
distractors.trap: Confuses historical knowledge assets with the current project's risk management plan.

distractors.text: A prioritized ranking of individual threats and opportunities with their probability and impact scores
distractors.trap: That analysis output lives in the risk register after assessment, not in the plan that defines the method.

distractors.text: The detailed response strategies assigned to each specific high-priority risk that has been identified
distractors.trap: Individual response plans are register content; the management plan only sets how responses are developed.

distractors.text: A signed agreement from stakeholders formally accepting the current level of residual risk on the project
distractors.trap: Risk acceptance records are outputs of executing the process, not the planning document that guides it.

explanation: The risk management plan describes how risk work will be conducted; individual risks and their details belong in the risk register.

topic: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/Deliverables
prompts: A manufacturing project is under pressure to accelerate delivery. The team suggests reducing supplier audits and environmental verification testing to save time and money, arguing the deliverable will still function. What should the project manager do?

A pharmaceutical project is behind schedule, and the team proposes skipping several compliance inspections and safety verification tests, claiming the product will work regardless. How should the project manager respond?

To hit an aggressive launch date, a consumer electronics team wants to drop regulatory certification checks and supplier quality audits, insisting the device functions fine without them. What is the best action?

correct.text: Maintain the defined quality and compliance activities rather than cutting the prevention and appraisal work
correct.why: The prevention and appraisal work being cut is part of cost of quality, so removing it raises failure costs, rework, and penalties later.

correct.text: Keep the planned supplier audits and environmental verification testing in place as originally defined
correct.why: Eliminating audits and verification trades minor short-term savings for far larger downstream defect and compliance costs.

correct.text: Preserve the agreed prevention and appraisal activities and continue the required compliance verification
correct.why: Dropping prevention and appraisal activities increases the likelihood of expensive post-delivery failures and penalties.

distractors.text: Accept the shortcut because the cost of quality mainly concerns technical defects rather than sustainability and compliance criteria
distractors.trap: Misunderstands quality as largely excluding environmental and regulatory requirements.

distractors.text: Replace the supplier audits with a team verbal agreement to act responsibly so the delivery schedule can be preserved
distractors.trap: Substitutes informal intent for defined quality assurance and control activities.

distractors.text: Move the sustainability checks to project closure since they are not strictly needed to produce the functioning deliverables
distractors.trap: Confuses acceptance and closure with in-process quality management.

distractors.text: Remove the sustainability criteria from the acceptance thresholds because the sponsor is prioritizing speed over verification
distractors.trap: Allows stakeholder pressure to override approved quality and compliance requirements.

distractors.text: Transfer this decision entirely to the procurement manager, since supplier sustainability is largely a sourcing matter rather than a project quality concern
distractors.trap: Artificially separates procurement decisions from product and deliverable quality management.

distractors.text: Reduce the audits now but schedule a single comprehensive compliance review just before final delivery to catch any issues
distractors.trap: A late catch-all review is appraisal too late to prevent the defects that in-process controls are meant to stop.

distractors.text: Perform a quick cost-benefit analysis and cut the environmental tests that appear least likely to reveal a real problem
distractors.trap: Selectively dropping required verification still weakens compliance and gambles with regulatory and failure costs.

distractors.text: Escalate the decision to the sponsor and let leadership decide whether speed outweighs the defined quality obligations
distractors.trap: Approved compliance requirements are not the PM's to trade away for schedule; escalation here abdicates quality accountability.

explanation: Sustainability-related prevention and appraisal activities are part of cost of quality, so cutting them raises later failure and compliance costs.

prompts: A project team has added functionality that was not part of the original design. Why is it important to remove that functionality?
During development, engineers build several features that were never in the approved requirements. Why should the project manager have this extra functionality removed?
A team delivers a module containing capabilities that no one requested and that fall outside the baselined design. Why is removing this unrequested work important?

correct.text: It keeps the project aligned to the approved scope and ensures the team adheres to the baselined design specifications
correct.why: Removing unapproved functionality prevents scope creep and keeps deliverables aligned to the approved baseline.

correct.text: It preserves the integrity of the scope baseline and prevents unapproved work from creeping into the deliverables
correct.why: Protecting the scope baseline stops unauthorized work from expanding the project's deliverables.

correct.text: It maintains control over what the project delivers by holding the team to the originally approved requirements
correct.why: Holding the team to approved requirements maintains control over what the project actually delivers.

distractors.text: It prevents the overall project from exceeding both its approved budget and its formally committed delivery timeline
distractors.trap: Treats cost and schedule protection as the primary reason while overlooking that the core issue is unauthorized scope.

distractors.text: It ensures that all of the team members end up feeling personally satisfied with the individual contributions that each of them made to the build.
distractors.trap: Confuses scope governance with protecting team morale and individual ownership of the work produced.

distractors.text: It allows for much greater flexibility during project execution and actively encourages the team to keep pursuing further ongoing innovation.
distractors.trap: Incorrectly frames removing unauthorized work as a way to promote adding still more unapproved changes.

distractors.text: It avoids the need for essentially any future change requests related to the evolving design of the finished product
distractors.trap: Assumes eliminating one scope issue removes the formal change control process going forward.

distractors.text: It guarantees that the customer will accept the final delivered product without raising any additional concerns at the handover
distractors.trap: Removing gold-plating supports acceptance but does not guarantee sign-off, which depends on meeting agreed criteria.

distractors.text: It ensures the extra features can later be repackaged and sold to the client as a separate formally approved change request
distractors.trap: The reason to remove unauthorized work is scope control, not to stage a future upsell of the same features.

distractors.text: It reduces the amount of testing and documentation that the quality assurance team must complete before the release date
distractors.trap: Less test effort is a side effect, not the governing reason; the core issue is unapproved scope, not workload.

distractors.text: It confirms that the project manager retains full authority over every technical decision the developers make during the build
distractors.trap: Scope governance concerns the baseline, not asserting control over routine technical choices within scope.

explanation: Removing unauthorized functionality controls scope creep and keeps the deliverable aligned to the approved design baseline.

prompts: A project manager is leading a hospital network's patient portal upgrade. The first release was intentionally limited so clinics could start using secure appointment requests while other capabilities are still being built. After seeing the release notes, the chief medical officer complains that the portal is missing prescription renewal and test result features and demands that the next release date remain unchanged. The delivery team says adding those capabilities by that date would require removing planned testing and training. What should the project manager do next?

A project manager is delivering a bank's mobile app in phased releases; the first phase deliberately shipped only balance checks and transfers. A senior vice president reviews the plan, insists loan applications and card controls must be in the next release, and refuses to move the announced launch date. Engineering warns that meeting the date would mean cutting security testing and user onboarding. What should the project manager do next?

A project manager runs a university's incremental rollout of a new student portal, where the initial release covered only course registration so departments could begin using it. The provost objects that financial aid and transcript features are missing and insists the next release date stay fixed. The team responds that including them on time would force them to drop regression testing and staff training. What should the project manager do next?

A project manager oversees a manufacturer's staged ERP deployment; the first release intentionally delivered only order entry. The operations director demands that inventory and invoicing modules ship in the next release without changing the committed go-live date, but the team says doing so would require eliminating integration testing and end-user training. What should the project manager do next?

correct.text: Review the release objectives, constraints, and stakeholder expectations with the team, then adjust the upcoming release timeline to reflect a shared understanding of scope and readiness.
correct.why: This aligns expectations to delivery reality before committing to a date that may undermine value or quality.

correct.text: Facilitate a working session with the delivery team and the executive to reconcile the requested scope against the date, and reset the release plan around what can be delivered with acceptable quality.
correct.why: Bringing both sides together surfaces tradeoffs and produces an informed, agreed plan rather than a forced one.

correct.text: Bring the team and the stakeholders together to weigh the desired features against schedule and readiness, then agree on a realistic release date that still protects planned testing and training.
correct.why: A jointly agreed date preserves quality safeguards while responding to the stakeholder's priorities.

distractors.text: Work with the delivery team to add the requested prescription renewal and test result features within the current release date by applying schedule compression such as approved overtime and added weekend capacity.
distractors.trap: Commits to compressing the schedule before confirming feasibility and the impact on testing and training.

distractors.text: Escalate the executive's demand to the project sponsor and ask the sponsor to decide whether the requested features should be added to the next release or deferred to a later release along with the reasons.
distractors.trap: Jumps to escalation before facilitating shared understanding and an informed adjustment.

distractors.text: Reaffirm the current release scope and date exactly as originally planned, and let the executive know that the committed release plan already communicated to the clinics will be delivered in full as it currently stands.
distractors.trap: Treats the plan as fixed even though stakeholder expectations and delivery realities have changed.

distractors.text: Move the release date out by about a month and have the team replan the remaining work along with the affected downstream milestones and dependencies, then share the fully revised schedule with the stakeholders once it is complete.
distractors.trap: Changes the schedule before validating expectations, options, and business impact with stakeholders.

distractors.text: Ask the product owner to prioritize which of the competing feature requests to include and which to defer for this release, and to communicate that prioritization decision to the affected clinical stakeholders.
distractors.trap: Relies on a single role to resolve a broader alignment issue involving readiness, expectations, and schedule tradeoffs.

distractors.text: Add the requested features to the current release and reduce the planned testing and training window to fit them into the date, with the delivery team validating the build during the live pilot rollout with the clinics.
distractors.trap: Sacrifices the quality safeguards the team warned against to hit a date, risking defects in a live, high-stakes environment.

distractors.text: Explain to the chief medical officer that release scheduling sits with the delivery team, and ask them to raise their feature priorities through the product backlog and the product owner rather than by fixing the release date.
distractors.trap: Sidelines a key stakeholder from a decision that needs expectation alignment, rather than engaging them on the tradeoffs.

distractors.text: Commit to delivering roughly half of the requested features by the original release date as a compromise, agreeing the split with the executive and scheduling the remaining features for a later release.
distractors.trap: Commits to an arbitrary partial scope before validating feasibility, value, and the impact on testing and training.

explanation: The project manager should first align stakeholder expectations with delivery reality by reviewing objectives, constraints, and readiness with the team, then adjust the release plan rather than forcing the date, escalating, or cutting quality.

prompts: A project manager is initiating a manufacturing line upgrade. The business case states the project must reduce energy use and waste while maintaining the approved launch date and budget target. Some team members suggest documenting sustainability goals later in benefits tracking so scope definition can move faster. What should the project manager do first?

A project manager is starting a data center modernization whose business case requires cutting power and cooling consumption while holding the committed go-live date and cost ceiling. Several engineers propose recording the energy-reduction goals later during benefits realization so the scope can be locked sooner. What should the project manager do first?

A project manager is launching a municipal fleet electrification program; the business case mandates measurable reductions in emissions and fuel use without exceeding the approved budget or schedule. Part of the team wants to defer the environmental targets to post-delivery tracking to speed up requirements definition. What should the project manager do first?

A project manager is kicking off a commercial building retrofit whose business case demands lower energy consumption and reduced construction waste while preserving the approved budget and completion date. Some stakeholders suggest capturing the sustainability aims later as tracked benefits so scope work can proceed quickly. What should the project manager do first?

correct.text: Incorporate measurable sustainability requirements, acceptance criteria, assumptions, and constraints into the project charter and management plans before baselining scope.
correct.why: Sustainability should be integrated into normal planning artifacts so it drives scope, decisions, and tradeoff management from the start.

correct.text: Build the energy and waste reduction targets into the charter, scope statement, and management plans as testable requirements before the scope baseline is set.
correct.why: Embedding the targets as requirements up front ensures they shape design decisions rather than being retrofitted later.

correct.text: Treat sustainability as a formal project requirement by defining measurable targets and acceptance criteria in the planning artifacts before scope is baselined.
correct.why: Formalizing the targets as requirements keeps them enforceable and visible throughout planning and delivery.

distractors.text: Record sustainability as a generally desirable benefit in the business case and revisit it later, after the team has confirmed that the current schedule baseline is genuinely achievable as planned.
distractors.trap: Treats sustainability as optional and outside core planning.

distractors.text: Defer the sustainability details to the closing phase of the project, validating environmental performance after the upgraded line is delivered.
distractors.trap: Confuses outcome validation with requirement definition.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to temporarily remove the sustainability expectations from the business case until the team finishes the initial technical design and can judge how feasible the targets are.
distractors.trap: Assumes uncertainty justifies eliminating a stated project driver.

distractors.text: Create a lessons-learned entry about sustainability and wait for the procurement function to define the applicable environmental standards and supplier requirements first.
distractors.trap: Misplaces planning work into organizational learning and another function.

distractors.text: Add the sustainability goals to the risk register as potential threats to the schedule, and address them later if they begin to endanger the approved launch date or budget target.
distractors.trap: Reframes a stated project driver as a mere schedule risk, deferring integration instead of planning for it.

distractors.text: Delegate the sustainability targets to a separate environmental workstream that runs in parallel, while the core scope definition proceeds independently on its own separate track.
distractors.trap: Silos a core business requirement, preventing it from shaping scope, tradeoffs, and acceptance criteria.

distractors.text: Document the sustainability objectives in the benefits realization plan for post-launch tracking, and keep them out of the charter while the team baselines the project scope.
distractors.trap: Moves a defining requirement into post-project tracking, exactly the shortcut that leaves scope blind to it.

explanation: Because sustainability is a stated business driver, the project manager should integrate measurable sustainability requirements, acceptance criteria, assumptions, and constraints into the charter and management plans before baselining scope, rather than deferring, siloing, or removing them.

prompts: A project team has finished identifying dozens of threats and opportunities for an enterprise resource planning rollout and entered them into the risk register. The sponsor asks the team to determine which risks deserve the most attention first. What should the project manager do next?

After a workshop, a project team has logged many threats and opportunities for a new e-commerce platform launch into the risk register. The steering committee wants to know which of those risks the team should focus on first. What should the project manager do next?

A project team has completed risk identification for a hospital wing construction project, capturing a long list of threats and opportunities in the register. Leadership asks the team to indicate where limited response effort should be concentrated. What should the project manager do next?

A project team has just populated the risk register with dozens of identified threats and opportunities for a satellite ground-station upgrade. The sponsor requests that the team determine which risks warrant attention ahead of the others. What should the project manager do next?

correct.text: Prioritize the identified risks by assessing each one's probability and impact.
correct.why: After risks are identified, qualitative risk analysis is used to rank them so response effort is focused appropriately.

correct.text: Perform qualitative risk analysis, rating each risk's probability and impact so the team can rank which threats and opportunities need attention first.
correct.why: Qualitative analysis is the defined next step that turns a raw register into a prioritized list.

correct.text: Rank the risks in the register by evaluating how likely each is and how severe its effect would be, then focus response effort on the highest-rated ones.
correct.why: Ranking by probability and impact directs limited attention to the risks that matter most.

distractors.text: Develop a detailed risk response strategy for each entry in the register now, assigning an owner and a budget to each identified threat and opportunity as you go.
distractors.trap: Assumes response planning comes before analyzing which risks matter most.

distractors.text: Revise the schedule and cost baselines now to absorb the effect of the listed risks, updating the approved project plan to reflect the identified threats and opportunities.
distractors.trap: Confuses identifying risks with approved changes to baselines.

distractors.text: Escalate the entire risk register to the sponsor and ask them to personally prioritize all of the logged threats and opportunities on the project team's behalf.
distractors.trap: Assumes the sponsor, not the project team, performs the initial risk analysis.

distractors.text: Calculate the precise monetary exposure of each risk and build a numeric simulation model of the register's overall cost and schedule impact across the whole project.
distractors.trap: Confuses qualitative prioritization with quantitative risk analysis.

distractors.text: Review the register together with the team to consolidate duplicate or overlapping entries into a single clean list of the remaining threats and opportunities to be ranked.
distractors.trap: Tidying the register is useful housekeeping, but it skips the probability-and-impact ranking the sponsor asked for.

distractors.text: Reorganize the risk register by category and assign a single owner to each grouping ahead of any assessment of the likelihood or severity of the individual risks.
distractors.trap: Organizing ownership is useful later, but it skips the prioritization step the sponsor actually asked for.

distractors.text: Hold another workshop to identify additional threats and opportunities that may have been missed, expanding the register further before ranking any of the risks already captured.
distractors.trap: Identification is already complete; the next step is analysis, not another round of identification.

distractors.text: Sort the register alphabetically or by the date each risk was logged, then work through the entries in that fixed order during the response planning that follows.
distractors.trap: Processing order is not prioritization; risks must be ranked by probability and impact, not by name or logging date.

explanation: Once risks are identified, the next step is qualitative risk analysis, prioritizing them by probability and impact, not jumping to response planning, baseline changes, quantitative modeling, or dismissing risks.

prompts: A product rollout has generated a stream of approved and rejected scope change requests during execution. Senior leaders ask why the original scope needed so many corrections after planning was completed. What is the most likely root cause?

During a hospital information system implementation, dozens of scope changes surface after the plan is baselined. The steering committee wants to know why the original scope required so much rework. What is the most likely root cause?
An enterprise ERP deployment keeps issuing scope changes well into execution, and executives question why the baselined scope was so unstable. What is the most likely root cause?

correct.text: Requirements were not collected and analyzed in enough detail during planning.
correct.why: Incomplete or weak requirements definition is the most common cause of repeated scope changes later in the project.

correct.text: The original requirements were gathered too superficially to keep the scope stable.
correct.why: Shallow elicitation leaves gaps that later reappear as scope changes.

correct.text: Incomplete requirements elicitation left gaps that surfaced as changes later.
correct.why: Missing detail in requirements work drives corrections during execution.

distractors.text: Most of the planning effort went into perfecting the schedule network diagram rather than into the other essential planning work.
distractors.trap: Assumes schedule planning quality is the main driver of scope instability.

distractors.text: The sponsor approved and signed the project charter very early in the initiating phase of the project.
distractors.trap: Confuses authorization timing with the quality of scope definition.

distractors.text: The delivery team lacked adequate training in how to perform earned value analysis during execution.
distractors.trap: Mistakes a cost control skill gap for a root cause of changing scope.

distractors.text: The project team chose to deliver the whole scope of work through a predictive, plan-driven life cycle.
distractors.trap: Incorrectly blames the methodology instead of inadequate requirements work.

distractors.text: The project communications management plan was distributed to the stakeholders later than intended.
distractors.trap: Overgeneralizes a communication issue as the primary reason for missing scope details.

distractors.text: Stakeholders were engaged so frequently that their continual feedback kept reshaping the deliverables well after the baseline had been set.
distractors.trap: Frames healthy engagement as the cause; the changes trace to weak requirements, not to too much stakeholder input.

distractors.text: The work breakdown structure was decomposed into so many small work packages that the scope merely looked unstable on the status reports.
distractors.trap: Blames reporting granularity for changes that actually stem from incomplete requirements definition.

distractors.text: The quality management plan set acceptance criteria noticeably stricter than what the customer had originally asked the team to deliver.
distractors.trap: Attributes scope churn to quality standards rather than to the shallow requirements that left scope ambiguous.

distractors.text: The estimating team padded the original cost baseline heavily, making the later adjustments merely look like scope corrections on paper.
distractors.trap: Confuses cost estimating practices with the requirements gaps that genuinely drove the changes.

explanation: Repeated scope changes after baselining most often trace back to requirements that were not elicited and analyzed thoroughly, not to methodology, scheduling, or cost-control factors.

prompts: A project manager is overseeing the installation of automated sorting equipment in a distribution center. Midway through execution, earned value reports show a widening gap between planned progress and actual results, and several work packages are trending late. Functional leads are pressuring the project manager to add overtime and reorder upcoming tasks immediately to protect the milestone date. What should the project manager do first?

A project manager leading the rollout of a new claims-processing platform sees earned value trends diverging sharply from plan, with multiple work packages slipping. Department managers are urging her to authorize weekend overtime and resequence tasks right away to save the go-live date. What should she do first?

During a highway interchange construction project, cost and schedule performance indices both deteriorate midway through execution and key activities trend late. Site leads press the project manager to approve extra shifts and reshuffle the remaining work immediately. What should the project manager do first?

correct.text: Analyze the performance data with the relevant leads to determine what is driving the variances before selecting a corrective action.
correct.why: This identifies the real drivers of the variance so the response addresses the cause rather than just the symptom.

correct.text: Work with the team to diagnose the root cause of the cost and schedule variances before committing to any recovery action.
correct.why: Understanding why performance is off plan ensures the chosen action fixes the actual problem.

correct.text: Review the earned value trends with those closest to the work to understand the cause, then choose a targeted corrective action.
correct.why: Involving the people doing the work surfaces the true drivers before a response is selected.

distractors.text: Approve overtime for all of the delayed teams right away so that they can work the extra hours needed to recover the slipping milestone date as quickly as it can possibly be done
distractors.trap: This assumes more effort is the right fix before understanding why performance is off plan.

distractors.text: Compress the remaining schedule by fast tracking the dependent activities so that the work packages currently trending late are given a real chance to catch back up to the original plan
distractors.trap: This applies a schedule remedy prematurely and may increase risk if the underlying problem is not scheduling logic.

distractors.text: Escalate the widening variances to the project sponsor and request immediate approval to draw down the project contingency reserve so the recovery effort can be funded without delay
distractors.trap: This jumps to escalation and funding use before validating the nature and cause of the issue.

distractors.text: Issue a change request to revise the performance measurement baseline so that it simply reflects the current, slower pace at which the work is actually being completed by the teams
distractors.trap: This normalizes poor performance instead of investigating whether corrective action can restore control.

distractors.text: Replace the vendor team members on the delayed work packages, on the assumption that their weak performance is the underlying reason the schedule keeps slipping further behind plan
distractors.trap: This presumes a resource problem without evidence that staffing is the root cause.

distractors.text: Add a second shift of contract labor to the trailing work packages so that measured output visibly rises well before the upcoming milestone review is scheduled to take place
distractors.trap: This throws resources at the symptom and raises cost and coordination risk without knowing what is actually causing the variance.

distractors.text: Resequence all of the remaining activities strictly according to the functional leads' recommendations so that the most visible and politically sensitive tasks are finished first
distractors.trap: This adopts stakeholder pressure as the plan rather than analyzing whether resequencing addresses the real driver.

distractors.text: Instruct the team to report their progress on a weekly basis instead of daily so that short-term fluctuations stop distorting the variance trend that appears on the status reports
distractors.trap: This hides the signal instead of investigating it and delays discovery of the underlying cause.

explanation: When performance variances widen, the first step is to analyze the data with the team to find the root cause; committing to overtime, fast tracking, escalation, or rebaselining before that risks fixing the wrong thing.

prompts: A project team building a mobile banking app rejected a requested biometric login enhancement after the change control board determined the benefit did not justify the cost and schedule impact. Two months later, a different branch manager asks the project manager to implement the same enhancement immediately because customers are asking for it. What should the project manager do next?

On a hospital records project, the change control board previously rejected a proposed patient-portal messaging feature because its value did not justify the impact. Weeks later a department director insists the same feature be added right away because staff keep requesting it. What should the project manager do next?

An e-commerce replatforming project had a requested one-click reorder feature rejected by the CCB on cost-benefit grounds. Later a regional sales lead demands the same feature be built immediately to match a competitor. What should the project manager do next?

correct.text: Explain that the change can proceed only through a new, formally submitted and approved change request.
correct.why: A previously rejected change cannot be implemented without going through formal change control and receiving approval.

correct.text: Tell the requester the enhancement must go back through the change control process and be approved before any work begins.
correct.why: Reintroducing a rejected change still requires formal submission and board approval.

correct.text: Direct the request into a fresh change request so the board can weigh the current cost and benefit before authorizing any work.
correct.why: Only a newly evaluated and approved change request can authorize the previously rejected enhancement.

distractors.text: Proceed to add the requested feature to the current work now that a more senior stakeholder is the one asking for it this time around
distractors.trap: Lets stakeholder seniority override the approved change control process.

distractors.text: Update the project management plan to include the enhancement now and record the supporting justification for the change afterward
distractors.trap: Confuses updating planning documents with obtaining authorization to do the work.

distractors.text: Escalate the request to the project sponsor for a quick decision instead of taking it through the change control board
distractors.trap: Substitutes executive authority for the established governance process on a scope change.

distractors.text: Handle the enhancement as a routine defect repair on the existing functionality and complete it as corrective maintenance
distractors.trap: Misclassifies a new feature request as corrective work on an accepted requirement.

distractors.text: Reassess the impact and resubmit the previously rejected request to the board unchanged, without adding any new basis for it this time
distractors.trap: Returns the same request to the board with no new justification behind it.

distractors.text: Add the enhancement to the product backlog for the team to prioritize in an upcoming iteration alongside the other pending work
distractors.trap: Entering a rejected change on the backlog does not authorize it; approval is still required.

distractors.text: Tell the branch manager that a change the board has already formally rejected cannot be brought back to project governance for reconsideration
distractors.trap: Overstates the rule; a rejected change can return through a new, properly justified request.

distractors.text: Ask the change control board chair to approve the enhancement directly without the requester submitting a formal change request
distractors.trap: Seeks an informal approval that skips the formal submission the process requires.

explanation: A change the board previously rejected can only be implemented through a new, formally submitted and approved change request; seniority, plan edits, escalation, or relabeling it as a defect do not authorize the work.

prompts: A project manager is leading the construction of a regional emergency operations center. During an internal review before a milestone handoff, a city inspector points out that a backup communications room shown in the approved design package was never built. The project manager confirms that the room was included in the authorized scope and accepted drawings. What should the project manager do first?

A project manager overseeing a data-center build discovers during a pre-handoff walkthrough that a redundant cooling unit shown in the approved and accepted design was never installed. The unit was clearly part of the authorized scope. What should the project manager do first?

During a pre-commissioning review of a water treatment plant, an auditor notes that a chemical dosing station included in the approved, accepted design drawings was never constructed, and the project manager confirms it was in the authorized scope. What should the project manager do first?

correct.text: Meet with the project team to understand how the scoped deliverable was overlooked and agree on the right corrective action.
correct.why: This addresses the issue by first understanding the breakdown and then developing a fact-based correction.

correct.text: Bring the team together to learn how the approved item was missed, then decide on a fact-based correction with them.
correct.why: Understanding the cause first leads to a correction that actually fixes the execution breakdown.

correct.text: Investigate with the team why the authorized deliverable was skipped before committing to any specific recovery plan.
correct.why: Diagnosing the miss before acting ensures the response targets the real cause.

distractors.text: Submit a formal change request to add the missing backup communications room into the project scope baseline as a newly identified requirement.
distractors.trap: Treats an already-approved requirement as new scope instead of fixing an execution miss.

distractors.text: Direct the contractor to begin constructing the missing room right away and resequence the remaining schedule around the added work.
distractors.trap: Jumps to construction before understanding the cause, impact, and recovery path.

distractors.text: Inform the client that the omission has been noted and will be corrected during a later phase once the other outstanding work is finished.
distractors.trap: Defers a known scope gap without analysis or agreement on the correction.

distractors.text: Escalate the oversight to the project sponsor and ask for a decision on who should be accountable for allowing the miss to occur.
distractors.trap: Moves to escalation and accountability before the team investigates what happened.

distractors.text: Perform a full quality audit of the completed work packages across the entire project before taking any targeted action on this specific gap.
distractors.trap: Broadens the response to a full audit instead of targeting the identified gap.

distractors.text: Reallocate money from the project contingency reserve to fund and physically start the missing work ahead of any further review or approval.
distractors.trap: Spends reserve and starts work before the cause and recovery path are understood.

distractors.text: Update the design documentation to reflect what was actually built on site.
distractors.trap: Reconciles documentation to the as-built state rather than correcting the missed work.

distractors.text: Request a conditional acceptance from the inspector that lets the handoff proceed with the deliverable still outstanding.
distractors.trap: Seeks to hand the item off outstanding instead of investigating and correcting it.

explanation: When an authorized deliverable is found missing, the first step is to work with the team to understand how it was overlooked and agree on a corrective action; raising a change request, ordering immediate rework, deferring, or escalating skips the needed diagnosis.

prompts: Midway through a construction project, a site engineer flags a newly discovered permit-related threat that was not known during planning. Which statement best reflects PMI guidance about the Identify Risks process?

Halfway through a pharmaceutical trial, a researcher raises a new regulatory threat that no one had anticipated during the initial planning effort. Which statement best reflects PMI guidance on how Identify Risks should operate?

During the build phase of a software platform, a developer surfaces a previously unknown data-privacy threat. Which statement best describes how the Identify Risks process is meant to work according to PMI?

correct.text: It is performed repeatedly throughout the project and can update both the risk register and the risk report
correct.why: Identify Risks is iterative and can produce updates to records for individual risks and for overall project risk.

correct.text: It is iterative, so newly discovered threats are added to the risk register and reflected in the risk report
correct.why: New findings feed the individual-risk records and the overall project-risk picture at any point in the project.

correct.text: Risk identification continues across all phases, and each new finding can refresh the risk register and the risk report
correct.why: Because identification never fully closes, the register and report stay current as conditions change.

distractors.text: It is carried out once the project management plan is approved and is not substantially repeated as delivery progresses
distractors.trap: Assumes risk identification is a one-time planning activity and misses risks that emerge later in the project.

distractors.text: It produces the risk report, while the risk register is instead created separately during later risk analysis activities
distractors.trap: Confuses the outputs of Identify Risks with the products of later risk processes.

distractors.text: It focuses on overall project risk and leaves the identification of individual project risks to the other risk processes
distractors.trap: Mistakes overall project risk as the sole focus and overlooks the individual risks it also identifies.

distractors.text: It should begin once risks have been prioritized, with the initial ranking of exposure guiding when items are documented
distractors.trap: Reverses the process order by treating prioritization as a prerequisite for identification.

distractors.text: It is owned by the risk manager, who is responsible for surfacing new threats on behalf of the wider team
distractors.trap: Risk identification is a collaborative activity, and everyone on the project can surface new threats.

distractors.text: It is triggered through an approved change request, with unplanned threats entering the register after change control has reviewed them
distractors.trap: Emerging risks are recorded as they are found and do not require a change request to be identified.

explanation: Identify Risks is iterative and repeated throughout the project, producing ongoing updates to the individual risks recorded in the risk register and to the overall project risk captured in the risk report.

topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: A project manager is directing a cloud migration for a retail company. Midway through execution, a new data residency constraint is identified, and initial estimates show the current design may exceed the approved budget and timeline. The architecture lead recommends immediately moving the highest-risk systems to a premium hosting option to avoid delays. What should the project manager do first?

A project manager is running an office relocation for a law firm. Partway through, a new building-code requirement surfaces that could push the project past its budget and deadline. A vendor urges signing an expensive expedited fit-out contract right away to protect the move-in date. What should the project manager do first?

A project manager is leading a mobile-app launch for a bank. During execution, a new accessibility regulation emerges that may exceed the approved cost and schedule. The tech lead pushes to immediately license a premium third-party component to stay on track. What should the project manager do first?

correct.text: Assess the issue thoroughly and evaluate the feasible response options before deciding how to proceed
correct.why: Understanding impacts, tradeoffs, and alternatives lets the project manager select the most appropriate solution.

correct.text: Analyze the impact of the new constraint and compare the realistic alternatives before committing to any single course of action
correct.why: Comparing options first avoids locking in a costly choice before the full picture is understood.

correct.text: Understand the tradeoffs and develop several viable options with the team before selecting the most appropriate response
correct.why: Developing options collaboratively produces a better-informed and more defensible decision.

distractors.text: Approve the premium technical solution immediately to protect the current deadline without first weighing any other alternatives
distractors.trap: This commits to one option before comparing alternatives and their overall impacts.

distractors.text: Remove the lower-priority scope items at once to stay within the existing budget before analyzing other possible responses with the team
distractors.trap: This assumes scope reduction is the best answer without evaluating other feasible solutions.

distractors.text: Request additional funding from the sponsor right away because the new condition was genuinely unforeseen during the planning phase
distractors.trap: This jumps to one remedy before confirming the full problem and the available choices.

distractors.text: Instruct the team to continue exactly as planned while simply tracking the growing overrun as an open project risk for now
distractors.trap: This delays decision-making instead of actively analyzing a material issue that is already occurring.

distractors.text: Escalate the matter to the steering committee and ask them to choose the least costly option on the project's behalf
distractors.trap: This transfers responsibility upward before the project manager has analyzed the situation and developed options.

distractors.text: Direct the architecture lead to proceed with the premium hosting since they are closest to the technical details of the affected systems
distractors.trap: Deferring to one expert's preference skips the project manager's own analysis of impacts and alternatives.

distractors.text: Reset the budget and schedule baselines to match the new estimates so that the project's performance metrics continue to appear green
distractors.trap: Rebaselining to hide variance avoids the real analysis and bypasses proper change control.

explanation: Before committing to any remedy, the project manager should assess the issue and evaluate the feasible alternatives so the chosen response reflects the full set of impacts and tradeoffs.
