prompts: A project manager is drafting the risk management plan for a complex product launch. The team is debating what belongs in that plan versus in other risk documents. Which item should NOT be included in the risk management plan?

While preparing the risk management plan for a new airport terminal, a team argues over which details go in the plan and which belong elsewhere. Which of the following does NOT belong in the risk management plan?

A project manager is documenting the risk management plan for an enterprise software rollout and wants to keep plan-level content separate from individual risk records. Which item should NOT appear in the risk management plan?

correct.text: A specific opportunity that has already been identified for the project
correct.why: Individual positive or negative risks are documented in the risk register, not in the risk management plan.

correct.text: An individual positive risk that the team has already spotted for this particular initiative
correct.why: Named individual risks belong in the risk register, which the plan governs but does not contain.

correct.text: A particular threat or opportunity discovered on the project, which instead belongs in the risk register
correct.why: The plan defines how risk is managed; specific identified risks live in the register.

distractors.text: The risk categories and risk breakdown structure the project will use to organize and group its identified risks
distractors.trap: Categorization structure is a planning-level decision that does belong in the risk management plan.

distractors.text: The roles and responsibilities assigned for carrying out the project's ongoing risk management activities
distractors.trap: Governance information such as roles is part of the plan, not the register.

distractors.text: The overall methodology and defined approach the team will follow for performing risk management on the project
distractors.trap: The methodology is exactly the kind of process approach the plan is meant to capture.

distractors.text: The definitions of the probability and impact scales that the project will apply during its risk analysis
distractors.trap: Analysis criteria such as scales are established in the plan, so they do belong there.

distractors.text: The risk-related budgeting and contingency funding approach that the project will apply throughout its lifecycle
distractors.trap: Funding and contingency approach is a planning-level element that belongs in the risk management plan.

distractors.text: The timing and cadence for when risk management activities will be performed across the different project phases
distractors.trap: Timing of risk activities is defined in the plan, so it is correctly included there.

distractors.text: The stakeholder risk thresholds and appetite that will guide how aggressively the team responds to identified risks
distractors.trap: Risk thresholds and appetite are planning inputs captured in the plan itself.

explanation: The risk management plan defines how risk management will be conducted, including methodology, roles, categories, scales, timing, and thresholds; individual identified risks and opportunities are recorded in the risk register instead.

topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: A project manager is leading a hospital electronic records upgrade. Midway through execution, a new cybersecurity testing requirement is added after an internal audit, but the project reserve has already been consumed by earlier integration work. The testing is essential before go-live, and the sponsor warns that no additional funding will be approved this quarter. What should the project manager do next?

A project manager is running a utility company's billing-system replacement. During execution, regulators impose a mandatory fraud-detection review, yet the contingency reserve is already spent. The review must finish before launch, and the sponsor states no extra money is available this period. What should the project manager do next?

A project manager oversees a university's learning-platform migration. Late in execution, a required data-protection assessment is added after a compliance check, but reserves are exhausted. It must be completed before go-live, and the sponsor confirms no new funding will come this quarter. What should the project manager do next?

correct.text: Work with the team to review remaining work, find lower-priority items, and reallocate existing budget to fund the testing
correct.why: Engaging the team closest to the work analyzes feasible tradeoffs before escalating or cutting scope.

correct.text: Engage the team closest to the work to analyze feasible tradeoffs and see whether funds can be shifted from lower-value tasks
correct.why: The people doing the work best understand where budget can be freed up without harming outcomes.

correct.text: Collaborate with the team to reprioritize the remaining scope and free up existing budget to cover the mandatory testing
correct.why: Reprioritizing within the approved budget addresses the gap before seeking outside help.

distractors.text: Submit an immediate change request asking the sponsor to increase the overall budget because the new testing is now mandatory
distractors.trap: It assumes external funding is the first step instead of first assessing internal options within the approved budget.

distractors.text: Delay the mandatory cybersecurity testing until after go-live so that the current schedule baseline is not affected at all
distractors.trap: It ignores a critical compliance need and creates unacceptable risk by postponing required work.

distractors.text: Direct the team to stop all noncritical tasks until enough money has gradually accumulated in the project's operating account
distractors.trap: It imposes a blunt operational pause without analysis and may worsen schedule and cost performance.

distractors.text: Remove several planned training activities from the project plan right away in order to free up funds for the testing effort
distractors.trap: It makes an unvalidated decision about cuts before understanding impacts and alternatives with the team.

distractors.text: Ask procurement to renegotiate all current vendor contracts in order to create the savings needed for the required testing work
distractors.trap: It jumps to one tactic without first determining whether contract savings are realistic or sufficient.

distractors.text: Absorb the extra testing cost by quietly extending the timeline and letting the schedule slip until next quarter's funding opens
distractors.trap: It trades an unauthorized schedule slip for the funding problem without transparent analysis or agreement.

distractors.text: Transfer the entire testing scope to the operations team so that it falls outside the project's budget and becomes their burden
distractors.trap: It offloads mandatory project work onto another group to dodge the constraint rather than solving it.

explanation: With no added funding available, the project manager should first engage the team closest to the work to analyze tradeoffs and reallocate existing budget before escalating, cutting essential scope, or deferring mandatory work.

topic: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/Methods and Practices
prompts: A city government authorizes a six-month effort to design and launch a new online permit portal that has never existed before. Which description best fits this work?
A hospital charters a nine-month effort to build a patient-scheduling app that the organization has never had before. Which description best fits this work?
A university funds a one-time initiative to design and launch a brand-new alumni donation platform. How is this work best characterized?

correct.text: A temporary endeavor undertaken to create a unique product, service, or result
correct.why: This is the PMBOK definition of a project.

correct.text: A time-bound effort with a defined start and end that produces a one-of-a-kind outcome
correct.why: Captures both the temporary nature and the uniqueness that define a project.

correct.text: A finite piece of work that delivers a novel deliverable and then concludes
correct.why: Emphasizes the temporary duration and unique result of project work.

distractors.text: A standing operational function established to sustain day-to-day business output over the long run
distractors.trap: Confuses projects with ongoing operations.

distractors.text: A repetitive activity performed on a fixed cycle to maintain existing services
distractors.trap: Assumes recurring work is a project because it supports business value.

distractors.text: A business initiative undertaken mainly to improve the organization's profitability or operational efficiency
distractors.trap: Incorrectly narrows projects to business-improvement efforts.

distractors.text: A temporary assignment that counts as a project once it begins to change the organizational structure
distractors.trap: Adds a condition that is not required for work to be a project.

distractors.text: An ongoing program of related workstreams that runs indefinitely to deliver continuous organizational value
distractors.trap: Describes a program or operations, not the temporary and finite nature of a project.

distractors.text: A standing service-delivery function staffed to keep an existing platform running smoothly over the long term
distractors.trap: This is operational support, which lacks the defined end point of a project.

distractors.text: A recurring seasonal effort that repeats each year with essentially the same scope and deliverables
distractors.trap: Repeating identical work with the same scope is operations, not a unique undertaking.

explanation: A project is a temporary endeavor undertaken to create a unique product, service, or result; ongoing, repetitive, or indefinite work is operations, not a project.

topic: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/Deliverables
prompts: A software-enabled construction project has an aggressive deadline. The team proposes skipping planned verification of energy performance and material traceability to recover two weeks of schedule float. The sponsor says sustainability reporting can be fixed after go-live if needed. What should the project manager do?

A green data-center project is behind schedule, and the team suggests skipping the agreed carbon-footprint and recycled-material verifications to claw back time. The sponsor says the sustainability report can be corrected after launch. What should the project manager do?

On a solar-powered manufacturing line rollout with a tight deadline, engineers propose dropping the planned emissions and supply-chain traceability checks to save two weeks. The sponsor is comfortable fixing the sustainability evidence later. What is the best course of action?

correct.text: Maintain the defined quality activities, look for schedule recovery elsewhere, and explain that the sustainability acceptance criteria are part of deliverable quality
correct.why: Sustainability-related verification belongs to quality management when it is part of accepted requirements and should not be dropped casually.

correct.text: Keep the agreed energy and traceability verifications and pursue other ways to recover the two weeks, since those checks are accepted requirements
correct.why: Accepted criteria cannot be quietly traded away for schedule; recovery must come from elsewhere.

correct.text: Preserve the planned verification work and explain that the sustainability criteria form part of the deliverable's quality baseline
correct.why: The criteria are part of the quality baseline, so removing them changes the definition of done.

distractors.text: Remove the energy and traceability checks from the current project and have that quality verified later during operations, once the deliverable has been formally accepted
distractors.trap: Treats prevention and verification as post-project issues instead of project quality work.

distractors.text: Replace the formal energy and traceability verification with the delivery team's own professional judgment and documented sign-off in order to keep the launch on schedule
distractors.trap: Bypasses the agreed verification controls and project governance that the accepted quality criteria depend on.

distractors.text: Accept the sponsor's direction to skip the checks and proceed, prioritizing the delivery deadline on this predictive, schedule-driven effort
distractors.trap: Lets a single constraint override quality requirements the stakeholders have already approved.

distractors.text: Transfer ownership of the checks to procurement and manage the sustainability evidence as a supplier responsibility under the vendor contract
distractors.trap: Confuses supplier inputs with the project's own accountability for deliverable quality.

distractors.text: Log a formal change request to descope the sustainability verification checks and let the change control board weigh trading that deliverable quality for the two weeks of schedule float
distractors.trap: Uses the change process as a vehicle to abandon accepted quality criteria rather than to protect them.

distractors.text: Split the difference by keeping the energy performance checks in scope but deferring the material traceability verification until a post-go-live audit window once operations begin
distractors.trap: Partially drops accepted criteria, still delivering a product that does not meet its agreed quality baseline.

distractors.text: Document the sponsor's decision as a formally accepted risk in the project risk register and proceed with the reduced verification scope to protect the delivery deadline
distractors.trap: Reframing a quality shortfall as an accepted risk does not make the deliverable meet its requirements.

explanation: Sustainability verification that is part of accepted acceptance criteria is deliverable quality; the project manager should preserve those activities and find schedule recovery elsewhere rather than descope them for the deadline.

topic: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/Methods and Practices
prompts: A project manager is leading a hybrid initiative to develop an AI-based claims triage service for an insurance company. The work involves uncertain business rules, emerging technical design, and frequent changes in stakeholder expectations. Early increments are inconsistent in quality, and leaders want an immediate control mechanism. What should the project manager do next to improve quality and learning while the solution is still evolving?

A hybrid team is building an AI-driven fraud-detection tool for a bank amid shifting rules and emerging technical design. Early increments vary in quality and executives want an immediate control. What should the project manager do next to improve quality and learning while the solution keeps evolving?

A project manager leads an uncertain effort to create a machine-learning demand-forecasting service, with volatile requirements and inconsistent early output. Leaders demand a quick quality fix. What next step best improves quality and learning while the design is still emerging?

correct.text: Introduce pair programming for the complex development work and schedule frequent customer feedback sessions on working increments
correct.why: These agile practices improve built-in quality and rapid learning when requirements and technical approaches are still emerging.

correct.text: Bring in pairing on the hardest components and hold regular reviews of working software with the customer to learn quickly
correct.why: Collaboration on complex work plus short feedback loops build quality in and surface misunderstandings early.

correct.text: Use collaborative development practices like pairing alongside short, frequent stakeholder feedback loops on real increments
correct.why: Built-in quality and continuous validation fit uncertain, evolving work better than late-stage controls.

distractors.text: Set up an independent test team to review each completed increment against the acceptance criteria before it is released to users.
distractors.trap: Relies on downstream inspection after the work is built rather than building quality in through fast feedback.

distractors.text: Facilitate a detailed requirements workshop to firm up the volatile business rules and baseline them before any further increments are built.
distractors.trap: Treats genuine uncertainty as something to lock down, blocking the iterative discovery the work depends on.

distractors.text: Increase the frequency of detailed quality-metric reporting to executives, providing a weekly view of the team's defect and rework trends.
distractors.trap: Improves management visibility of quality rather than building quality into the emerging work.

distractors.text: Assign the single strongest developer to lead the riskiest and most uncertain features individually while the rest of the team handles routine work.
distractors.trap: Forgoes collaborative quality techniques and concentrates knowledge of the hardest work in one person.

distractors.text: Schedule one comprehensive customer review once a near-complete solution is ready, giving stakeholders a full end-to-end walkthrough in a single session.
distractors.trap: Postpones learning to the end and raises the risk of building the wrong solution at high, hard-to-reverse cost.

distractors.text: Establish a quality gate at each phase boundary that a review board must sign off before the next increment is allowed to proceed further.
distractors.trap: Heavyweight phase gates add control ceremony but do not build quality into uncertain, evolving work.

distractors.text: Engage an external reviewer to check each release against a defined compliance checklist before the increment is shared with stakeholders.
distractors.trap: A fixed external checklist cannot keep pace with rapidly evolving rules and inspects quality in too late.

distractors.text: Prepare a detailed up-front design document for the whole team to follow as the basis for building each increment before coding begins.
distractors.trap: Big up-front design assumes stable requirements, which contradicts the uncertain and emerging nature of the work.

explanation: In uncertain, evolving work, quality and learning improve through built-in practices such as pair programming and frequent feedback on working increments, not through late inspection, requirement freezes, or added reporting.

prompts: A project manager is overseeing the construction of a regional distribution center. Delivery of specialized steel components is running late because of a supplier production problem, and the same crane crew is scheduled for the next sequence of structural work. The sponsor asks whether overtime should be approved immediately to recover the lost time. What should the project manager do next?

A project manager leading a wind-farm build learns that custom turbine gearboxes will arrive several weeks late due to a foundry issue, and the specialized installation team is already booked for the following work package. An executive pushes to authorize weekend shifts right away to claw back the time. What should the project manager do first?

On a hospital expansion, a critical shipment of custom air-handling units is delayed by a manufacturer defect, while the mechanical crew is due to start the next phase. The sponsor wants overtime approved on the spot to protect the opening date. What is the best next step for the project manager?

correct.text: Assess the delayed delivery to determine its effect on the schedule, cost, dependencies, and critical path before selecting a recovery response.
correct.why: The project manager should understand the full schedule and budget consequences of the delay before choosing an action.

correct.text: Evaluate how the late delivery affects float, downstream sequencing, and the critical path before committing to any corrective action.
correct.why: Quantifying the true impact prevents spending money on a remedy that may not address the real problem.

correct.text: Analyze the schedule and budget impact of the supplier delay first, then choose the response that best fits the actual consequences.
correct.why: A data-driven analysis ensures the selected recovery option is proportionate and effective.

distractors.text: Authorize overtime for the installation crew at once so downstream work can be accelerated as soon as the delayed materials finally arrive on site.
distractors.trap: Selects a costly response before confirming whether it addresses the actual schedule impact.

distractors.text: Replace the underperforming supplier right away with an alternate vendor so that similar delivery delays cannot recur during any of the remaining phases of the project.
distractors.trap: Takes a drastic procurement action without analyzing near-term project impact or feasibility.

distractors.text: Compress all of the remaining activities in the structural work package through crashing and fast-tracking to preserve the original milestone date the sponsor is expecting.
distractors.trap: Applies schedule compression prematurely without identifying which activities are affected or critical.

distractors.text: Inform the sponsor that the final completion date will inevitably slip and then update the approved baseline schedule accordingly to reflect the later projected finish dates.
distractors.trap: Assumes delay to the overall project without assessing float, dependencies, or recovery options.

distractors.text: Move the crane crew to a different area of the project site and continue all of the other planned work exactly as scheduled without changing anything else.
distractors.trap: Takes an operational action without first determining whether alternate work is actually available and beneficial.

distractors.text: Submit a formal change request to extend the project deadline before you have quantified how much time the delay will actually cost.
distractors.trap: Formalizes a baseline change with no analysis to justify the amount of the extension.

distractors.text: Ask the supplier to expedite a partial shipment of the components and absorb the premium freight charges out of the contingency reserve.
distractors.trap: Commits contingency funds to expediting before confirming the partial shipment would actually recover the critical path.

distractors.text: Reassign the idle crane crew to a lower-priority package and revisit the structural sequence only after the missing components eventually arrive.
distractors.trap: Reshuffles resources reactively without analyzing whether the swap protects the critical path or wastes crew time.

explanation: Before committing to any recovery action, the project manager must analyze the delay's effect on schedule, cost, dependencies, and critical path so the chosen response fits the real impact.

prompts: The diagram shows part of a project network diagram for a project with a tight deadline. Start dates are early morning and finish dates are evening. If all tasks are scheduled to begin at their early start dates, what is true?

A scheduler reviews the network diagram for a fast-tracked product launch. Each activity is labeled with its early start and early finish. Assuming every task begins on its early start date, which statement about the activities is correct?
The network diagram for a facility upgrade is shown, with each activity marked with early start and early finish dates. If all work begins as early as possible, which of the following is true?

correct.text: Activity B has a total float of 10 days.
correct.why: Because B sits off the critical path, it can be delayed 10 days without pushing the project finish.

correct.text: Activity B can be delayed by 10 days without pushing the project finish, giving it a total float of 10 days.
correct.why: Total float is measured against the project completion date, which B does not affect until 10 days of slip accumulate.

correct.text: The total float of Activity B is 10 days because it lies off the critical path.
correct.why: Only non-critical activities carry positive total float, and B's is 10 days.

distractors.text: Activity B has a free float of 10 days, meaning it can slip a full 10 days without delaying the early start of its immediate successor.
distractors.trap: Confuses free float with total float; B's slack relative to its successor is not the same as its total float here.

distractors.text: Activity A has a free float of 10 days and can therefore absorb slippage without affecting the early start of Activity C.
distractors.trap: Activity A sits on the critical path (A to C to D), so it carries essentially no free float.

distractors.text: Activity A has a total float of 10 days and can be delayed that long without impacting the project completion date.
distractors.trap: Activity A is on the critical path, so its total float is only the small system offset, not 10 days.

distractors.text: Every activity on the critical path A to C to D shares the same 10-day total float, so the schedule contains no truly critical sequence.
distractors.trap: Critical-path activities have zero (or minimal) total float by definition, so this cannot be true.

distractors.text: Activity D has a total float of 10 days because it is the final activity scheduled before the project deadline.
distractors.trap: Being last does not grant float; as a critical-path finish activity D has no total float to spare.

distractors.text: Activity C has a free float of 10 days relative to its successor Activity D on the same path.
distractors.trap: Activity C is on the critical path A to C to D and therefore has no free float relative to D.

distractors.text: The critical path itself carries 10 days of total float built in as a deliberate schedule reserve for the tight deadline.
distractors.trap: Float is not a reserve you build into the critical path; the critical path has the least float by definition.

explanation: Activity B lies off the critical path (A to C to D), so it can absorb 10 days of delay without affecting the project finish, giving it a total float of 10 days.

prompts: A project manager is overseeing a chain-wide retail point-of-sale hardware rollout across 80 stores. At the end of month 4 of an 8-month schedule, the sponsor asks whether the deployment is ahead or behind the approved timeline. The project manager has confirmed that the planned value is 520,000 and the earned value is 460,000. What should the project manager do next to assess schedule performance?

A program manager running a 120-branch network switch upgrade is at the halfway point of a 10-month plan. Leadership wants to know if the rollout is ahead or behind plan. Planned value is 780,000 and earned value is 690,000. What is the next step to evaluate schedule performance?

Midway through a six-month, multi-site solar panel installation, the sponsor asks whether the work is tracking ahead of or behind the approved schedule. The project manager has planned value of 300,000 and earned value of 265,000. What should the project manager do next to determine schedule status?

correct.text: Calculate schedule variance by subtracting planned value from earned value to determine whether the project is ahead of or behind schedule.
correct.why: Schedule variance is EV minus PV, which directly shows schedule status in earned value management.

correct.text: Compute SV equals EV minus PV, since a negative result confirms the deployment is running behind the approved timeline.
correct.why: A negative schedule variance is the objective signal that work completed is worth less than what was planned by now.

correct.text: Use earned value minus planned value to find the schedule variance and report whether the rollout is ahead of or behind plan.
correct.why: This applies the correct EVM formula for schedule performance rather than a cost metric.

distractors.text: Compare the actual cost incurred so far against the earned value to determine whether the schedule performance across all of the affected deployment sites is acceptable to the sponsor.
distractors.trap: Confuses cost performance analysis with schedule variance analysis.

distractors.text: Rebaseline the entire deployment schedule now to reflect the lower progress reported so far at this midpoint of the multi-month hardware rollout.
distractors.trap: Treats a performance signal as justification for changing the baseline before any analysis is done.

distractors.text: Fast-track the remaining installation activities across all of the outstanding deployment sites within the current sponsor reporting period.
distractors.trap: Jumps to schedule compression before quantifying the actual schedule condition.

distractors.text: Calculate the cost variance instead and report whether the deployment's spending performance is tracking acceptably to plan for the sponsor this period.
distractors.trap: Uses the wrong EVM metric for the schedule question being asked.

distractors.text: Ask the team to prepare a fresh milestone-based forecast from their own judgment rather than using the earned value data already confirmed.
distractors.trap: Bypasses the available objective method for measuring schedule variance from the confirmed data.

distractors.text: Divide planned value by earned value to produce a performance ratio and report that figure as the indicator of how far behind the store deployment has fallen.
distractors.trap: Inverts the schedule performance index formula and reports a ratio rather than the requested variance.

distractors.text: Recompute earned value at the end of the next reporting month first, and then determine the schedule variance from that updated figure.
distractors.trap: Delays a calculation that can be performed now with the data already confirmed.

distractors.text: Calculate the estimate at completion using the current cost performance index to project the final deployment rollout budget for the sponsor.
distractors.trap: Answers a forecasting-of-cost question instead of assessing current schedule performance.

explanation: Schedule variance equals earned value minus planned value; here EV minus PV is negative, showing the deployment is behind the approved schedule.

prompts: A project manager is defining scope for a mobile banking app. Several stakeholders request biometric login for the first release, but the organization lacks the approved security infrastructure needed to support it this year. How should the project manager handle this request in the scope definition?

A project manager is defining scope for a hospital patient portal. Stakeholders want video-visit functionality in the first release, but the organization has not yet secured the telehealth platform required to support it this year. How should the project manager handle this request in the scope definition?

While defining scope for a retail e-commerce platform, several stakeholders push for a same-day delivery feature that depends on a logistics partnership the company has not yet established. How should the project manager treat this request when defining scope?

correct.text: Document the requested capability as an exclusion in the project scope statement so the boundaries are clear
correct.why: The scope statement records what is out of scope so stakeholders understand the project limits.

correct.text: Record the feature as out of scope in the scope statement to set accurate stakeholder expectations
correct.why: Explicit exclusions prevent later assumptions that the capability will be delivered.

correct.text: List the capability under scope exclusions so everyone understands it is not part of this release
correct.why: Documenting exclusions defines the boundary between included and excluded work.

distractors.text: Add the capability to the approved scope baseline now and request the necessary infrastructure funding at a later date
distractors.trap: Baselining work before feasibility is approved treats an uncertain request as committed scope.

distractors.text: Tell stakeholders to simply wait until the project closing phase to revisit the feature for a possible future release
distractors.trap: Confuses future enhancement discussion with proper current scope definition.

distractors.text: Include the capability in the requirements documentation and confirm it to stakeholders as formally committed project scope
distractors.trap: Assumes every stakeholder request becomes approved scope even when it cannot be delivered.

distractors.text: Submit a defect report so the missing security infrastructure can be corrected by the responsible platform vendor
distractors.trap: Mistakes a current organizational limitation for a defect in a project deliverable.

distractors.text: Place the capability in the project risk register and track it as the planned technical solution the team will pursue
distractors.trap: Confuses an excluded feature with an uncertainty that needs to be monitored.

distractors.text: Escalate the disagreement directly to the project sponsor and let senior leadership decide whether to force the feature in
distractors.trap: Escalates a routine scoping decision the project manager can resolve through normal scope definition.

distractors.text: Approve the request now and adjust both the schedule and cost baselines to absorb all of the additional feature work
distractors.trap: Commits the project to unfeasible work and rebaselines before any change has been authorized.

explanation: When a requested capability cannot be delivered, the project manager documents it as an exclusion in the scope statement so stakeholders clearly understand the project boundaries.

topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: A hospital is implementing a new electronic medication administration system across multiple departments. Midway through execution, a regulator issues updated documentation requirements that force the team to add specialized validation activities and extended audit support from an external vendor. The work is necessary to remain compliant, and the sponsor agrees it must stay in scope. The project manager confirms the current cost baseline will no longer reflect the approved work. What should the project manager do first?

A city transit agency is deploying a new fare-collection system across several rail lines. Midway through execution, a new safety regulation forces the team to add certification testing and extended on-site vendor support. The sponsor agrees the work must stay in scope, and the project manager confirms the current cost baseline no longer reflects the approved work. What should the project manager do first?

During execution of a bank's core-system migration, a new compliance mandate requires additional data-privacy controls and outside auditor time. The sponsor confirms the work is mandatory and in scope, but the approved cost baseline can no longer account for it. What is the project manager's first step?

correct.text: Submit a formal change request to update the cost baseline so the approved budget reflects the required work
correct.why: A baseline is revised only through formal change control after the impact has been identified and documented.

correct.text: Raise a change request through integrated change control to revise the cost baseline for the mandated work
correct.why: Formal change control is the only authorized path to modify an approved baseline.

correct.text: Process the added compliance cost through a documented change request that updates the cost baseline
correct.why: The cost impact must be formally approved before the baseline can be changed.

distractors.text: Draw on the available management reserve to cover the added compliance costs and continue the work under the current approved baseline.
distractors.trap: Assumes that having reserve available removes the need for formal approval and a revision of the cost baseline.

distractors.text: Direct the team to scale back planned training and end-user support elsewhere in the plan to offset the new compliance expenses within the current budget.
distractors.trap: Cuts other planned work prematurely instead of processing the confirmed budget impact through change control.

distractors.text: Ask the finance department to set up a separate tracking code for the extra compliance costs and carry them there through to project closeout.
distractors.trap: Improves accounting visibility but still avoids actually revising the approved project cost baseline.

distractors.text: Notify stakeholders that the mandate makes the budget variance unavoidable and proceed with the required compliance work while tracking the overrun.
distractors.trap: Treats communication alone as sufficient governance for what is actually a cost baseline change.

distractors.text: Re-estimate the remaining work packages across the project before deciding whether any baseline action is necessary
distractors.trap: Adds unnecessary delay after the cost impact of the required change has already been confirmed.

distractors.text: Absorb the additional cost within the existing work-package budgets and report the resulting overrun at the next performance review
distractors.trap: Hides an approved scope change inside existing budgets instead of formally revising the baseline.

distractors.text: Confirm the detailed compliance requirements with the regulator before opening any change to the project budget
distractors.trap: Seeks further regulator confirmation though the sponsor has already confirmed the work is mandatory and in scope.

explanation: An approved cost baseline can only be revised through formal change control, so the project manager first submits a change request to reflect the mandated compliance work.

topic: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/Methods and Practices
prompts: A pharmaceutical company launches its first direct-to-patient digital therapeutics product, combining regulated clinical content, mobile app development, and outsourced device integration. The organization usually runs equipment validation projects with highly predictive controls, and several sponsors insist on using the same governance templates and tracking tools immediately. The assigned project manager sees major uncertainty in product features, vendor handoffs, and regulatory review timing. What should the project manager do first?

A medical-device manufacturer starts its first cloud-connected patient-monitoring product, blending regulated firmware, a consumer mobile app, and an outsourced analytics service. The company normally runs hardware projects with strict predictive controls, and several sponsors want the same governance templates applied at once. The project manager sees heavy uncertainty in features, vendor handoffs, and regulatory timing. What should the project manager do first?

An insurance company launches its first customer-facing AI claims assistant, combining regulated data handling, rapid app iteration, and a third-party model vendor. Leaders push to reuse the standard predictive playbook from prior back-office system upgrades. Given the significant unknowns, what should the project manager do first?

correct.text: Evaluate the project's characteristics, uncertainty, compliance needs, and delivery options with the team before selecting a methodology and its tools
correct.why: PMI-aligned judgment assesses the work and context first, then tailors methods and tools to fit the project.

correct.text: Assess the work, its risks, and the regulatory context with the team before committing to any delivery approach or toolset
correct.why: Tailoring decisions should follow a deliberate analysis of the project's real needs.

correct.text: Analyze the product complexity, vendor dependencies, and compliance demands first, then tailor an approach that fits
correct.why: The appropriate method is chosen only after understanding the specific project conditions.

distractors.text: Adopt the organization's standard predictive methodology and its established reporting tools, the same ones already used on the prior equipment validation projects.
distractors.trap: Assumes organizational precedent should override project-specific tailoring.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsors to choose the methodology and the governance templates that will define the overall delivery approach for the project team to follow here.
distractors.trap: Shifts a core project management responsibility onto the sponsor.

distractors.text: Begin execution using a hybrid approach now and then adjust the ways of working later on if and when the team happens to run into significant delivery issues.
distractors.trap: Starts delivery before deliberately assessing which approach actually fits the project's uncertainty and compliance needs.

distractors.text: Select the toolset preferred by the external device integration vendor for the collaboration and the handoffs that will run across all of the partner companies involved.
distractors.trap: Optimizes for one stakeholder's convenience instead of overall project fit.

distractors.text: Create the full detailed schedule and cost baseline first, and then determine which methodology would best support the plan that has already been drawn up and approved.
distractors.trap: Reverses the correct sequence by planning in detail before establishing the delivery approach that should shape that plan.

distractors.text: Engage an external consultant to recommend a recognized industry-standard framework for the whole team to adopt on this project before the nature of the work is understood.
distractors.trap: Outsources a tailoring decision that should rest on the team's own assessment of the project's real conditions.

distractors.text: Adopt a fully agile delivery approach across the product's feature development and the large amount of consumer mobile work that the initiative clearly involves here.
distractors.trap: Jumps to a single predetermined approach without weighing the project's regulatory, compliance, and vendor constraints.

explanation: PMI guidance is to assess the project's characteristics, uncertainty, and compliance context with the team first, then tailor the methodology and supporting tools to fit.

prompts: A project manager is planning a pharmaceutical manufacturing facility upgrade with many uncertain activities, including equipment delivery variability, validation rework, and labor productivity swings. Senior leaders want to know the probability of finishing before a market launch milestone and whether the contingency reserve is adequate. What should the project manager do next?

A project manager is planning a large semiconductor fab expansion with many uncertain activities, including tool delivery variability, cleanroom qualification rework, and labor productivity swings. Executives want to know the probability of finishing before a customer-commitment date and whether the contingency reserve is sufficient. What should the project manager do next?

While planning a major offshore wind-farm installation with weather-dependent tasks, vessel availability risk, and uncertain commissioning durations, leadership asks for the likelihood of meeting the grid-connection deadline and whether the reserves are adequate. What should the project manager do next?

correct.text: Run a Monte Carlo simulation on the key schedule and cost uncertainties to model the range and probability of possible outcomes
correct.why: This is the appropriate quantitative technique for forecasting the likelihood of meeting date and budget targets under uncertainty.

correct.text: Use a Monte Carlo simulation across the uncertain activities to produce a probability distribution of completion dates and costs
correct.why: Simulation aggregates individual uncertainties into an overall probabilistic forecast.

correct.text: Perform quantitative risk analysis with a simulation model to estimate the probability of hitting the milestone and reserve adequacy
correct.why: Modeling aggregate uncertainty answers the probability-of-success and reserve questions.

distractors.text: Add more contingency reserve to the project now to protect the launch milestone from the schedule and cost overruns that currently seem likely
distractors.trap: Responds before quantifying the probability distribution and understanding the actual exposure.

distractors.text: Compress the schedule by crashing the critical-path activities that appear to be the most risky and uncertain under the current baseline plan
distractors.trap: Jumps to a schedule response without first analyzing whether the forecast actually justifies intervention.

distractors.text: Escalate to the sponsor that the launch target is unrealistic because multiple high-risk and uncertain tasks currently remain unresolved on the plan
distractors.trap: Treats uncertainty as a confirmed outcome instead of using data-driven analysis to estimate probabilities.

distractors.text: Create a qualitative risk register that ranks all of the threats by probability and impact and then use that subjective ranking as the final forecast
distractors.trap: Confuses qualitative risk analysis with quantitative forecasting of aggregate project outcomes.

distractors.text: Baseline the schedule now and simply compare actual performance against it later to determine whether the identified risk is materializing over time
distractors.trap: Delays needed predictive analysis until execution rather than forecasting during planning.

distractors.text: Build a detailed deterministic schedule using single-point duration estimates for every activity in order to calculate one firm committed finish date
distractors.trap: Single-point estimates ignore the very variability leaders are asking the team to quantify.

distractors.text: Interview subject-matter experts to gather optimistic and pessimistic opinions for each activity and then average them together into one single target date
distractors.trap: Expert averaging is not the probabilistic modeling needed to answer the likelihood question.

explanation: To forecast the probability of meeting the milestone and test reserve adequacy under many uncertainties, the project manager runs a Monte Carlo simulation of the schedule and cost.

prompts: Midway through a construction project, the project manager learns that a newly issued environmental permitting rule might delay a critical site activity. The project manager logs the uncertainty in the risk register. What should the project manager do next?

On a software project, the product owner hears that a pending data-privacy regulation could force a redesign of a key module. The project manager records the uncertain event in the risk register. What is the appropriate next step?

A manufacturing project manager identifies that a single-source supplier of a key component may become unavailable due to industry consolidation and enters this uncertainty into the risk register. What should the project manager do next?

correct.text: Assess the risk's probability and impact so it can be prioritized and you can decide whether deeper quantitative analysis is warranted
correct.why: After a risk is identified and documented, the next step is to analyze it before selecting a response.

correct.text: Perform qualitative analysis to rank the risk by likelihood and severity before any response is chosen
correct.why: Qualitative analysis prioritizes the risk relative to others so effort is spent where it matters most.

correct.text: Evaluate how likely the event is and how much it would affect objectives, then prioritize it in the register for response planning
correct.why: Understanding likelihood and impact is the prerequisite for planning an effective response.

distractors.text: Submit a formal change request to add budget and schedule contingency to cover the potential permitting delay to the critical activity
distractors.trap: Commits an approved change before the risk has been analyzed or prioritized.

distractors.text: Escalate the risk to the sponsor for a decision on how the potential permitting delay should be handled across the rest of the project
distractors.trap: Confuses stakeholder notification with the sequencing of risk analysis and response planning.

distractors.text: Implement the most probable workaround now to keep the critical site activity clear of the potential permitting delay to the milestone
distractors.trap: Skips risk analysis and jumps straight to action without understanding likelihood or impact.

distractors.text: Move the item from the risk register to the issue log and begin managing it as a present problem that originates outside the project
distractors.trap: Mistakenly treats an uncertain future event as a current issue simply because it comes from outside the project.

distractors.text: Update the communications plan and share the newly identified event with each of the relevant stakeholder groups involved in the project
distractors.trap: Focuses on broad communication rather than the immediate risk management step.

distractors.text: Assign the risk to a functional owner and ask them to begin drafting a full mitigation plan for the potential permitting delay
distractors.trap: Response ownership and mitigation planning belong after analysis establishes the risk's priority, not before it.

distractors.text: Add a schedule buffer to the affected critical site activity to protect the delivery milestone against the potential permitting delay ahead
distractors.trap: Adding a buffer is a response action taken prematurely, before the risk has been assessed or prioritized.

distractors.text: Fold the uncertainty into the existing contingency reserve and manage it as part of the project's overall reserve rather than analyzing it on its own
distractors.trap: Absorbing the risk into general reserves skips assessing its own probability and impact.

explanation: Once a risk is identified and logged, the next step is qualitative analysis of its probability and impact to prioritize it before planning a response.

prompts: During schedule training for the team, the project manager explains which scheduling artifact governs the minimum project duration. Which scheduling artifact identifies the longest path of dependent activities and thus determines the project's minimum duration?
A project manager is asked which scheduling concept reveals the shortest possible time to complete the project given the network of activity dependencies. Which one is it?
In a scheduling workshop, the team wants to know which chain of activities has zero total float and therefore controls when the project can finish. Which artifact answers this?

correct.text: The critical path, the longest sequence of dependent activities, which determines the project's minimum duration
correct.why: The critical path is the longest sequence of dependent activities; delays on it directly delay the project.

correct.text: The critical path, because it is the chain of activities with zero total float whose delay pushes out the finish date
correct.why: Activities on the critical path have no slack, so any slip extends the overall schedule.

correct.text: The critical path, the connected string of dependencies that sets the shortest possible time to complete the project
correct.why: The minimum project duration equals the length of the longest dependent path through the network.

distractors.text: A resource histogram showing each resource's allocation and utilization across all of the project's planned activities over the full timeline.
distractors.trap: A real project artifact, but not the one that serves this question's purpose.

distractors.text: The risk register, which lists the project's threats and opportunities together with their assigned owners and planned response strategies.
distractors.trap: A real project artifact, but not the one that serves this question's purpose.

distractors.text: A burn-up chart plotting the amount of scope completed against the total scope planned across the project's elapsed timeline to date.
distractors.trap: A real project artifact, but not the one that serves this question's purpose.

distractors.text: The Gantt bar chart, which lays out every activity against a calendar timeline and visually shows each one's start and finish dates
distractors.trap: A Gantt chart displays scheduling information visually but does not by itself identify the longest dependent path.

distractors.text: The milestone list, which enumerates the significant checkpoints and target dates the project is required to meet along the way
distractors.trap: Milestones mark key dates and events but do not reveal the dependency chain that governs the minimum duration.

distractors.text: The near-critical path, the sequence carrying the smallest amount of float remaining after the single longest path through the network
distractors.trap: The near-critical path still has some float and therefore does not itself set the project's minimum duration.

explanation: The critical path is the longest sequence of dependent activities in the schedule network; its length determines the project's minimum duration.

prompts: A project manager overseeing a national retail point-of-sale hardware rollout learns that store installations are slipping across multiple regions. Some technicians say shipping is late, others blame incomplete site readiness, and regional managers claim training materials arrived too late. The go-live milestone is now at risk, and the sponsor asks for immediate corrective action. What should the project manager do first?

A project manager leading a multi-department hospital IT system deployment finds that go-lives are slipping across several sites. Clinical staff blame missing hardware, IT blames incomplete network prep, and vendors blame late data migration. The steering committee demands immediate action. What should the project manager do first?

A project manager running a multi-site solar panel installation program sees that several sites are falling behind. Crews blame permit delays, others cite bad weather, and procurement points to late parts. The sponsor wants the schedule recovered now. What should the project manager do first?

correct.text: Bring the relevant team members together to analyze the delay drivers and pinpoint the root causes before deciding on corrective action
correct.why: The best first step is to understand the actual causes of the slippage so that corrective action targets the real problem.

correct.text: Investigate the actual causes of the slippage first, so any corrective action is aimed at the real problem rather than a symptom
correct.why: Acting before diagnosis risks fixing the wrong thing while the true bottleneck persists.

correct.text: Facilitate a working session to diagnose why the installations are slipping before committing to any specific recovery action
correct.why: A structured diagnosis surfaces the dominant causes among the competing explanations.

distractors.text: Crash the remaining installation activities by bringing in additional technicians and adding extra shifts across all of the affected regions.
distractors.trap: Assumes a resource shortage is the main issue without confirming the actual cause of the delays.

distractors.text: Rebaseline the project schedule to reflect the missed go-live milestone and reset the stakeholders' delivery expectations across the program.
distractors.trap: Rebaselining is premature before the team understands the nature and extent of the underlying problem.

distractors.text: Escalate the delays to the steering committee and ask them to decide which region should be prioritized for the recovery effort first.
distractors.trap: Shifts responsibility upward before the project manager has performed basic analysis.

distractors.text: Direct the regional leads and their field crews to add overtime shifts across the affected sites until the rollout schedule is recovered.
distractors.trap: Applies pressure instead of diagnosis and may worsen morale without solving the true bottleneck.

distractors.text: Review the recent vendor scorecards and remove the lowest-performing vendor, redistributing their remaining installation work to the others.
distractors.trap: Acts on an assumed vendor cause rather than evidence about what is actually driving the slippage.

distractors.text: Fast-track the remaining regions by overlapping the site preparation and installation activities to compress the schedule that is left.
distractors.trap: Fast-tracking is a recovery action chosen before the true cause of the delays has actually been understood.

distractors.text: Reallocate the budget contingency to expedited shipping across the affected regions where the technicians have reported late deliveries.
distractors.trap: Commits money to one assumed cause without first confirming that shipping is actually driving the slippage.

distractors.text: Report to the sponsor that the go-live date will slip and propose a revised milestone that the team is confident it can comfortably meet.
distractors.trap: Announcing a slip and revising the date skips the diagnosis that could still protect the original milestone.

explanation: Before selecting corrective action, the project manager should diagnose the root causes of the slippage so the response addresses the real problem rather than an assumed one.

prompts: A project manager is overseeing the development of a new medical device. The sponsor wants a detailed end-to-end schedule, but several design decisions depend on clinical trial feedback and pending guidance from an external standards body expected later in the project. Team leads say near-term engineering work is clear, but downstream validation and packaging tasks cannot yet be defined reliably. What should the project manager do next?

A project manager developing a new aerospace component must produce a schedule, but downstream certification tasks depend on a regulator's ruling expected months from now. Near-term design work is well understood; later verification work is not. What should the project manager do next?

A project manager building an enterprise software platform is pressed for a full schedule, yet the design of later modules depends on user research still in progress. The next sprint's work is clear, but downstream modules cannot be defined reliably yet. What should the project manager do next?

correct.text: Plan the near-term work in detail and keep later-phase activities at a higher level until more information becomes available
correct.why: This applies rolling wave planning: imminent work is planned in detail while future work is progressively elaborated as uncertainty resolves.

correct.text: Apply rolling wave planning by elaborating the imminent work fully while progressively refining the downstream tasks as uncertainty resolves
correct.why: Rolling wave planning matches the level of detail to the information available at each point in the project.

correct.text: Detail the engineering work that is clear now and progressively elaborate the validation and packaging tasks as the missing inputs arrive
correct.why: Downstream work is planned at a higher level and refined once the dependent decisions are made.

distractors.text: Hold off on schedule development until the external decisions are finalized, then plan out the whole project at once in a single, fully detailed pass
distractors.trap: This assumes planning should stop until uncertainty disappears instead of using progressive elaboration.

distractors.text: Create a fully detailed schedule for the remaining phases using the most optimistic assumptions available and present it as the end-to-end plan
distractors.trap: This reflects premature precision and treats unknown future work as if it were already defined.

distractors.text: Shorten the planning horizon by dropping the uncertain downstream validation and packaging activities from the schedule baseline for the time being
distractors.trap: This confuses uncertainty with exclusion and would leave known future scope unmanaged.

distractors.text: Ask the functional managers to independently estimate the remaining unresolved activities and lock those detailed estimates into the approved project baseline
distractors.trap: This assumes expert judgment can eliminate uncertainty when key inputs do not yet exist.

distractors.text: Switch the project over to a fully agile, adaptive delivery approach and set aside predictive scheduling for the rest of the remaining phases
distractors.trap: This mistakes a scheduling technique for a methodology change and ignores that hybrid or predictive planning can still be appropriate.

distractors.text: Build a fully detailed downstream schedule now and attach a large management reserve to cover whatever the external standards body eventually decides
distractors.trap: Padding the plan with reserve does not make genuinely undefined future work plannable in detail today.

distractors.text: Outsource the downstream validation and packaging planning to an external consultant and have them produce the full baseline for issue
distractors.trap: Handing the work to a consultant does not resolve the missing inputs that make detailed planning premature.

distractors.text: Freeze the project scope at the current design and build a full schedule from it rather than waiting on the clinical or standards inputs
distractors.trap: Freezing scope ignores that the pending inputs are expected to legitimately change the downstream design.

explanation: Rolling wave planning is appropriate here: plan imminent work in detail and progressively elaborate later-phase activities as the dependent information becomes available.

topic: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/Methods and Practices
prompts: A project manager is leading a construction modernization effort across multiple job sites. Supervisors currently track daily work packages on paper forms, but an operations manager proposes a new field reporting application that could improve coordination and issue visibility. Several foremen are enthusiastic, while others worry it will slow crews down during peak activity. Senior leadership asks the project manager whether the application should be rolled out to every site immediately. What should the project manager do first?

A project manager oversees a hospital network upgrading how nurses log patient rounds. Units currently use paper checklists, and a clinical director proposes a new mobile rounding app. Some charge nurses are eager while others fear it will slow them during busy shifts. Executives ask whether to deploy it to every unit at once. What should the project manager do first?

An IT project manager is modernizing how a retail chain's store associates record inventory counts. Stores currently use handheld scanners with manual logs, and a district manager suggests a new tablet-based counting app. Some store leads welcome it while others worry it will slow related tasks during rush periods. Leadership asks whether to launch it chain-wide immediately. What should the project manager do first?

correct.text: Have part of the team pilot the application, evaluate the results, then decide whether broader rollout is justified
correct.why: A controlled pilot generates evidence about fit, benefits, and disruption before committing the full project.

correct.text: Run a controlled pilot at one or two sites first, measure the impact, and use that evidence to guide the wider decision
correct.why: Limited trials produce real-world data on effectiveness before scaling the change everywhere.

correct.text: Trial the tool on a small scale, gather data on benefits and disruption, and let the results inform full adoption
correct.why: Small-scale validation reduces risk by confirming value before broad commitment.

distractors.text: Roll the application out across the sites now, standardizing the whole project on a single field reporting approach
distractors.trap: Confuses standardization with readiness and skips validation of the tool in the real project environment.

distractors.text: Analyze the delivery risks of changing established team workflows during active execution and keep the current paper process in place for the time being.
distractors.trap: Weighs the change risk in the abstract instead of testing whether the tool improves delivery.

distractors.text: Refer the tool selection decision back to senior leadership to make the final call, and provide them with the information gathered so far from the job sites
distractors.trap: Hands the decision upward rather than gathering the decision-quality evidence a pilot would provide.

distractors.text: Survey the project team members and adopt whichever option receives the most support in their responses
distractors.trap: Treats popularity as a substitute for empirical validation of effectiveness.

distractors.text: Schedule training on the new application for the entire project team first, and assess afterward whether the tool was actually the right choice to adopt
distractors.trap: Commits broad resources before confirming the tool should be adopted at all.

distractors.text: Direct each site supervisor to adopt the application at their own pace across their crews throughout the peak activity periods
distractors.trap: Uncontrolled voluntary adoption yields no comparable evidence and produces inconsistent, unmeasurable results.

distractors.text: Commission a detailed cost-benefit analysis from the vendor and weigh the enterprise-wide rollout decision on their projected figures
distractors.trap: Relies on the vendor's own projections rather than independent evidence from the actual project environment.

distractors.text: Plan the full rollout for after the current peak workload subsides and then deploy the application to all of the sites at once
distractors.trap: Still skips validation entirely and gambles the full project on an unproven tool once timing is convenient.

explanation: Before a full rollout, the project manager should run a limited pilot to gather real evidence on the tool's fit, benefits, and disruption, then decide whether broader adoption is justified.

prompts: A company is launching a mobile banking app. The sponsor asks for clarity on the difference between the app capabilities users will receive and the work the project team must perform to deliver them. Which statement is correct?

A team is building an online retail platform. The sponsor wants to understand the difference between the shopping capabilities customers will receive and the effort the team must expend to deliver them. Which statement is correct?

A healthcare startup is developing a patient scheduling system. A stakeholder asks how the functions the software provides differ from the work needed to produce that software. Which statement is correct?

correct.text: Product scope defines the features and functions of the deliverable, while project scope defines the work required to build it
correct.why: PMI distinguishes what the product is from the work needed to produce it.

correct.text: Product scope is what the product does; project scope is the work the team performs to build and deliver it
correct.why: One describes the deliverable's capabilities, the other the effort to create them.

correct.text: Product scope covers the deliverable's characteristics, and project scope covers the effort needed to produce those characteristics
correct.why: The distinction separates the product's attributes from the work to realize them.

distractors.text: Project scope defines the product's features and functions, while product scope lists the individual tasks and activities that make up the schedule
distractors.trap: Reverses the definitions of product scope and project scope.

distractors.text: Product scope and project scope describe the same body of work, since both are captured together during scope planning as one set of documents
distractors.trap: Treats two related but distinct scope terms as identical and ignores the distinction PMI draws.

distractors.text: Product scope is owned by the project manager, while project scope is owned by the sponsor
distractors.trap: Substitutes role ownership for the actual conceptual difference.

distractors.text: Project scope applies to predictive projects, while product scope applies to agile projects that deliver in iterations
distractors.trap: Incorrectly maps core scope concepts to delivery approaches.

distractors.text: Product scope is documented in the project charter, while project scope lives in the work breakdown structure, and the two are kept separate
distractors.trap: Confuses where scope is documented with what each type of scope actually means.

distractors.text: Product scope is measured against the schedule baseline, while project scope is judged against the product's documented acceptance criteria
distractors.trap: Swaps the measures: product scope is judged against requirements, project scope against the plan.

distractors.text: The two terms work as interchangeable in agile delivery but are kept as distinct concepts whenever a predictive, plan-driven approach is used
distractors.trap: The conceptual distinction holds in every delivery approach, not only predictive ones.

explanation: Product scope is the features and functions of the deliverable; project scope is the work required to produce that deliverable. This distinction holds across delivery approaches.

prompts: A project manager moving from predictive to agile delivery is considering how the team will be composed. Agile teams are typically:
A delivery lead transitioning a group from waterfall to Scrum is planning how to staff the team. In agile, teams are usually:
An organization adopting iterative delivery is deciding how its development teams should be structured. Agile teams are generally:

correct.text: Cross-functional and stable, holding the mix of skills needed to deliver end-to-end usable increments
correct.why: The Agile Practice Guide describes agile teams as cross-functional and stable so they can deliver complete value without external dependencies.

correct.text: A stable, self-contained group whose combined skills let it produce complete increments without external handoffs
correct.why: Stability and cross-functionality let the team deliver value on its own and compound learning over time.

correct.text: Cross-functional and kept together over time so the members can deliver full value and build shared learning
correct.why: Keeping a cross-functional team intact lets capability and trust grow across iterations.

distractors.text: Re-formed at the start of each iteration with a different mix of members drawn from across the organization as needs change
distractors.trap: Reshuffling membership prevents the stability and shared learning agile teams rely on.

distractors.text: Staffed primarily with senior specialists who each bring deep experience in a single technical domain relevant to the product
distractors.trap: Emphasizes seniority in narrow domains over the balanced, cross-functional mix agile favors.

distractors.text: Made up of narrow specialists who focus on the one role they were assigned and hand work off to the next specialist
distractors.trap: Rigid single-role specialization with handoffs undermines the cross-functional capability agile depends on.

distractors.text: Assembled from part-time contributors who each divide their attention across several concurrent projects at the same time
distractors.trap: Fractional, divided attention prevents the focus and stability a dedicated agile team needs.

distractors.text: Organized around a single lead who assigns and reviews the tasks in order to keep the work consistent across the iteration
distractors.trap: Command-and-control assignment contradicts the self-organizing nature of agile teams.

distractors.text: Formed fresh for each release by borrowing whichever specialists happen to be free from the functional departments
distractors.trap: Ad hoc borrowing per release breaks continuity and prevents the team from maturing together.

explanation: Agile teams are cross-functional and stable, holding all the skills needed to deliver complete, usable increments without external dependencies, which lets team learning compound over time.

prompts: A project manager is leading a hospital expansion project. Midway through interior construction, a city utility authority announces that nearby infrastructure work will delay permanent power connection by six weeks. Some team members immediately want to compress downstream activities, while others want to push multiple milestones without analysis. What should the project manager do first?

On a data center build, a fiber carrier informs the project manager that a regional outage will delay the primary network link by roughly a month. Part of the team wants to fast-track cabling and testing, while others push to slide every milestone. What should the project manager do first?

A film production manager learns that a permitting delay will hold up access to a key location for five weeks. Some crew leads want to reshuffle the shooting schedule immediately; others want to extend the whole calendar. What should the project manager do first?

During a highway resurfacing project, the supplier warns that a specialty aggregate shipment will arrive six weeks late. Team members split between crashing later phases and blanket-extending all dates. What should the project manager do first?

correct.text: Assess how the delay affects the critical path and key milestones, then review the recovery options with the team before changing the schedule.
correct.why: The project manager should understand the actual impact and collaborate on a response before committing to any schedule change.

correct.text: Analyze the impact of the delay on dependencies and milestones, and discuss the possible responses with the team first.
correct.why: Understanding what actually shifts, then choosing collaboratively, prevents premature and possibly harmful schedule changes.

correct.text: Perform a schedule impact analysis to see what actually moves, then work with the team to choose the best path forward.
correct.why: Impact analysis provides the facts needed to select an appropriate, team-supported response.

distractors.text: Fast-track the remaining construction and procurement activities and overlap the planned downstream sequence throughout the rest of the project schedule.
distractors.trap: Assumes compression is feasible and beneficial before analyzing dependencies, risks, and deliverable impact.

distractors.text: Issue a revised master schedule that pushes each downstream date out so the remaining activities line up with the newly announced power-connection date for the site.
distractors.trap: Treats the extension as automatically affecting every remaining activity without first validating the true impact.

distractors.text: Escalate the delay to the project sponsor and request approval to release additional contingency funds against the impact ahead of any schedule impact analysis.
distractors.trap: Jumps straight to escalation and a funding request before determining whether the delay actually changes scope, dates, or recovery options.

distractors.text: Direct each workstream lead to create an independent workaround plan and implement whichever option is quickest.
distractors.trap: Encourages fragmented action without integrated analysis of the whole project schedule and its deliverables.

distractors.text: Notify the customer that the final handover date will move out, and share a revised delivery timeline covering the remaining project work and milestones.
distractors.trap: Communicates an outcome before confirming whether mitigation or resequencing could still protect key deliverables.

distractors.text: Enter the delay into the scheduling tool as a fixed hard constraint and let the software recalculate each dependent finish date across the plan.
distractors.trap: Automated recalculation replaces judgment about recovery options and hides opportunities to protect critical milestones.

distractors.text: Reassign the idle crews to other active projects until the delayed external dependency is resolved on site.
distractors.trap: Dispersing the team abandons the schedule problem instead of analyzing how to keep the project on track.

distractors.text: Convene a full lessons-learned session on the external coordination breakdown before making any decision about adjusting the remaining project schedule.
distractors.trap: Retrospective learning is premature here; the immediate need is to analyze impact and choose a response.

explanation: Before compressing, extending, or escalating, the project manager must analyze how the delay affects the critical path and milestones and then decide with the team.

prompts: A city tourism board is preparing a week-long cultural festival across multiple venues. In the final month, the coordination team logs issues involving shuttle timing, vendor access windows, signage placement, volunteer check-in flow, and backup power at the main stage. Team members keep raising these concerns, but attention has been spread evenly across all of them and progress is slow. What should the project manager do first?

A university is launching a multi-building research conference. Weeks out, the team is tracking parking overflow, catering timing, badge printing, AV in breakout rooms, and speaker travel. Effort is being split equally across every item and little is getting resolved. What should the project manager do first?

A retailer is opening five stores on the same weekend. The launch team logs concerns about fixture delivery, staff training, POS setup, signage, and grand-opening promotions, but is treating them all as equally urgent and falling behind. What should the project manager do first?

A hospital is coordinating a large community vaccination drive across several clinics. The team is juggling appointment flow, cold-storage backup, staffing gaps, supply restocking, and traffic control, giving each the same attention while progress stalls. What should the project manager do first?

correct.text: Assess each logistical challenge for likelihood and impact on the event objectives, then focus the team on the highest-priority items first.
correct.why: Risk-based prioritization ensures limited time and resources address the issues that most threaten the outcome.

correct.text: Evaluate the probability and consequence of each open issue and direct the limited time toward the ones that most threaten the event.
correct.why: Weighing likelihood against impact concentrates effort where it protects objectives most effectively.

correct.text: Rank the logistical concerns by their impact on event success and concentrate the team's effort on the top priorities.
correct.why: Prioritizing by impact stops effort from being diluted evenly across items of very different importance.

distractors.text: Assign a dedicated team member to each reported issue and give each logistical challenge equal attention in the weeks leading up to the opening of the event.
distractors.trap: Distributing effort evenly values fair coverage over concentrating on the highest-impact problems.

distractors.text: Call an urgent all-hands session with the vendors and ask each of them to commit to immediate fixes for each open logistical concern currently on the list.
distractors.trap: Driving broad action across all of the issues skips determining which problems pose the greatest threat to the event.

distractors.text: Update the issue log with fuller and more detailed descriptions of each concern and assign the owners for them at the next scheduled status review meeting.
