explanation: When requirements will evolve on user feedback, an adaptive approach with frequent increments and built-in inspection points lets the team course-correct continuously.

prompts: During contract negotiation for an agile-led product engagement, the customer hands over a one-page brief that fixes scope, date, and budget and asks the team to commit to all three for a 12-month delivery. What is the project manager's MOST appropriate first response?
A prospective client insists an agile team lock in a fixed scope, a fixed launch date, and a fixed budget before signing. As the project manager, what should your first response be?
A sponsor demands that an agile team guarantee full scope, an immovable go-live date, and a capped budget simultaneously. What is the PM's best opening response?

correct.text: Recommend a delivery strategy in which scope flexes through prioritization while the date and budget stay fixed, so the team commits to the highest-value work.
correct.why: Negotiating which dimension flexes is part of recommending an execution strategy (ECO Task 1 enabler).

correct.text: Propose fixing time and cost but letting scope vary by delivering prioritized features first, and explain that trade-off to the customer.
correct.why: Flexing one dimension via prioritization is the agile-appropriate way to honor fixed constraints.

correct.text: Advise holding the date and budget firm while treating scope as the flexible dimension, sequencing the work by value.
correct.why: Prioritized scope delivery lets the team protect the fixed date and budget.

distractors.text: Recommend running the engagement as a fully predictive, plan-driven project with detailed up-front baselines agreed for scope, schedule, and budget before any work begins.
distractors.trap: Switches the delivery approach instead of negotiating which of the three dimensions can flex.

distractors.text: Accept the three fixed constraints exactly as written in the brief and build a detailed delivery plan that commits the team to all of them across the 12-month engagement.
distractors.trap: Committing to fixed scope, date, and budget together sets the team up to fail on at least one.

distractors.text: Propose a fixed-price contract built around a detailed, signed-off scope statement agreed with the customer before delivery begins.
distractors.trap: Locks scope up front, the opposite of flexing it through prioritization under a fixed date and budget.

distractors.text: Ask the customer to relax the budget cap and extend the delivery date, keeping the full committed scope in place.
distractors.trap: Flexes time and cost to preserve full scope, inverting the agile priority of flexing scope instead.

distractors.text: Escalate the brief to your own senior management and ask them to renegotiate all three of the constraints directly with the customer before you offer any response.
distractors.trap: Hands off the recommendation the PM should own to senior management.

distractors.text: Propose delivering the full committed scope in a single release on the fixed date and add a sizable contingency reserve to the fixed budget to cover the delivery risk.
distractors.trap: Keeps full scope and pads the budget rather than flexing scope through prioritization.

distractors.text: Recommend a detailed risk-management plan and quantitative reserve analysis aimed at protecting the fixed scope, date, and budget throughout the 12-month engagement.
distractors.trap: Analyzes risk to defend all three constraints instead of resolving the conflict by flexing scope.

explanation: The PM's best first response is to recommend a strategy where one dimension - scope - flexes via prioritization while time and cost stay fixed, rather than accepting or refusing fixed-everything.

prompts: An agile team's product backlog has grown to over 800 items, many old and no longer relevant. What is the BEST corrective action?
A mobile-banking product owner opens a backlog that has swollen to nearly a thousand entries, a large share of them years old and no longer tied to current goals. What is the BEST corrective action?
A hardware startup's Kanban backlog holds hundreds of aging cards that no longer reflect the roadmap, and the team struggles to find what matters next. What should the team do FIRST?
During onboarding, a new Scrum Master finds an insurance-claims backlog crowded with obsolete stories that bury the current priorities. What is the BEST corrective action?

correct.text: Refine the backlog: close or remove stale items, reorder the remaining work by current value, and prepare the top items for upcoming iterations.
correct.why: An unrefined backlog hides current priorities and wastes refinement effort on obsolete items.

correct.text: Hold a refinement session to prune obsolete stories and re-rank what remains so the highest-value items rise to the top.
correct.why: Refinement keeps the backlog a lean, ordered, ready-to-pull list.

correct.text: Groom the backlog with the team, removing items no longer relevant and resequencing the rest around current priorities.
correct.why: Collaborative grooming builds shared understanding while trimming the backlog.

distractors.text: Archive the current backlog for reference and rebuild a fresh one directly from the current roadmap, carrying over the items the team identifies as still relevant to today's goals.
distractors.trap: Starting a parallel rebuild risks dropping valid in-flight items and the history behind current priorities that in-place refinement would retain.

distractors.text: Keep the full backlog list intact and use tags and filtered views to surface the currently relevant items for the team to work from each iteration.
distractors.trap: Preserving everything leaves the stale bulk in place, which still buries current priorities and inflates the effort of each refinement pass.

distractors.text: Have the product owner refine the whole backlog alone ahead of the team and publish the finished, reordered result for the developers to simply pull from.
distractors.trap: Refining without the team forgoes the estimation and feasibility input that makes reordering reliable, even though it saves the team time.

distractors.text: Route proposed removals and reordering through the project's change control process for approval before any backlog item is closed, reordered, or removed.
distractors.trap: Applying formal change control to routine backlog upkeep is a predictive practice that stifles the lightweight, continuous refinement agile expects.

distractors.text: Split the oversized backlog into several smaller sub-backlogs, each owned by a different team member for ongoing maintenance.
distractors.trap: Fragmenting ownership breaks the single ordered list and the product owner's accountability for priority.

distractors.text: Schedule a workshop to write detailed specifications for the backlog items first, and afterward decide which of them are worth keeping for the current roadmap.
distractors.trap: Elaborating items that may be deleted wastes effort; pruning stale work should precede any detailed specification.

distractors.text: Use a fixed calendar cutoff to bulk-remove the backlog items older than a set date across the whole list.
distractors.trap: Age alone is a blunt proxy for value and can discard still-relevant work that simply has not yet risen to the top.

explanation: A bloated backlog is fixed by refinement, removing stale items and reordering by current value so the top is ready for upcoming work.

prompts: An agile team's iteration burn-down has fallen below trend for three iterations in a row, and stakeholders are starting to notice. What should the Scrum Master do FIRST?
A telecom delivery team has missed its burn-down trend three sprints straight, and the steering committee is asking questions. What should the Scrum Master do FIRST?
For the third consecutive iteration, a healthcare app team's burn-up shows less completed work than forecast, worrying sponsors. What is the BEST first step?
A game studio's Scrum team keeps ending sprints behind its burn-down projection, now three sprints running. What should the coach do FIRST?

correct.text: Investigate the root causes at the retrospective, impediments, estimate accuracy, and Definition of Done, then act on what the data reveals.
correct.why: Empirical data drives inspection; root-cause investigation precedes corrective action.

correct.text: Use the retrospective to inspect the empirical data and surface why the team keeps slipping, then agree on targeted improvements.
correct.why: The retrospective is the built-in inspect-and-adapt point for team performance.

correct.text: Bring the trend to the team, dig into impediments and estimating gaps together, and adapt the process based on the findings.
correct.why: Involving the team uncovers real blockers and builds ownership of the fix.

distractors.text: Re-estimate the remaining backlog with the team and issue a revised release forecast to stakeholders based on the velocity of the last three iterations.
distractors.trap: Re-forecasts the numbers before diagnosing why the team keeps slipping.

distractors.text: Reduce the amount of work the team commits to in the next iteration so the forecast lines up with recent velocity, then carry on with the cadence.
distractors.trap: Adjusts the commitment to fit the trend rather than investigating why the trend fell.

distractors.text: Work with the product owner to re-prioritize the product backlog so the highest-value items are pulled forward into the coming iterations of the release.
distractors.trap: Reorders value delivery but does not address why the team keeps slipping.

distractors.text: Facilitate a backlog refinement session to break large stories into smaller, more predictable pieces before the next iteration begins.
distractors.trap: Improves estimate granularity before confirming that estimation is the real cause.

distractors.text: Coach the team on planning-poker estimation to tighten estimate accuracy over the coming iterations.
distractors.trap: Applies an estimation fix before the data shows estimation is the actual impediment.

distractors.text: Bring the burn-down trend to the sponsor and product owner and agree a revised set of delivery expectations for the remaining scope of the release.
distractors.trap: Resets expectations before understanding the underlying cause of the slip.

distractors.text: Hold a focused impediment-removal session with the functional managers to clear the blockers the team has raised over the last three iterations.
distractors.trap: Acts on assumed blockers before the retrospective inspects the empirical data.

explanation: Empirical data should trigger inspection: investigate root causes at the retrospective before taking corrective action.

prompts: A customer has pulled in the deadline, and the project manager is reviewing ways to recover the schedule. Which two techniques are used to compress a schedule?
A construction PM must finish a wing two months sooner after the client moved the handover date. Which pair of techniques compresses the schedule?
Facing a market window that just moved up, a product launch manager needs to shorten the critical path. Which two techniques are the classic ways to compress a schedule?
An IT migration must go live earlier than baselined to meet a regulatory date. Which two schedule compression techniques apply?

correct.text: Fast tracking, overlapping activities planned in sequence, and crashing, adding resources to critical-path activities.
correct.why: These are the two classic compression techniques, each with cost/risk trade-offs.

correct.text: Crashing the critical path by adding resources, and fast tracking by running sequential activities in parallel.
correct.why: Crashing trades cost for time; fast tracking trades rework risk for time.

correct.text: Compress the schedule by fast tracking overlapping phases and crashing with additional staff or equipment.
correct.why: Both directly shorten duration rather than merely reporting progress.

distractors.text: Burn-down charts and burn-up charts, both of which merely visualize how much work remains rather than actually compressing the schedule or its overall duration.
distractors.trap: These are tracking tools; they display progress but do not shorten duration.

distractors.text: Velocity tracking and story-point estimation, which help the team forecast future delivery timing but do nothing to compress the schedule itself.
distractors.trap: Forecasting techniques predict timing but do not compress the schedule.

distractors.text: Resource leveling and parking-lot management, both of which balance workload and organize deferred ideas rather than actually shortening the schedule.
distractors.trap: Resource leveling can actually lengthen the schedule, not compress it.

distractors.text: Rolling-wave planning and progressive elaboration, which simply add more and more detail to future work as it gradually becomes clearer over time.
distractors.trap: These planning techniques refine scope over time but do nothing to shorten the critical path.

distractors.text: Reducing project scope by removing deliverables and de-scoping lower-priority features until the smaller remaining body of work fits inside the new date.
distractors.trap: Scope reduction is a separate response that changes the product, not a schedule compression technique.

distractors.text: Monte Carlo simulation and critical-chain buffering, techniques used to model schedule uncertainty and protect the plan against variation rather than compress it.
distractors.trap: These analyze and protect the schedule but do not compress its planned duration.

distractors.text: Resource smoothing performed strictly within existing float, reassigning slack among activities while keeping the critical path and the end date unchanged.
distractors.trap: Smoothing works only within available float and by definition leaves the end date unchanged.

explanation: Fast tracking (overlapping activities) and crashing (adding resources to the critical path) are the two schedule compression techniques.

prompts: A project must procure services in a domain where the customer's needs are expected to change rapidly as work proceeds. Which contract type is GENERALLY most appropriate?
A research program will buy specialist lab work whose requirements will shift as early findings come in. Which contract type generally fits best?
An agile software vendor is being engaged for evolving, loosely defined product work. Which contract type is generally most suitable?
A startup outsources design services expected to change direction frequently during the engagement. Which contract type is generally most appropriate?

correct.text: A time-and-materials or cost-reimbursable contract with controls and incentives sized to the uncertainty.
correct.why: Changing requirements make firm-fixed-price risky; flexible types with caps and incentives fit better.

correct.text: A cost-reimbursable arrangement with ceilings and gain-share incentives that flexes as requirements evolve.
correct.why: Ceilings cap buyer exposure while the seller is fairly reimbursed for changing work.

correct.text: A time-and-materials deal with not-to-exceed caps, letting scope adjust while bounding the buyer's cost.
correct.why: T&M suits undefined scope; not-to-exceed caps manage the buyer's risk.

distractors.text: A series of separately fixed-price phases, each with its scope fully fixed before that phase's work is authorized to begin.
distractors.trap: Fixing each phase's scope up front still cannot absorb requirements that shift rapidly during the work.

distractors.text: A firm-fixed-price contract that covers the entire project's scope and full duration under a single agreed price.
distractors.trap: FFP on unstable scope forces costly changes and risk premiums onto both parties.

distractors.text: A lump-sum turnkey contract with a single fixed delivery handed over at the very end of the project's full duration.
distractors.trap: A single fixed lump-sum delivery cannot absorb rapidly evolving requirements.

distractors.text: A fixed-price-incentive-fee contract that ties the seller's profit to hitting the originally baselined cost and schedule targets.
distractors.trap: Incentive fees still assume a stable baseline, which does not exist when requirements change rapidly.

distractors.text: A firm-fixed-price contract padded with a large management reserve the seller can draw on for any change that arises.
distractors.trap: Padding an FFP just prices in risk the buyer pays for whether or not changes occur.

distractors.text: A cost-plus-fixed-fee contract whose statement of work is fully locked and frozen before any work is authorized to begin.
distractors.trap: Freezing the SOW defeats the flexibility that changing requirements demand.

distractors.text: A unit-price contract with rates fixed for a predefined catalog of repeatable deliverables agreed in full up front.
distractors.trap: Unit pricing needs well-defined, repeatable units, unavailable when scope is still evolving.

explanation: When requirements change rapidly, flexible contract types (T&M or cost-reimbursable) with controls and incentives beat firm-fixed-price.

prompts: A team member reports that 24 hours of nonstop rain is expected and the predefined risk trigger for site flooding has been met. The risk response plan already includes protective actions. What should the project manager do next?
A monitoring alert shows a vendor's delivery delay has crossed the trigger threshold defined for a supply risk, and a response is already documented. What should the PM do next?
Currency rates hit the trigger point set for a financial risk on an overseas project, and the response plan spells out the hedging actions. What should the PM do next?
Server load passes the predefined trigger for an outage risk during a product launch, and the response plan already lists the mitigation steps. What should the PM do next?

correct.text: Implement the risk response that was already planned for the flooding risk.
correct.why: The trigger is met and a response is defined, so the correct action is to execute it.

correct.text: Execute the predefined contingency actions now that the trigger condition has been met.
correct.why: A triggered risk with a ready plan calls for action, not further analysis.

correct.text: Carry out the prepared response plan for this risk exactly as it was designed in advance.
correct.why: Planned responses exist precisely to be enacted when their trigger fires.

distractors.text: Confirm with the site lead that the trigger conditions match the risk plan, then prepare the team to implement the planned response.
distractors.trap: Re-confirming a trigger that is already met adds delay when the response is defined and ready.

distractors.text: Notify stakeholders that the risk is likely to occur and prepare an issue log entry in case the site is affected.
distractors.trap: Notifying and logging treats a triggered risk as paperwork instead of executing the ready response.

distractors.text: Wait for the flood to actually occur and only then implement the contingency plan.
distractors.trap: Waiting lets the risk become an issue, the opposite of a proactive, planned response.

distractors.text: Reopen the risk register to reassess the probability and impact scores for the flooding risk before doing anything else.
distractors.trap: Re-scoring a risk whose trigger has already fired wastes time that should go to executing the response.

distractors.text: Convene the change control board to obtain formal approval before enacting the pre-approved contingency response.
distractors.trap: A response already planned and reserved does not need fresh change approval to be executed.

distractors.text: Escalate the situation to the project sponsor and await direction before taking any protective action on site.
distractors.trap: Escalating for direction when a response is already authorized needlessly delays protective action.

distractors.text: Draft a brand-new mitigation plan tailored to today's specific weather forecast rather than using the existing one.
distractors.trap: Building a new plan under time pressure ignores the ready, tested response designed for exactly this trigger.

explanation: When a risk trigger is met and a response is already planned, the PM executes the planned response.

prompts: A project team decides to use active risk acceptance for a known risk. Which action best reflects this response strategy?
A manufacturing team chooses active acceptance for a minor equipment-failure risk. Which action best fits that strategy?
For a low-priority weather risk, an events team elects active acceptance. Which action best reflects this response?
A software team applies active acceptance to a small third-party API risk. Which action best represents that strategy?

correct.text: Establish contingency reserves of time, cost, or resources to draw on if the risk occurs.
correct.why: Active acceptance acknowledges the risk and prepares reserves without avoiding or reducing it upfront.

correct.text: Set aside a contingency reserve and a fallback plan while otherwise leaving the risk untreated.
correct.why: Preparing reserves is the defining move of active, versus passive, acceptance.

correct.text: Acknowledge the risk and hold reserves ready to absorb its impact rather than trying to prevent it.
correct.why: The team plans to cope with the impact instead of altering probability or impact beforehand.

distractors.text: Prepare a set of actions in advance that would reduce the severity of the risk's impact if it were to occur during delivery.
distractors.trap: Reducing impact is mitigation, not acceptance.

distractors.text: Take deliberate preventive steps now that lower the likelihood of the risk occurring in the first place during the project.
distractors.trap: Reducing probability is mitigation, not acceptance.

distractors.text: Add extra resources to accelerate the overall project work independently of this particular risk's likelihood or impact.
distractors.trap: Adding resources to speed up work does not represent active acceptance of a specific risk.

distractors.text: Transfer the financial consequences of the risk to a third party through an insurance policy or a supplier warranty.
distractors.trap: Shifting consequences to another party is risk transfer, a different response than acceptance.

distractors.text: Redesign the affected part of the plan to remove the risk's cause and prevent the threat from arising for the team.
distractors.trap: Eliminating the cause is avoidance, the opposite of accepting the risk.

distractors.text: Acknowledge the risk and take no advance preparation, handling any consequences informally if and when it occurs.
distractors.trap: Taking no preparatory action is passive acceptance, which lacks the reserves that define active acceptance.

distractors.text: Escalate the risk beyond the project to a program-level owner considered better positioned to respond to it directly.
distractors.trap: Escalation moves ownership elsewhere rather than accepting and reserving for the risk within the project.

explanation: Active acceptance means acknowledging a risk and setting aside contingency reserves to handle it if it occurs.

prompts: A team designing production equipment must subcontract software for a complex control system, but because the equipment design is still evolving the software requirements are not yet stable. Procurement requires a fixed-price contract, and the submitted prices look unusually low. Which statement is most accurate?
A civil project awards a fixed-price subcontract for drainage work while the surrounding site design is still changing, and one bid comes in surprisingly low. Which statement is most accurate?
A fixed-price contract is signed for firmware while the hardware it runs on is still being revised, and the winning price seems too good to be true. Which statement is most accurate?
Under organizational policy a fixed-price contract is used for integration services even though the interface requirements keep shifting, and bids are notably low. Which statement is most accurate?

correct.text: Under a fixed price, the buyer still bears the cost of approved changes or unexpected scope adjustments.
correct.why: Fixed price does not remove the buyer's responsibility to pay for approved changes as scope evolves.

correct.text: Even with a fixed-price award, any scope change the buyer approves becomes the buyer's cost, not the seller's.
correct.why: The fixed price covers the agreed scope only; changes are billed on top.

correct.text: A fixed price locks the base scope, but the buyer absorbs the cost of changes it later authorizes.
correct.why: Evolving requirements will generate change orders the buyer funds.

distractors.text: If the vendors have underbid the work, that risk sits with them, and the project manager can proceed without raising it during selection.
distractors.trap: Overlooks that unrealistically low bids increase delivery and relationship risk down the line.

distractors.text: A fixed-price contract fits well here because it shifts the risk of changing requirements onto the seller for the duration of the work.
distractors.trap: Fixed price does not transfer change risk when requirements are unclear and changes get approved.

distractors.text: A more flexible contract type might suit the uncertainty better, though organizational procurement procedure takes precedence over project preference here.
distractors.trap: Procedures matter, but sound procurement judgment still requires matching contract type to uncertainty.

distractors.text: The unusually low bids point to a favorable deal for the buyer, so awarding to the lowest bidder is the sound course here.
distractors.trap: Abnormally low bids often signal misunderstanding or hidden change claims later, not a genuine bargain.

distractors.text: Once the fixed-price contract is signed, the seller is bound to deliver the evolving scope without charging the buyer anything further.
distractors.trap: The seller owes only the originally agreed scope; evolving requirements become chargeable changes.

distractors.text: Because the price is fixed, the project can rely on informal handling of design updates rather than a formal change control process.
distractors.trap: Fixed-price work still requires formal change control precisely because changes carry cost and schedule impact.

distractors.text: The buyer should have the seller build a large contingency into the fixed price to cover the range of possible future changes.
distractors.trap: Forcing broad contingency into the price makes the buyer pay for changes that may never occur.

explanation: A fixed-price contract covers only the agreed scope; the buyer still pays for changes it approves as requirements evolve.

prompts: A project uses configuration management to control changes to product components and related documentation. Which activity is NOT a purpose of configuration control?
An aerospace program runs configuration management across its parts and drawings. Which of these is NOT a purpose of configuration control?
A software release process applies configuration management to code and docs. Which activity does NOT belong to configuration control?
A medical-device team maintains configuration management over components and records. Which activity is NOT a purpose of configuration control?

correct.text: Verifying that a configuration item's identification is accurate, complete, and aligned with project needs.
correct.why: This is a configuration identification activity, not configuration control.

correct.text: Confirming that configuration items are correctly identified and labeled against project requirements.
correct.why: Establishing item identity belongs to identification, which precedes control.

correct.text: Checking the completeness and accuracy of a configuration item's identity attributes.
correct.why: Defining and verifying identity is identification, a separate process from control.

distractors.text: Ensuring that each proposed and approved change to the configuration items is fully analyzed for its impact before it proceeds.
distractors.trap: Analyzing change impacts is a core part of configuration control.

distractors.text: Ensuring that each proposed and approved change to the configuration items is documented in the project's change records.
distractors.trap: Documenting proposed and approved changes supports controlled change implementation.

distractors.text: Managing and controlling the requested changes to project configuration items by applying the agreed change-handling rules.
distractors.trap: Applying agreed rules to manage configuration changes is a central purpose of configuration control.

distractors.text: Reviewing and dispositioning each requested change to a configuration item through the agreed approval authority.
distractors.trap: Reviewing and approving change requests is exactly what configuration control does.

distractors.text: Maintaining an auditable record of the status and version of each approved change to a configuration item over time.
distractors.trap: Status accounting of approved changes is part of controlling configurations.

distractors.text: Ensuring that authorized, approved versions of configuration items are the ones released into the controlled product baseline.
distractors.trap: Gatekeeping approved versions into the baseline is a purpose of configuration control.

distractors.text: Tracking the implementation of approved changes to confirm each was applied to the correct configuration item.
distractors.trap: Verifying that approved changes were correctly applied falls under configuration control.

explanation: Verifying a configuration item's identification is a configuration identification activity, not a purpose of configuration control.

prompts: An agile team disputes two concepts. One is the shared, story-independent standard that all work must meet to be considered complete, owned by the development team. The other is the specific set of conditions a particular feature must satisfy to be accepted, owned by the product owner. Which mapping is correct?
Two agile terms are being confused: a global completion standard the developers apply to every item, versus per-feature acceptance conditions the product owner defines. Which mapping is correct?
A new team argues over which term is the team's universal quality bar for all work and which is the product owner's feature-specific pass/fail checks. Which mapping is correct?
During a workshop, members mix up the developers' team-wide completion standard with the product owner's story-level acceptance conditions. Which mapping is correct?

correct.text: Definition of Done is the developers' universal quality bar applied to every backlog item; Acceptance Criteria are the product owner's pass/fail checks unique to each feature.
correct.why: DoD is a global quality bar owned by developers; acceptance criteria are feature-specific and owned by the product owner.

correct.text: The team-owned Definition of Done applies to all work, while the product owner's Acceptance Criteria are written specifically for each story.
correct.why: DoD is global and developer-owned; acceptance criteria are per-feature and product-owner-owned.

correct.text: Definition of Done is a global completion standard held by the developers; Acceptance Criteria are per-feature conditions owned by the product owner.
correct.why: This preserves both the correct ownership and the correct scope of each term.

distractors.text: Acceptance Criteria are the development team's global completion standard applied uniformly across the work, while the Definition of Done is the product owner's separate set of pass/fail conditions written for each individual user story.
distractors.trap: Ownership and scope are reversed for both terms.

distractors.text: Both the Definition of Done and the Acceptance Criteria are owned and maintained by the product owner, who applies each of them at the individual user-story level across the backlog items.
distractors.trap: The Definition of Done is owned by the development team, not the product owner.

distractors.text: Both the Definition of Done and the Acceptance Criteria are owned by the development team, with the Definition of Done set per feature and the Acceptance Criteria serving as the release-wide global standard.
distractors.trap: The product owner owns acceptance criteria, and the scopes of the two terms are swapped here.

distractors.text: The Definition of Done and the Acceptance Criteria are effectively interchangeable terms for one shared completion checklist that either the developers or the product owner can define as work requires.
distractors.trap: They are distinct artifacts with different owners and scopes, not synonyms.

distractors.text: Acceptance Criteria form the broad organization-wide quality standard that is set and owned by the agile coach, while the Definition of Done is negotiated fresh and rewritten separately for each individual user story.
distractors.trap: Neither term is owned by the coach, and both scope and ownership are misstated.

distractors.text: The Definition of Done is set by the customer at release time, while the Acceptance Criteria are maintained internally by the development team and revised again for each new sprint.
distractors.trap: The customer does not own the DoD, and the product owner, not the developers, owns acceptance criteria.

distractors.text: Both terms describe the same single release checklist, but the Definition of Done covers bug fixes and defect work while the Acceptance Criteria cover brand-new customer-facing features.
distractors.trap: The DoD applies to all increments, not just bugs, and the two are not one checklist split by work type.

explanation: DoD is the developers' global quality bar for all work; Acceptance Criteria are the product owner's feature-specific pass/fail conditions.

prompts: Midway through delivery a stakeholder asks whether a particular test actually maps back to an approved business objective, and whether any requirements have been left untested. Which single tool best answers both concerns?
An auditor wants to confirm each test ties to an approved objective and that no requirement went untested. Which single tool best answers both questions?
A sponsor asks the PM to show that every requirement links to a business goal and to a verifying test. Which single tool best provides this?
Before a release gate, QA needs to prove each requirement traces from its objective through to a test, with no gaps. Which single tool best does this?

correct.text: The requirements traceability matrix, which links each requirement to its business objective and its verifying tests.
correct.why: It traces requirements from origin through design and test, exposing untested requirements and orphaned tests.

correct.text: A requirements traceability matrix mapping each requirement from business objective through design to test coverage.
correct.why: Bidirectional traceability answers both the mapping and the coverage questions at once.

correct.text: The traceability matrix, which reveals both orphaned tests and requirements that were never tested.
correct.why: A single view of requirement-to-test links surfaces gaps in either direction.

distractors.text: The requirements management plan, which defines how requirements will be planned, tracked, and processed throughout the project but traces nothing on its own.
distractors.trap: It describes the process but does not itself trace individual requirements to objectives and tests.

distractors.text: The scope baseline, comprising the approved project scope statement, the work breakdown structure, and the WBS dictionary that details each element.
distractors.trap: It defines approved scope but provides no requirement-to-test traceability.

distractors.text: The product backlog, an ordered list of the user stories and work items the team intends to deliver over the coming iterations.
distractors.trap: It orders work but offers no bidirectional traceability to objectives and tests.

distractors.text: The stakeholder register, which records the identities, interests, expectations, and influence of everyone involved in or affected by the project.
distractors.trap: It catalogs people, not the linkage between requirements, objectives, and tests.

distractors.text: The quality management plan, which lays out the quality standards, metrics, roles, and procedures the project will follow to meet its requirements.
distractors.trap: It governs how quality is managed but does not trace individual requirements to their tests.

distractors.text: A test summary report listing exactly which test cases passed and which ones failed during the most recent execution cycle of the test suite.
distractors.trap: It shows test outcomes but not which requirements or objectives each test traces back to.

distractors.text: The change log, which records the current status and final disposition of every change request that has been raised on the project.
distractors.trap: It tracks changes, not the mapping of requirements to objectives and verifying tests.

explanation: The requirements traceability matrix links requirements to business objectives and tests, exposing untested requirements and orphaned tests.

prompts: A new quality lead on the project asks the project manager to clarify the two terms they hear used loosely in meetings. What is the PRIMARY difference between quality assurance and quality control?

A junior engineer joining a manufacturing project keeps confusing quality assurance with quality control and asks the project manager to explain how the two actually differ. What is the PRIMARY distinction?
During a software project kickoff, a stakeholder uses 'QA' and 'QC' interchangeably. How should the project manager describe the PRIMARY difference between the two?

correct.text: QA focuses on the processes used to produce deliverables, while QC inspects the finished deliverables themselves.
correct.why: QA is process-focused (are the right processes being followed) and QC is product-focused (does the output conform).

correct.text: QA is process-oriented and verifies the right procedures are being followed, whereas QC is product-oriented and measures conformance.
correct.why: PMBOK Guide quality concepts: assurance evaluates process adherence; control measures actual results.

correct.text: Quality assurance evaluates whether correct processes are in place, while quality control tests the actual outputs for defects.
correct.why: Same distinction stated as auditing processes versus inspecting products.

distractors.text: They are largely the same activity, with QA being the term favored in regulated industries and QC the term generally used everywhere else.
distractors.trap: They are distinct disciplines; the labels are not an industry-naming convention.

distractors.text: QA is generally handled through automated tooling, while QC is done manually by inspection staff.
distractors.trap: Either QA or QC can be automated or manual; the split is not about tooling.

distractors.text: Quality control generally comes before quality assurance in the standard order of quality activities.
distractors.trap: The two are not ordered stages; there is no fixed QC-before-QA sequence.

distractors.text: QA is the responsibility of the sponsor and steering committee, while QC is handled by the individual team members doing the hands-on work.
distractors.trap: Both sit within the project's quality approach; responsibility is not divided by seniority.

distractors.text: QA applies mainly during the planning phase and QC applies mainly during closing, so the two operate in separate phases of the overall project life cycle.
distractors.trap: Both operate throughout execution; they are not confined to separate phases.

distractors.text: QA measures customer satisfaction through post-delivery surveys, whereas QC calculates the financial cost of quality reported to the sponsor.
distractors.trap: Neither term is defined by satisfaction surveys or cost accounting; both descriptions misstate the concepts.

distractors.text: QA and QC differ mainly in reporting frequency, with assurance reported monthly to executives and control reported daily to the delivery team.
distractors.trap: The difference is process versus product focus, not how often each is reported.

explanation: Quality assurance is process-focused and confirms the right processes are followed, while quality control is product-focused and inspects deliverables for conformance.

prompts: A critical specialist's availability is reduced by 30% halfway through a project. What is the project manager's MOST appropriate response?
Midway through a construction project, the lead structural engineer's availability is unexpectedly cut by roughly a third. What is the project manager's MOST appropriate response?
Halfway through a product launch, the only data scientist on the team has their time reduced by 30% because of a competing priority. What should the project manager do?

correct.text: Re-plan the resource allocation, assess the impact on schedule and scope, and discuss the available options with stakeholders.
correct.why: Resource availability changes drive re-planning across schedule and scope with stakeholder input.

correct.text: Analyze how the reduced availability affects the critical path and deliverables, then bring recovery options to the sponsor for a decision.
correct.why: Impact analysis followed by option-based decision making is the disciplined response.

correct.text: Update the plan to reflect the new capacity, quantify the schedule and scope impact, and collaborate with stakeholders on the best path forward.
correct.why: Replanning plus quantified impact and collaboration reflects sound resource management.

distractors.text: Ask the rest of the team to take on extra hours to cover the specialist's reduced availability and keep the original delivery date fully on track.
distractors.trap: Leaning on the team's extra effort protects the date without first analyzing the true schedule and scope impact.

distractors.text: Bring in another available person from the organization to backfill the specialist's hours, matching them to the tasks as closely as the circumstances allow.
distractors.trap: A swap made for availability rather than fit risks quality before the real impact of the change has been evaluated.

distractors.text: Ask the resource or functional manager to confirm how long the specialist's reduced availability is likely to last before settling on a response.
distractors.trap: Gathering more detail on the reduction is reasonable but defers the replanning and stakeholder decision the change already calls for.

distractors.text: Add several junior resources to support the specialist's tasks and redistribute the workload across them to keep the schedule intact.
distractors.trap: Adding generalists to specialized work rarely restores the lost capacity and can slow progress further.

distractors.text: Submit a change request to reduce the affected scope up front, ahead of confirming whether the key deliverable is genuinely at risk from the change.
distractors.trap: Cutting scope before the impact is understood reacts prematurely to a threat that may not materialize.

distractors.text: Escalate to the sponsor and request that the specialist's original availability be restored to the project as a matter of priority.
distractors.trap: Pressing to reverse the change passes over the viable replanning options rather than managing the reduced capacity.

distractors.text: Focus the specialist's remaining hours on the highest-priority critical-path tasks and reschedule the rest, settling the trade-offs within the team.
distractors.trap: Settling the trade-offs internally skips the stakeholders who need to weigh the impact and priorities.

explanation: A material change in a key resource's availability calls for re-planning, impact analysis on schedule and scope, and a collaborative decision with stakeholders.

prompts: An organization assigns team members to multiple projects simultaneously, each at 25-50%. What is the MOST significant project management implication?

A functional organization routinely staffs each analyst on three or four initiatives at once, each taking a quarter to half of their time. What is the MOST significant implication for project planning?
A PMO assigns engineers across several concurrent programs at 25-50% each. What is the MOST significant effect the project manager must account for in planning?

correct.text: Frequent context-switching reduces effective throughput, so planning must account for the productivity cost of fragmented attention.
correct.why: High context-switching lowers real capacity, so fractional allocations do not fully convert to output.

correct.text: Splitting people across many projects lowers real capacity, so estimates should reflect that fractional allocations are not fully productive.
correct.why: Planning that assumes 100% productive use of fractional time overstates throughput.

correct.text: The overhead of switching between assignments erodes usable capacity, so schedules must not assume fractional time converts fully into completed work.
correct.why: Switching cost is a real drag on delivery that must be planned for.

distractors.text: The main planning implication is to confirm that each person's percentage allocations across their several concurrent projects add up neatly to their total available capacity.
distractors.trap: Totaling allocations to full capacity ignores the productivity lost to switching between projects.

distractors.text: The key implication is simply that more of the projects can progress in parallel, so the overall portfolio advances on several different fronts at the very same time.
distractors.trap: Parallel fractional work spreads attention thin rather than speeding multiple projects along.

distractors.text: The main implication is that resource leveling can be handled by the functional managers who own the shared staff pool.
distractors.trap: Competing cross-project demands make the project manager's leveling more necessary, not something to hand off.

distractors.text: The most significant implication is the morale strain of juggling several competing assignments at once, which regular one-on-one check-ins with each member can help address.
distractors.trap: The primary implication is reduced throughput, not chiefly a morale or check-in matter.

distractors.text: The main implication is a shorter overall calendar duration, since the work on the different projects proceeds concurrently.
distractors.trap: Parallel fractional work often lengthens durations rather than shortening the calendar.

distractors.text: The key implication is that ongoing capacity forecasting becomes the shared functional managers' responsibility across the staff pool rather than the individual project manager's.
distractors.trap: The project manager still owns forecasting delivery for the project regardless of who staffs it.

distractors.text: The most significant implication is the more complex labor-cost allocation and time-tracking effort spread across the several different projects each person supports.
distractors.trap: The primary effect is on throughput and delivery pace, not merely on cost accounting.

explanation: Fractional multi-project assignment introduces context-switching overhead that reduces effective throughput, so plans must account for the cost of fragmentation.

prompts: A senior executive asks for a single percentage representing 'how done' the project is. What is the MOST appropriate response?
A board member wants one number that captures 'how far along' the initiative is. What is the project manager's MOST appropriate response?
During a steering committee, a director asks the project manager for a single figure summarizing overall completion. How should the PM respond?

correct.text: Provide a balanced view covering scope completed, value released, remaining backlog, forecast, and key risks.
correct.why: Executives need decision-relevant status, not a single reductive number.

correct.text: Give the executive what they need to decide: what is done, what value is delivered, what remains, and the forecast with major risks.
correct.why: A rounded picture supports informed decisions better than one percentage.

correct.text: Share a rounded status of completed scope, delivered value, outstanding work, and the current forecast rather than a single number.
correct.why: Balanced reporting reflects true progress and risk.

distractors.text: Report a single precise percentage derived purely from story points completed and present that one number to the executive as the definitive project status.
distractors.trap: A lone points-based figure hides value, risk, and forecast.

distractors.text: Refuse to answer the executive's question entirely, on the grounds that any single completion percentage is inherently meaningless and always misleading.
distractors.trap: Declining sidesteps a responsibility the PM should own.

distractors.text: Provide a percentage that actually represents team meeting attendance for the period instead of any measure of real work completion or delivered value.
distractors.trap: Attendance is unrelated to how complete the work is.

distractors.text: Give the executive the percentage of the total budget that has been spent so far and describe that spend figure as an accurate proxy for how complete the work is.
distractors.trap: Spend is not the same as progress; money burned does not equal work done.

distractors.text: Report the percentage of tasks marked complete in the tracking tool without checking whether the underlying deliverables have actually been accepted by anyone.
distractors.trap: Unvalidated task counts overstate real, accepted progress.

distractors.text: Quote the percentage of the schedule that has elapsed to date and present elapsed calendar time as if it were equivalent to true project progress.
distractors.trap: Time elapsed says nothing about how much value has been delivered.

explanation: Rather than a single reductive percentage, provide a balanced status of scope, value, remaining work, forecast, and risks so the executive can make decisions.

prompts: A project sponsor reviews a draft project charter and says it should be shorter and focused on high-level information. Which item is usually not part of a project charter and should be removed?
Reviewing a draft charter, a sponsor complains it reads more like a detailed plan and wants it trimmed to high-level content. Which item is usually NOT part of a charter and should be removed?
A governance board says a submitted project charter is too granular and should stay strategic. Which of the following does NOT normally belong in a charter?

correct.text: The detailed control account and work package descriptions belong in later planning, not in the high-level charter.
correct.why: Granular WBS decomposition is developed during planning, not authorization.

correct.text: The granular decomposition of the work into control accounts and work packages should be removed from the charter.
correct.why: The charter carries high-level information, not detailed breakdown.

correct.text: Low-level work breakdown detail such as work packages and control accounts is not appropriate content for a charter.
correct.why: Such detail exceeds the charter's high-level intent.

distractors.text: The level of authority granted to the project manager to commit organizational resources and make the key decisions across the project.
distractors.trap: The charter typically defines the project manager's authority, so it belongs there rather than being removed.

distractors.text: The underlying business need, justification, and strategic purpose that the project has been initiated to address for the organization.
distractors.trap: The business need is a core reason for authorizing the project and belongs in the charter.

distractors.text: The high-level risks identified for the project that may affect its feasibility, its funding, or its overall direction in the early stages.
distractors.trap: High-level risks are appropriate charter content because they inform early decisions.

distractors.text: The summary milestone schedule showing the major target dates and key checkpoints expected across the overall project timeline.
distractors.trap: A high-level milestone summary is legitimate charter content.

distractors.text: The names of the assigned project manager and the project sponsor together with their principal roles and responsibilities on the project.
distractors.trap: Naming the project manager and sponsor is standard, appropriate charter content.

distractors.text: The measurable project objectives and the high-level success criteria against which the project's overall outcome will be judged.
distractors.trap: High-level objectives and success criteria belong in the charter.

explanation: A charter holds high-level authorization information; detailed decomposition such as control accounts and work packages is created later and does not belong in it.

prompts: A project team needs the approved reference used to compare planned scope with actual results and assess scope variance. Which set of components makes up the scope baseline?
An auditor asks the team to point to the approved reference used to measure scope performance against plan. Which components make up the scope baseline?
A new project manager wants to know which approved documents together form the scope baseline used to assess scope variance. Which set is correct?

correct.text: The approved project scope statement, the WBS, and the WBS dictionary together form the scope baseline.
correct.why: These three approved components constitute the scope baseline.

correct.text: The scope baseline consists of the approved scope statement plus the WBS and its supporting WBS dictionary.
correct.why: Standard definition of the scope baseline.

correct.text: It is the approved combination of the scope statement, the work breakdown structure, and the WBS dictionary.
correct.why: Same three elements restated.

distractors.text: The cost baseline, the quality baseline, and the schedule baseline combined together to serve as the single reference for measuring overall project performance.
distractors.trap: These are other planning baselines, not the scope baseline.

distractors.text: The configuration management plan along with the configuration records that are used to control changes to the product throughout its entire development lifecycle.
distractors.trap: These support change control but do not define the scope baseline.

distractors.text: The procurement statement of work paired with the project scope statement, which together are used to define what an external seller is contractually obligated to deliver.
distractors.trap: These may inform scope but are not the approved baseline for measuring scope performance.

distractors.text: The requirements documentation, the requirements traceability matrix, and the stakeholder register that collectively describe what the project is expected to accomplish.
distractors.trap: These are requirements and stakeholder artifacts, not the scope baseline components.

distractors.text: The approved schedule baseline together with the milestone list and the activity attributes that establish the timeline against which delivery progress is measured.
distractors.trap: This is schedule-domain content, not the scope baseline.

distractors.text: The project management plan as a whole, since the scope baseline is simply another name for the complete integrated set of all subsidiary management plans.
distractors.trap: The scope baseline is one component, not the entire project management plan.

explanation: The scope baseline is the approved scope statement, the WBS, and the WBS dictionary, used as the reference to measure scope performance.

prompts: A Scrum team completes its sprint retrospective at the end of a timeboxed sprint. What should happen next as the team moves into the following sprint?
An agile team wraps up its retrospective at the close of a two-week timeboxed sprint. What should happen next?
A development team finishes the retrospective for its current sprint. According to Scrum, what happens as the team transitions into the following sprint?

correct.text: Begin the next sprint immediately, because timeboxed sprints follow one another continuously without a gap.
correct.why: In Scrum, sprints are back-to-back timeboxes; the next begins right after the previous ends.

correct.text: Start the following sprint right away, since Scrum sprints are back-to-back timeboxes with no break between them.
correct.why: There is no gap between sprints.

correct.text: Move straight into the next sprint, as a new sprint begins immediately after the previous one ends.
correct.why: Continuous cadence is a core Scrum property.

distractors.text: Refine the upcoming sprint's objective and elaborate the detailed work needed to support its successful execution.
distractors.trap: Useful planning, but it does not change that the next sprint begins immediately.

distractors.text: Define a fresh set of objectives and key results (OKRs) for the upcoming sprint and gain formal agreement on them with stakeholders.
distractors.trap: OKRs are not a standard Scrum requirement for starting a sprint.

distractors.text: Document the completed sprint's results and route them for formal sponsor acceptance and sign-off before any new work continues.
distractors.trap: Sprint outcomes are reviewed with stakeholders, not held for formal sponsor sign-off before continuing.

distractors.text: Hold a backlog refinement session with the product owner to reshape the release plan before the team commits to the next sprint.
distractors.trap: Refinement supports planning but does not gate the immediate start of the next sprint.

distractors.text: Compile the retrospective's action items into a written report and route them through a management review before restarting development.
distractors.trap: Retrospective actions feed the next sprint; work is not paused for management review.

distractors.text: Wait for the product owner to fully re-prioritize and re-estimate the whole product backlog before the next sprint is started.
distractors.trap: Ongoing refinement does not gate the immediate start of the next sprint.

explanation: Scrum sprints are continuous timeboxes, so the next sprint starts immediately after the previous one ends and its retrospective concludes.

prompts: A stakeholder says the team can accept lower product grade if needed, but quality must still be maintained. Which statement best reflects current PMI thinking about grade and quality?
A client says they can live with a more basic product tier as long as it still works reliably. Which statement best reflects PMI thinking about grade versus quality?
A sponsor is willing to reduce the feature category of the deliverable but insists standards not slip. Which statement about grade and quality is correct?

correct.text: A low grade can be acceptable, but poor quality is never acceptable.
correct.why: Grade is the category of features; quality is conformance and fitness for use.

correct.text: A lower grade may be acceptable when it fits the intended use, whereas poor quality is not acceptable.
correct.why: Low grade can suit needs; failing to meet requirements cannot.

correct.text: It is acceptable to deliver a lower-grade product, but the product must still meet its quality requirements.
correct.why: Quality conformance is mandatory regardless of grade.

distractors.text: Poor quality can be acceptable in some cases, whereas a low grade is not acceptable to customers expecting a full feature set.
distractors.trap: Poor quality is never acceptable; the requirements must still be met.

distractors.text: Neither a low grade nor poor quality is acceptable, and any drop in either one leaves the deliverable short of its stated requirements.
distractors.trap: Low grade can be acceptable when it aligns with stakeholder needs and intended use.

distractors.text: Grade and quality describe the same idea, so lowering one lowers the other and the two terms can be used interchangeably on the project.
distractors.trap: Grade and quality are different concepts and should not be treated as interchangeable.

distractors.text: Grade describes conformance to requirements while quality describes the category of features, so it is a poor grade that cannot be tolerated.
distractors.trap: The definitions are reversed; grade is the feature category and quality is conformance.

distractors.text: A low grade is acceptable once the sponsor signs a formal waiver, and any poor quality is then offset by adding compensating features in a later release.
distractors.trap: Low grade needs no waiver-plus-compensation scheme, and poor quality is not offset by extra features.

distractors.text: Quality can be traded off to hit the deadline as long as the product's grade, meaning its class of features, is kept at the level first promised to the customer.
distractors.trap: Quality is not a schedule trade-off; conformance must be maintained.

explanation: Grade is the category or level of features and can legitimately be low, but quality means conformance to requirements and is never acceptable when poor.

prompts: A quality manager is categorizing cost-of-quality items for a project. Which item is a cost of conformance rather than a cost of non-conformance?
A cost engineer is sorting quality-related expenses into conformance and non-conformance buckets. Which item is a cost of conformance?
During cost-of-quality analysis, a project accountant must identify which expense is a cost of conformance rather than of non-conformance. Which is it?

correct.text: Quality training together with product inspection and testing are costs of conformance.
correct.why: Prevention (training) and appraisal (inspection/testing) costs are spent to avoid defects.

correct.text: Prevention spending such as training and appraisal spending such as inspection and testing are costs of conformance.
correct.why: Both categories are incurred to prevent or detect defects before delivery.

correct.text: Money spent on training and on inspecting and testing deliverables to prevent defects is a cost of conformance.
correct.why: These are proactive quality investments, not failure costs.

distractors.text: Rework and scrap generated by internal defects discovered before the product ever reaches the customer, which teams often misclassify as ordinary conformance spending.
distractors.trap: These are internal failure costs, a form of cost of non-conformance.

distractors.text: Warranty claims and product recalls that arise after the deliverable has been handed over to the customer and defects surface in the field during actual use.
distractors.trap: These are external failure costs, a form of cost of non-conformance.

distractors.text: Liability costs and the lost future business that result from defective products reaching the customer and damaging the organization's hard-earned reputation.
distractors.trap: These are external failure costs, a category of cost of non-conformance.

distractors.text: The internal failure costs of downtime, re-testing, and re-inspection that are incurred when defective work is sent back to be corrected before it can be released.
distractors.trap: Rework-driven downtime and re-testing are non-conformance costs.

distractors.text: The external failure costs of expedited replacement shipping and on-site repair visits needed to fix defective products that were already delivered to end customers.
distractors.trap: Post-delivery fixes are external failure, a cost of non-conformance.

distractors.text: The penalty payments and legal settlement costs owed to clients under contract when delivered deliverables fail to meet the agreed acceptance criteria in the field.
distractors.trap: Penalties for delivered defects are external failure, a cost of non-conformance.

explanation: Costs of conformance are prevention (e.g., training) and appraisal (e.g., inspection and testing) costs spent to avoid defects, unlike failure costs.

prompts: A Kanban team finds work piling up in the testing column while developers keep pulling new stories into development. The team wants a Lean mechanism that exposes the bottleneck and forces work to be finished before more is started. What should they apply?
A Kanban team notices cards stacking up in the review column while developers keep starting new work. Which Lean mechanism forces finishing before starting and exposes the bottleneck?
A support team's board shows testing overwhelmed while new tickets keep entering development. What Lean practice should they adopt to surface and manage the constraint?

correct.text: Set a work-in-progress (WIP) limit on the board columns to expose the bottleneck.
correct.why: Limiting WIP surfaces bottlenecks and pulls the team toward finishing started work.

correct.text: Apply WIP limits so the team must finish started work before pulling any new work in.
correct.why: WIP limits enforce finish-before-start and reveal the constraint.

correct.text: Introduce a WIP limit on the columns, which surfaces the constraint and pulls the team toward completing work.
correct.why: The core Lean flow mechanism for this exact symptom.

distractors.text: Increase the batch size so more stories move through the testing column together to clear the visible backlog faster.
distractors.trap: Larger batches worsen flow and hide bottlenecks, the opposite of Lean principles.

distractors.text: Move the unfinished testing work into the next iteration and let the developers keep pulling new stories into development in the current one.
distractors.trap: Ignores the bottleneck and increases work in progress, which is the root problem.

distractors.text: Remove the testing column from the board and fold its work into the development column to simplify how the flow is visualized.
distractors.trap: Hides the problem rather than managing flow; the testing work still must be done.

distractors.text: Add a second, parallel testing column so the team has somewhere to place the overflow work while development keeps starting new stories.
distractors.trap: Adding capacity for overflow lets WIP grow instead of limiting it.

distractors.text: Reassign the developers to help with testing after they have first started the remaining backlog stories for the iteration.
distractors.trap: Starting the rest of the backlog first maximizes WIP, worsening the bottleneck.

distractors.text: Extend the length of the iteration so there is more calendar time for the testing column to catch up with the pace of the development column.
distractors.trap: More time does not address the flow imbalance; the bottleneck persists.

explanation: A WIP limit on the board columns exposes the bottleneck and forces the team to finish started work before pulling new work, the core Lean flow mechanism.

prompts: A project manager new to the organization is drafting the resource section of the project plan. A project's resource plan should include which of the following?
A newly promoted PM on a hospital construction program is assembling the resource plan. What should that resource plan cover?
An IT project lead is writing the resourcing section of a plan for a data-center migration. Which elements belong in a complete resource plan?
A first-time PM in a manufacturing firm is documenting resources for a new production line. A well-formed resource plan should include which of the following?

correct.text: Both human and physical resources, with the quantity, type, timing, and source documented for each one.
correct.why: PMBOK Guide 8 (Resources Performance Domain) covers both human and physical resources, addressing quantity, type, timing, and source.

correct.text: A combined listing of people and physical assets, each recorded with its type, quantity, timing, and source.
correct.why: A complete resource plan spans both resource categories and captures the same acquisition attributes for each.

correct.text: People and physical resources together, capturing how many are needed, of what kind, when, and from where.
correct.why: Quantity, type, timing, and source form the basis for acquiring and managing every resource the project uses.

distractors.text: A plan limited to the physical resources — the equipment, materials, and facilities — required for project execution.
distractors.trap: A resource plan must address human resources too, not just physical assets.

distractors.text: A plan covering just the human resources the project needs across its planned phases and activities.
distractors.trap: This omits the physical resources that the plan is equally responsible for defining.

distractors.text: A plan confined to the contracted vendor resources brought in from outside the organization, with internal staff and equipment arranged elsewhere.
distractors.trap: Internal human and physical resources belong in the plan too; externally contracted vendors are one slice of it.

distractors.text: A detailed staffing roster naming the individual team members with their hourly billing rates, while setting aside the physical equipment, materials, and facilities the work requires.
distractors.trap: Names and rates are staffing detail, but the plan must also cover the physical resources the work needs.

distractors.text: The organizational breakdown structure paired with a detailed RACI responsibility chart that maps who is accountable for delivering each area of the project's planned work.
distractors.trap: Responsibility assignments do not quantify or schedule the actual human and physical resources needed.

distractors.text: The resources that carry a direct budget cost, setting aside the internally shared equipment and facilities the organization treats as overhead.
distractors.trap: Shared and overhead resources still need planning; cost treatment does not decide what belongs in the plan.

distractors.text: A prioritized procurement schedule for the externally purchased materials, with internal labor arranged separately by the relevant functional managers.
distractors.trap: The resource plan is meant to integrate labor and materials, not push labor planning outside the plan.

explanation: A project's resource plan covers both human and physical resources, documenting the quantity, type, timing, and source of each as the basis for acquisition and management.

prompts: A predictive project is mid-execution. Market conditions have changed, and the planned deliverable will produce significantly less business value than originally expected. What should the project manager do?
Halfway through a predictive product build, a competitor's launch has sharply reduced the expected value of the planned deliverable. How should the PM respond?
A regulatory change mid-project means a compliance system still under construction will now deliver far less value than its business case promised. What should the PM do?

correct.text: Raise the value change to governance and propose adjusting the effort by re-scoping, re-prioritizing, or stopping.
correct.why: PMBOK Guide 8 'Focus on Value': value is the primary indicator of project success, so an eroding value case belongs with governance.

correct.text: Escalate the drop in expected value to the governing body and recommend re-scoping, re-prioritizing, or halting the work.
correct.why: Decisions to alter or stop a project on value grounds sit with governance, informed by the PM's analysis.

correct.text: Bring the reduced business value to governance and jointly decide whether to re-scope, re-prioritize, or terminate.
correct.why: The PM surfaces the value change and options; the governing body owns the direction-setting decision.

distractors.text: Reduce the project's overall budget and continue delivering it at a correspondingly reduced scope aligned to the new market conditions
distractors.trap: Adjusting budget and scope without governance sidesteps the decision the value change calls for.

distractors.text: Update the project business case with the revised, lower value figures and store the updated document in the project records for later reference
distractors.trap: Records the value change instead of raising it to governance for a decision.

distractors.text: Continue the project as originally planned and deliver the full approved scope defined in the existing, already-signed business case
distractors.trap: A prior approval does not survive a material collapse in the value the project will deliver.

distractors.text: Accelerate delivery to complete the current deliverable quickly, then move the team directly onto the next initiative in the portfolio
distractors.trap: Rushing to finish a low-value deliverable spends resources without confronting the value problem.

distractors.text: Keep managing the value shortfall within the team's own reporting and plan to disclose it to stakeholders in the eventual project closeout report
distractors.trap: Deferring disclosure to closeout keeps a material value change from governance when a decision is needed now.

distractors.text: Ask the team to revise the project's cost and benefit estimates and rerun the business-case analysis before raising anything externally
distractors.trap: Re-running the numbers internally is useful but stops short of routing the value decision to governance.

distractors.text: Renegotiate the project's success criteria and performance targets down to the level of value the deliverable will now produce
distractors.trap: Moving the goalposts hides the value problem instead of routing it to governance for a real decision.

explanation: When expected value materially drops, the PM escalates to governance and proposes options — re-scoping, re-prioritizing, or stopping — because value is the primary measure of project success.

prompts: A project manager is being challenged by a governance member about the frequency of formal status reviews on their project. Which is the MOST appropriate frequency for evaluating project status?
A steering-committee member questions why a PM holds formal status reviews as often as they do. What is the most appropriate basis for setting review frequency?
During a portfolio audit, an auditor challenges how often a project's status is formally evaluated. Which approach to setting that frequency is most appropriate?

correct.text: Set a cadence appropriate to the project's rate of change, risk profile, and decision-making needs.
correct.why: PMBOK Guide 8: status evaluation cadence is contextual — high-uncertainty projects warrant more frequent inspection.

correct.text: Match the review frequency to how fast the project changes, how risky it is, and when decisions are required.
correct.why: Cadence is driven by context, not by a fixed universal interval.

correct.text: Choose an evaluation rhythm driven by the project's volatility, level of uncertainty, and upcoming decision points.
correct.why: Volatile, higher-risk work is inspected more often; stable work less often.

distractors.text: A fixed daily status review, scheduled at the same time each day and carried through from the start of the project to its close.
distractors.trap: A daily cadence set in advance ignores the project's context and burdens stable, low-risk work.

distractors.text: A single comprehensive status evaluation carried out once at project closeout, after the deliverables have been completed.
distractors.trap: An end-only review forfeits the in-flight visibility that lets the team correct course.

distractors.text: A fixed quarterly status review, held on a set calendar interval and kept the same throughout the life of the project.
distractors.trap: A rigid quarterly cadence can be too slow for fast-moving or higher-risk projects.

distractors.text: Whatever standard review interval the project management office mandates for the projects across its wider portfolio.
distractors.trap: A portfolio-wide PMO interval overrides the project-specific factors that should set cadence.

distractors.text: A review frequency set to match the reporting and visibility expectations of the project's most engaged stakeholder.
distractors.trap: Letting the most vocal stakeholder set cadence replaces risk and change with politics.

distractors.text: A review cadence tied to the finance department's monthly accounting close and aligned with its regular reporting calendar.
distractors.trap: The accounting cycle is unrelated to the project's rate of change and decision needs.

distractors.text: Status reviews carried out at each point where a measured performance variance crosses a predefined control-account threshold.
distractors.trap: Waiting for a breach removes the proactive inspection that catches problems early.

explanation: The appropriate status-evaluation cadence is contextual, driven by the project's rate of change, risk profile, and decision needs rather than a fixed universal interval.

prompts: Vendor performance has been weakening. The PM has documented missed dates and quality issues. What should the PM do FIRST?
A supplier's delivery dates and quality have been slipping, and the PM has logged each miss. What should the PM do FIRST?
A subcontractor on a construction project has repeatedly missed milestones, all documented by the PM. What is the PM's best first action?

correct.text: Communicate the documented concerns to the vendor and engage them on root cause and corrective actions.
correct.why: PMBOK Guide 8 (procurement) and ECO Task 5 'Evaluate vendor performance': a formal performance dialog precedes contract enforcement.

correct.text: Share the recorded performance issues with the vendor and work jointly on identifying causes and remedies.
correct.why: Corrective action starts with a direct, evidence-based conversation before escalation.

correct.text: Meet with the vendor to review the documented shortfalls and agree on corrective measures going forward.
correct.why: Engaging the vendor on the evidence is the first, least-drastic step.

distractors.text: Apply the contract's payment-adjustment provisions for the missed deliverables and notify the vendor of the deductions being made.
distractors.trap: A contractual payment remedy is a later step, not the first response before a performance dialog.

distractors.text: Review the contract's service-level terms and document each area where the vendor's performance has fallen short of the agreed thresholds.
distractors.trap: Continues internal documentation instead of opening the corrective dialog with the vendor.

distractors.text: Begin the contract termination process for the vendor based on the documented performance shortfalls to date.
distractors.trap: Termination is a last resort, not the first response to correctable performance issues.

distractors.text: Escalate to the vendor's executive leadership and request a replacement account team before meeting with the current one.
distractors.trap: Jumping over the current team skips the corrective conversation and damages the working relationship.

distractors.text: Begin sourcing a replacement supplier in parallel while the current vendor continues delivering its remaining scope.
distractors.trap: Re-sourcing in parallel skips the corrective dialog and ignores current contractual obligations.
