correct.text: Continuous integration frequently merges and validates code changes, while continuous delivery extends that flow to keep every validated increment release-ready
correct.why: Integration is about merging and validating; delivery is about staying ready to release.

correct.text: Continuous integration merges and automatically checks work often, whereas continuous delivery keeps those tested increments in a state that can be released at any time
correct.why: One validates merges; the other maintains release readiness.

correct.text: Continuous integration validates merged code through automated checks, and continuous delivery then ensures the result is always ready to ship
correct.why: Validation of merges versus continuous release readiness.

distractors.text: Continuous delivery is the practice that frequently merges and validates code changes in the shared codebase, while continuous integration is the follow-on practice that keeps each finished increment ready to release
distractors.trap: Reversed: integration merges and validates; delivery keeps work release-ready.

distractors.text: Both terms simply mean deploying the latest validated code straight to production automatically on each commit that any developer pushes to the shared repository
distractors.trap: Automatic production deploy describes continuous deployment, not these two practices, and conflates them.

distractors.text: Continuous integration is a formal planning event held at the start of the sprint, while continuous delivery is the retrospective the team runs together once the sprint has finished
distractors.trap: Both are engineering practices, not Scrum events.

distractors.text: Continuous integration means each developer works in a separate long-lived branch, while continuous delivery is the large merge event where all those branches are finally combined before a release
distractors.trap: CI is defined by frequent merging into a shared codebase, the opposite of long-lived isolated branches.

distractors.text: Continuous integration refers to keeping the team's tools and licenses up to date, while continuous delivery refers to shipping the signed contract deliverables to the customer on schedule
distractors.trap: Both terms describe automated code practices, not tooling maintenance or contractual deliverables.

distractors.text: Continuous integration is a manual code-review checkpoint performed by the architect, while continuous delivery is the automated pipeline that runs without that review step
distractors.trap: CI centers on automated build-and-test on merge, not a manual review gate.

distractors.text: Both describe the same practice of two developers pairing at one workstation, integrating and delivering the code together in real time
distractors.trap: That describes pair programming; CI and CD are distinct automation practices, not the same thing.

explanation: Continuous integration frequently merges and automatically validates code changes; continuous delivery extends that flow so every validated increment stays ready for immediate release.

prompts: A developer reorganizes the internal structure of a module to make it cleaner and easier to change, taking care that its external behavior stays exactly the same. Which practice is this, and how does it differ from defect repair?

An engineer rewrites the internals of a component so it is simpler to maintain, while making sure every output remains identical to before. What is this practice called, and how does it differ from defect repair?

A team tidies the internal design of working code to reduce future maintenance cost, deliberately preserving its observable behavior. Which practice describes this, and how does it contrast with defect repair?
During a code review, someone restructures a function purely to improve readability without altering what it produces. Name the practice and explain how it differs from defect repair.

correct.text: Refactoring, which improves internal design while keeping external behavior identical, whereas defect repair corrects behavior that is wrong or failing
correct.why: Refactoring preserves behavior and improves structure; defect repair changes incorrect behavior.

correct.text: Refactoring, because it restructures code without altering what it does, unlike defect repair which changes behavior that was broken
correct.why: Structure changes with behavior held constant versus fixing a malfunction.

correct.text: Refactoring, since it cleans up the internal structure with no change to outputs, in contrast to fixing an actual malfunction
correct.why: Behavior-preserving cleanup versus correcting a fault.

distractors.text: Defect repair, since any change a developer makes to existing code, whether structural or behavioral, amounts to correcting a fault in the delivered product
distractors.trap: Refactoring changes structure without changing behavior, so it is not defect repair.

distractors.text: Gold plating, since cleaning up code that already works adds unrequested polish that goes beyond the functionality the customer asked the team to deliver
distractors.trap: Refactoring adds no features; gold plating is unrequested extra functionality.

distractors.text: Hardening, since this kind of internal structural cleanup is the work that teams set aside for a dedicated hardening iteration near the end of a release
distractors.trap: Refactoring can occur continuously and is not confined to a hardening iteration.

distractors.text: Reengineering, since deliberately reworking a module's internal design to make it easier to maintain is exactly the kind of redesign that term names
distractors.trap: Reengineering overhauls a system's design and behavior, whereas refactoring preserves external behavior and adds nothing.

distractors.text: Preventive action, since reorganizing the structure is a documented change request raised to head off a defect that has not yet appeared anywhere in the system
distractors.trap: Preventive action targets a possible future problem; refactoring is behavior-preserving cleanup, not a risk response.

distractors.text: Corrective action, since a deliberate modification made by a developer to bring a component back into line with the agreed project plan falls under that heading
distractors.trap: Corrective action addresses nonconforming work; refactoring changes conforming, working code without altering behavior.

distractors.text: Technical debt repayment, since paying down accumulated shortcuts in the codebase is what any change to a module's internal structure amounts to
distractors.trap: Refactoring may reduce technical debt but it is behavior-preserving cleanup that can occur anytime, not by definition debt repayment.

explanation: Refactoring improves the internal design of code while keeping external behavior identical; defect repair, by contrast, changes behavior that is wrong or failing.

prompts: Earned Value and Actual Cost are easy to confuse because both relate to work that is underway. What truly distinguishes them?
A PM is reviewing a cost report and keeps mixing up the budgeted worth of completed work with the money actually paid to do it. What is the real difference between Earned Value and Actual Cost?
A sponsor asks why the figure for the value of work performed differs from the amount actually spent this period. How should you distinguish Earned Value from Actual Cost?
During an earned value analysis a team debates which number reflects the authorized budget for finished work and which reflects real expenditure. What truly separates Earned Value from Actual Cost?

correct.text: Earned Value is the budgeted value of the work completed, while Actual Cost is the real money actually spent to complete it
correct.why: EV is measured in budget terms while AC is the actual expenditure.

correct.text: Earned Value expresses finished work in budget terms, whereas Actual Cost is the true expenditure incurred to do that work
correct.why: Budgeted worth of completed work versus real money spent.

correct.text: Earned Value measures completed work at its authorized budget, while Actual Cost measures what was really paid out
correct.why: Authorized budget of work done versus cash actually spent.

distractors.text: Earned Value is the money actually spent so far, while Actual Cost is the budgeted value that was planned for the completed work
distractors.trap: Reverses the two: Earned Value is expressed in budget terms and Actual Cost is the money spent.

distractors.text: Earned Value is the total approved budget for the whole project, while Actual Cost is the time-phased portion of that budget spread across the schedule
distractors.trap: The total budget is BAC and its time-phased form is PV; neither describes EV or AC here.

distractors.text: Earned Value and Actual Cost tend to converge to the same figure once the work is finished, differing while the work is still in progress
distractors.trap: They coincide only when cost performance is exactly on plan; any cost variance keeps them apart even at completion.

distractors.text: Earned Value is the forecast of what the remaining work will eventually cost, while Actual Cost is the original budget approved for the whole project at the outset
distractors.trap: A forecast of remaining cost is EAC or ETC and the approved total is BAC; neither is EV or AC.

distractors.text: Earned Value is measured mainly at project closeout during final reconciliation, while Actual Cost is tracked continuously across the reporting periods
distractors.trap: EV is measured every reporting period alongside AC, not mainly at closeout.

distractors.text: Earned Value is schedule progress expressed as days ahead or behind plan, while Actual Cost is that same progress expressed in dollars of cost variance
distractors.trap: EV is a budgeted-cost measure, not a schedule figure, and cost variance is EV minus AC rather than AC itself.

distractors.text: Earned Value and Actual Cost are two labels for the same underlying figure, differing chiefly in which team, finance or delivery, reports it
distractors.trap: They are distinct measures that coincide only when actual cost equals the budgeted value of the work done.

explanation: Earned Value is the budgeted value of the work completed, while Actual Cost is the real money actually spent to complete that work.

prompts: Contingency reserve and management reserve both set money aside, but they are governed very differently. Which statement is correct?

A PM must explain to finance why one pot of money is inside the cost baseline for known risks and another sits above it for surprises. Which statement about contingency versus management reserve is correct?

During budget planning a sponsor asks which reserve the project manager can draw on directly for identified risks and which requires management release for unforeseen work. Which statement is correct?
An auditor wants to know why one reserve is part of the cost baseline and the other is held outside it. Which statement correctly distinguishes contingency reserve from management reserve?

correct.text: Contingency reserve covers identified risks and sits inside the cost baseline, while management reserve covers unforeseen work and sits outside the baseline
correct.why: Contingency is part of the baseline the PM controls for known risks; management reserve is held above the baseline for unknown-unknowns.

correct.text: Contingency reserve is held within the baseline for known risks the PM manages, whereas management reserve is held above the baseline for unknown-unknowns
correct.why: In-baseline known-risk funding versus out-of-baseline unforeseen funding.

correct.text: Contingency reserve funds identified risks from inside the baseline, while management reserve funds unforeseen work from outside it
correct.why: The dividing line is inside versus outside the cost baseline.

distractors.text: Management reserve is the reserve that covers identified risks and sits inside the cost baseline, while contingency reserve is the pot that covers unforeseen work and is held outside the baseline
distractors.trap: Reversed - contingency is the in-baseline reserve for known risks, management reserve is the out-of-baseline reserve.

distractors.text: Both reserves sit inside the cost baseline and both are controlled by the project manager, who can draw down on either one as needed without going to higher management for release
distractors.trap: Only contingency is in the baseline; management reserve is outside it and usually released by management, not the PM alone.

distractors.text: Both reserves sit outside the cost baseline, and each one calls for a formal change-control approval before the project manager is permitted to spend from them
distractors.trap: Contingency is inside the baseline and can be drawn on as risks occur; only management reserve requires escalation and a baseline change.

distractors.text: Contingency reserve is set aside for schedule delays while management reserve is set aside for cost overruns, so each reserve protects a different project constraint
distractors.trap: Both are cost reserves; the real distinction is known versus unknown risks and inside versus outside the baseline, not schedule versus cost.

distractors.text: Contingency reserve is funded by the customer as a separate line item on the invoice, while management reserve is drawn from the performing organization's general overhead account
distractors.trap: Funding source is not the distinction; contingency sits within the baseline and management reserve above it, regardless of who funds them.

distractors.text: Both reserves are sized as a flat ten percent of the total project budget and are released as soon as a risk event has been logged against the project
distractors.trap: Reserves are sized by risk analysis, not a fixed percentage, and management reserve requires management approval to release.

distractors.text: Contingency reserve calls for formal steering-committee approval before each use, while management reserve can be drawn by the project manager as project needs arise
distractors.trap: Reversed control: contingency is the PM's to use for known risks, and management reserve is what needs higher approval.

explanation: Contingency reserve funds identified risks and lives inside the cost baseline under PM control; management reserve funds unforeseen (unknown-unknown) work and sits outside the baseline, released by management.

prompts: A critical production system has just failed during a live event and there is no time to analyze options or run experiments. Under the Cynefin framework, which domain is this and what is the correct first move?

During a live product launch, the payment service collapses and there is no time to run experiments or weigh options before customers are affected. In Cynefin terms, which domain is this and what is the first move?
A hospital's patient-records system goes down in the middle of an emergency, leaving no opportunity to analyze root cause first. Which Cynefin domain applies and what should the leader do first?

correct.text: This is the chaotic domain, so act first to stabilize the situation, then sense and respond
correct.why: In the chaotic domain there is no time to gather data, so the leader acts immediately to establish order before sensing and responding.

correct.text: Treat it as chaotic and take immediate action to restore order before sensing and responding
correct.why: Chaos calls for act-sense-respond, not analysis, because the priority is stopping the collapse.

correct.text: In chaos there is no time to gather data, so establish stability first, then sense and respond
correct.why: Acting to impose order is the correct opening move in the chaotic domain.

distractors.text: Complex domain: probe with small experiments before acting, since the situation is emergent
distractors.trap: Probing takes time the crisis does not allow; experimentation fits the complex domain, not an urgent collapse.

distractors.text: Complicated domain: pause and bring in experts to analyze the failure before choosing a response
distractors.trap: Analysis by experts fits the complicated domain; in chaos there is no time to analyze before acting.

distractors.text: Clear domain: apply the standard documented best-practice procedure that fits a stable situation
distractors.trap: A clear-domain best practice assumes a stable, well-understood situation, which an unfolding crisis is not.

distractors.text: Disorder domain: first break the incident into smaller parts and assign each piece to the domain that best matches it before acting
distractors.trap: Sorting into disorder is a diagnostic step that consumes the very time an active collapse denies you.

distractors.text: Complex domain: run a controlled pilot and study the feedback loops carefully before committing to any stabilizing action
distractors.trap: Running pilots and watching feedback loops is emergent complex-domain work, not a response to an active outage.

distractors.text: Complicated domain: convene a formal root-cause analysis with subject-matter experts to diagnose the failure thoroughly before responding
distractors.trap: Root-cause analysis is good-practice complicated-domain work but far too slow while the system is down live.

distractors.text: Clear domain: follow the documented incident runbook step by step because the cause is already well understood and repeatable
distractors.trap: A runbook assumes a known cause-and-effect situation; a novel live collapse is not a clear-domain routine.

explanation: A live, novel failure with no time to analyze sits in the chaotic domain, where the correct sequence is act-sense-respond: stabilize first, then investigate.

prompts: A team uses one checklist to decide whether an item is ready to be pulled into a Sprint and another to decide whether the finished item can be called complete. Which pairing correctly maps the Definition of Ready and the Definition of Done?

A development team keeps one checklist an item must satisfy before it can enter a Sprint, and a second checklist a finished item must satisfy to be called shippable. Which pairing maps the Definition of Ready and the Definition of Done?

Before pulling a user story into the Sprint, a team verifies it against one set of criteria; after coding it, they verify it against another. Which is the Definition of Ready and which is the Definition of Done?

correct.text: Definition of Ready sets the entry criteria for starting work, and Definition of Done sets the completion criteria for finishing it
correct.why: Ready is the gate to begin; Done is the gate to call work complete.

correct.text: Ready is the gate to begin an item, while Done is the gate that confirms the item is truly complete
correct.why: One governs starting, the other governs finishing.

correct.text: The Definition of Ready qualifies an item to be pulled in, and the Definition of Done confirms it meets completion standards
correct.why: Ready is about entry; Done is about acceptance of finished work.

distractors.text: The Definition of Ready confirms that a finished item is complete, while the Definition of Done confirms that an item is ready to be started
distractors.trap: Reversed: Ready is the gate to begin an item and Done is the gate that confirms it is finished.

distractors.text: Both are simply completion criteria describing finished work, just captured at different levels of detail and rigor for different audiences
distractors.trap: Only the Definition of Done concerns completion; the Definition of Ready is about an item's readiness to begin.

distractors.text: The Definition of Ready is owned by the customer, while the Definition of Done is owned and maintained by the Product Owner
distractors.trap: Both are team agreements rather than singular external ownership.

distractors.text: The Definition of Ready is a contractual document signed off by the customer, while the Definition of Done is set by senior management
distractors.trap: Neither is an external contract; both are collaborative team agreements, not top-down mandates.

distractors.text: Both are really the same quality checklist, simply applied once at the start and once at the end of the Sprint by different people
distractors.trap: They are distinct agreements with different purposes, not one checklist reused at two moments.

distractors.text: Definition of Ready lists all the acceptance tests to run, and Definition of Done lists the story points that were assigned to the item
distractors.trap: Done is not a point tally, and Ready is broader than a test list; both are readiness and completion criteria.

distractors.text: Definition of Ready is strictly enforced by the Scrum Master, while Definition of Done is optional and rarely written down anywhere
distractors.trap: Done is a core Scrum commitment, not optional, and neither is enforced by one role alone.

explanation: Definition of Ready is the entry gate that qualifies an item to start; Definition of Done is the completion gate that qualifies finished work as shippable.

prompts: One team reports it usually completes about 30 story points per Sprint; another reports it finishes about 12 work items per week regardless of size. Which measures are these, respectively?
A Scrum team says it typically burns down roughly 25 story points each Sprint, while a Kanban team says it ships about 15 tickets a week no matter their size. Which measure is each team reporting?
One team's dashboard shows 40 points delivered per iteration; another team's dashboard shows 8 items completed per day. Respectively, which metrics are these?

correct.text: Velocity for the story points per Sprint, and throughput for the count of items completed per period
correct.why: Velocity is story points per iteration; throughput counts items completed over time.

correct.text: The story points per Sprint is velocity, while the number of items finished per period is throughput
correct.why: Velocity measures points per iteration; throughput measures item count over time.

correct.text: Story points per iteration is velocity, and the number of items finished over time is throughput
correct.why: The point-based measure is velocity and the count-based measure is throughput.

distractors.text: Throughput for the story points per Sprint, and velocity for the count of items completed each week
distractors.trap: Reversed: story points per Sprint is velocity, and item count is throughput.

distractors.text: Both figures are really velocity, just expressed in the different units of story points versus item counts
distractors.trap: Throughput is a separate metric that counts items, not story points per iteration.

distractors.text: Both figures are cycle time measured two different ways across the team's overall workflow
distractors.trap: Cycle time measures duration per item, not points per Sprint or item counts per period.

distractors.text: Both figures are lead time, one measured in accumulated story points and the other in the number of items delivered
distractors.trap: Lead time is a duration from request to delivery, not a points total or a completed-item count.

distractors.text: The story points per Sprint is throughput and the item count per week is takt time that paces delivery to demand
distractors.trap: Takt time is the customer demand rate, not an item count, and throughput is items rather than points.

distractors.text: The points figure is the team's capacity and the item count is its work in progress limiting the flow of delivery
distractors.trap: Capacity and WIP are planning and flow constraints, not the completed-work measures velocity and throughput.

distractors.text: The points figure is utilization and the item count is the team's efficiency ratio over the reporting period
distractors.trap: Utilization and efficiency ratios are not what these numbers report; they are velocity and throughput.

explanation: Story points completed per iteration is velocity; the count of work items finished over a time period is throughput.

prompts: A coach explains that agile decisions rest on three pillars: making work visible, frequently checking results, and adjusting based on what is observed. Which principle and pillars are being described?

An agile mentor says the team's decisions rely on three things: keeping work openly visible, checking outcomes at regular intervals, and adjusting course from what they learn. Which principle and pillars is she describing?
A Scrum Master explains that the team avoids heavy up-front planning and instead makes work visible, reviews it often, and adapts. What underlying principle and its three pillars are being described?

correct.text: Empiricism, supported by the three pillars of transparency, inspection, and adaptation
correct.why: Empirical process control bases decisions on observed experience through transparency, inspection, and adaptation.

correct.text: This is empirical process control, whose pillars are transparency, inspection, and adaptation
correct.why: Decisions come from observed experience via transparency, inspection, and adaptation.

correct.text: The principle is empiricism, resting on transparency, inspection, and adaptation
correct.why: Making work visible, inspecting it, and adapting are the three empirical pillars.

distractors.text: Predictive control, supported by the constructs of baselines, variance, and formal change control
distractors.trap: Those are predictive constructs, not the empirical pillars of transparency, inspection, and adaptation.

distractors.text: Empiricism, supported instead by the activities of planning, estimating, and reporting each iteration
distractors.trap: The pillars are transparency, inspection, and adaptation, not planning and reporting activities.

distractors.text: Lean thinking, supported by the concepts of value, flow, and pull across the workflow
distractors.trap: Those are Lean concepts; the three pillars named belong to empirical process control.

distractors.text: Empiricism, supported by the Scrum values of commitment, courage, and respect that the team lives by daily
distractors.trap: Commitment, courage, and respect are Scrum values, not the three empirical pillars of the framework.

distractors.text: Empirical process control, resting on transparency, verification, and the continuous documentation of results
distractors.trap: Verification and documentation are not the named pillars; the third pillar is adaptation, not paperwork.

distractors.text: Predictive control, grounded in detailed up-front baselines, earned-value variance analysis, and formal change-control boards
distractors.trap: This describes a plan-driven governance model, the opposite of the empirical inspect-and-adapt approach.

distractors.text: Lean thinking, supported by identifying value, mapping the value stream, and establishing pull throughout the delivery system
distractors.trap: These are Lean principles, whereas the scenario clearly describes the empirical pillars of Scrum.

explanation: Basing decisions on observed experience is empiricism, which Scrum supports through the three pillars of transparency, inspection, and adaptation.

prompts: A coach says a Sprint is just a particular kind of iteration. Which statement best captures the relationship between Sprint and iteration?
A new team member asks whether Sprint and iteration mean the same thing. Which statement best captures how the two relate?
A trainer notes that all Sprints are iterations but not all iterations are Sprints. Which explanation of the relationship is correct?

correct.text: A Sprint is the specific Scrum timebox for producing an increment, while iteration is the broader term for any repeated cycle of work
correct.why: Sprint is the Scrum-specific name; iteration is the general concept.

correct.text: Sprint is the Scrum-specific name for a timeboxed cycle, and iteration is the general concept it falls under
correct.why: Every Sprint is an iteration, but iteration is the umbrella term.

correct.text: Every Sprint is an iteration, but iteration is the umbrella term used across many agile frameworks
correct.why: Sprint is a kind of iteration, specific to Scrum.

distractors.text: An iteration is the Scrum-specific term for the timebox, while a Sprint is the general word for a repeated work cycle used across agile frameworks.
distractors.trap: Reversed: Sprint is the Scrum-specific term and iteration is the general one.

distractors.text: They are unrelated concepts, since a Sprint is a type of team ceremony while an iteration is a kind of burndown progress chart used for reporting.
distractors.trap: Both refer to cycles of work, not a meeting or a chart; they are related, not unrelated.

distractors.text: A Sprint is by definition longer than an ordinary iteration and, unlike a general iteration, tends to span at least four full calendar weeks.
distractors.trap: There is no rule that a Sprint must be longer; it is a kind of iteration, not a longer one.

distractors.text: A Sprint applies chiefly to software teams, whereas an iteration is the term used for hardware and manufacturing development cycles.
distractors.trap: Neither term is restricted by industry; both describe timeboxed cycles regardless of the product.

distractors.text: An iteration contains several Sprints nested inside it as smaller sub-cycles, each one delivering a portion of the overall increment.
distractors.trap: The nesting is backwards; a Sprint is a type of iteration, not a container of them.

distractors.text: A Sprint is a fixed two-week period set by common convention, while an iteration may run to whatever length the delivering team chooses.
distractors.trap: Sprint length is not fixed at two weeks; it is a timebox the team sets, like any iteration.

explanation: A Sprint is Scrum's specific name for a timeboxed iteration that produces an increment; iteration is the broader, framework-agnostic term.

prompts: A team holds one ongoing activity to clarify, split, estimate, and reorder upcoming backlog items, and a separate session to select and commit the work for the next cycle. Which describes backlog refinement versus Sprint planning?

A Scrum team runs one continuous activity to clarify, split, and re-order upcoming stories, and a separate timeboxed session to pick and commit the work for the coming Sprint. Which contrasts backlog refinement with Sprint planning?
Throughout the Sprint a team keeps grooming future backlog items, and at the Sprint boundary it holds a session to commit the next batch of work. Which describes refinement versus Sprint planning?

correct.text: Backlog refinement prepares items ahead of time, while Sprint planning selects and commits the near-term work for the iteration
correct.why: Refinement readies the backlog continuously; planning commits the next Sprint.

correct.text: Refinement continuously readies upcoming backlog items, and Sprint planning commits the work for the next Sprint
correct.why: One is ongoing preparation, the other is the commitment session.

correct.text: The ongoing clarify-and-split activity is backlog refinement, and the commitment session is Sprint planning
correct.why: Refinement prepares; planning commits.

distractors.text: Sprint planning prepares the items ahead of time, while backlog refinement commits the iteration work
distractors.trap: Reversed: refinement is the ongoing preparation and planning makes the commitment.

distractors.text: Both are really just the same single meeting, known under two interchangeable names that different agile guides happen to use for it
distractors.trap: Synonym trap: refinement is continuous preparation and planning is the commitment session.

distractors.text: Backlog refinement demonstrates the finished increment, while Sprint planning reflects on how the process went
distractors.trap: Those describe the Sprint review and the retrospective, not refinement and planning.

distractors.text: Sprint planning is the continuous grooming activity spread across the whole Sprint, while backlog refinement is the one-time commitment ceremony held each cycle
distractors.trap: This inverts both events; refinement is the ongoing grooming and planning is the boundary commitment.

distractors.text: Backlog refinement is where the team gives the sponsor a demo of working software, and Sprint planning is where it inspects and adapts its way of working
distractors.trap: A demo and a process inspection are the review and retrospective, not refinement or planning.

distractors.text: Backlog refinement is run solely by the Product Owner in isolation, while Sprint planning is handled entirely by the Scrum Master on the team's behalf
distractors.trap: Both are collaborative team activities, not solo events owned by a single role.

distractors.text: Backlog refinement locks the Sprint scope so it cannot change, while Sprint planning leaves the upcoming items deliberately open and unestimated
distractors.trap: This reverses their purposes; refinement keeps items flexible and planning is what sets the Sprint commitment.

explanation: Backlog refinement is the ongoing preparation of upcoming items; Sprint planning is the session where the team selects and commits the work for the next iteration.

prompts: A Scrum Team holds one objective that gives purpose to the current Sprint and another that describes a future state of the product to aim for over many Sprints. Which describes the Sprint goal versus the product goal?

A Scrum Team keeps one objective that gives focus to the work of the current Sprint and another that describes the future product state they are building toward over many Sprints. Which contrasts the Sprint goal with the product goal?
A product owner points to a short-horizon objective for this Sprint and a long-horizon objective for the product overall. Which distinguishes the Sprint goal from the product goal?

correct.text: The Sprint goal is the near-term objective for one Sprint, while the product goal is the longer-term objective for the product
correct.why: Sprint goal scopes one Sprint; product goal guides many Sprints toward a future product state.

correct.text: The Sprint goal gives purpose to the current Sprint, and the product goal describes the future product state across many Sprints
correct.why: One is single-Sprint, the other spans the whole product.

correct.text: The single-Sprint objective is the Sprint goal, and the multi-Sprint objective is the product goal
correct.why: Different horizons: one Sprint versus the product overall.

distractors.text: The product goal is the near-term objective for one Sprint, while the Sprint goal is the longer-term objective for the product
distractors.trap: Reversed: the Sprint goal is near-term and the product goal is long-term.

distractors.text: The Sprint goal and the product goal are the same objective, tracked on two separate boards for the team's convenience.
distractors.trap: They operate at different horizons, one Sprint versus the whole product.

distractors.text: The Sprint goal is the list of all the backlog items selected for the Sprint, while the product goal is the list of the product's planned release dates
distractors.trap: Neither is a list of items or dates; both are single objectives at different horizons.

distractors.text: The product goal is the immediate target for just the current Sprint, while the Sprint goal describes the long-term vision for the whole product
distractors.trap: This swaps the two horizons; the near-term one is the Sprint goal and the long-term one is the product goal.

distractors.text: The Sprint goal enumerates the acceptance criteria for each story, while the product goal enumerates the budget allocated to each release
distractors.trap: Both are singular objectives, not itemized criteria or budget breakdowns.

distractors.text: The Sprint goal is decided by the developers alone during planning, while the product goal is set by executive stakeholders from outside the team
distractors.trap: Both goals are set collaboratively, with the Product Owner accountable, not imposed by one group.

distractors.text: The Sprint goal has to be fully achieved and formally closed out before the product goal can be defined and written down for the team.
distractors.trap: The product goal is a long-lived objective that exists independently of any single Sprint's completion.

explanation: The Sprint goal is the single objective giving purpose to one Sprint; the product goal is the longer-term objective the product advances toward across many Sprints.

prompts: Cost Variance and Schedule Variance both start from Earned Value, but they subtract different things. Which pair correctly states what each one subtracts?

A project manager computes two earned-value differences: both begin from Earned Value, but one subtracts what was actually spent and the other subtracts what was planned. Which pair correctly states what Cost Variance and Schedule Variance each subtract?
On an earned-value report, Cost Variance and Schedule Variance both start at Earned Value but subtract different terms. Which statement correctly matches each subtraction?

correct.text: Cost Variance subtracts Actual Cost from Earned Value, and Schedule Variance subtracts Planned Value from Earned Value
correct.why: CV equals EV minus AC (money efficiency), while SV equals EV minus PV (progress against the schedule baseline).

correct.text: Cost Variance is Earned Value minus Actual Cost, while Schedule Variance is Earned Value minus Planned Value
correct.why: AC drives the cost difference; PV drives the schedule difference.

correct.text: Cost Variance measures spend against value using Actual Cost, and Schedule Variance measures progress using Planned Value
correct.why: CV uses AC, SV uses PV.

distractors.text: Cost Variance subtracts Planned Value, and Schedule Variance subtracts Actual Cost, from Earned Value
distractors.trap: Reversed: AC is a cost concept and belongs in CV, PV is a schedule concept and belongs in SV.

distractors.text: Both variances subtract Actual Cost from Earned Value, but Schedule Variance then additionally divides the result by the elapsed time
distractors.trap: SV never uses Actual Cost; schedule performance is measured against Planned Value, not spend.

distractors.text: Both variances subtract Planned Value from Earned Value, but Cost Variance then additionally divides that difference by the Actual Cost
distractors.trap: That blends a variance with a ratio; CV uses AC as a subtraction, not PV, and it is a difference, not a quotient.

distractors.text: Cost Variance subtracts the Budget at Completion from Earned Value, while Schedule Variance subtracts the Estimate at Completion from it
distractors.trap: BAC and EAC are whole-project totals, not the AC and PV terms these variances actually use.

distractors.text: Cost Variance divides Earned Value by Actual Cost, and Schedule Variance divides Earned Value by Planned Value, to yield percentages
distractors.trap: Those quotients are the CPI and SPI performance indexes, not the variances, which are subtractions.

distractors.text: Both variances subtract Planned Value, but Cost Variance is then expressed as a running cumulative total across all reporting periods
distractors.trap: CV subtracts Actual Cost, not Planned Value, and neither is defined as a cumulative-only figure.

distractors.text: Cost Variance subtracts Actual Cost, and Schedule Variance subtracts the management reserve, from Earned Value
distractors.trap: Schedule Variance uses Planned Value, not any reserve amount, so the second term is wrong.

explanation: Cost Variance is EV minus Actual Cost (are we under or over budget), while Schedule Variance is EV minus Planned Value (are we ahead of or behind schedule).

prompts: Estimate at Completion and Estimate to Complete sound nearly identical. What is the precise difference in what each forecasts?
A sponsor confuses Estimate to Complete with Estimate at Completion. What is the precise difference in what each one forecasts?

On a status report, one figure predicts the cost of only the work remaining and another predicts the total cost of the entire project. Which is Estimate to Complete and which is Estimate at Completion?

correct.text: Estimate to Complete forecasts the cost of the remaining work only, while Estimate at Completion forecasts the total final cost
correct.why: ETC covers just the work left; EAC equals actual cost to date plus ETC, giving the whole-project total.

correct.text: Estimate to Complete covers just the work that is left, and Estimate at Completion is the whole-project total of money already spent plus ETC
correct.why: ETC is the remainder; EAC is AC-to-date plus that remainder.

correct.text: The remaining-work forecast is Estimate to Complete, and the whole-project final-cost forecast is Estimate at Completion
correct.why: One is the remainder, the other is the full total.

distractors.text: Estimate to Complete is the total final cost of the entire project, while Estimate at Completion is the forecast cost of the work that still remains
distractors.trap: Reversed: the to-complete figure covers just the remaining work, while the at-completion figure is the whole-project total.

distractors.text: Both figures forecast just the cost of the work still remaining, differing merely in the statistical confidence level at which each one is expressed
distractors.trap: Only ETC is the cost of remaining work; EAC adds the money already spent to that remaining amount.

distractors.text: Estimate to Complete is the originally approved project budget, while Estimate at Completion is simply the variance measured away from that approved budget figure
distractors.trap: The approved budget is BAC and the gap from it is VAC; neither of those defines ETC or EAC.

distractors.text: Estimate to Complete is the total projected cost of the whole project, while Estimate at Completion covers just the work that is left to do
distractors.trap: This inverts the two; ETC is the remainder and EAC is the whole-project projection.

distractors.text: Estimate to Complete adds the management reserve to the baseline, while Estimate at Completion subtracts the contingency reserve from it
distractors.trap: Neither figure is defined by adding or subtracting reserves; ETC is remaining work and EAC is the total forecast.

distractors.text: Both figures represent the same whole-project cost total, just recalculated with two different performance indexes at the end of each reporting period
distractors.trap: ETC and EAC are not the same total; ETC is only the remaining portion, while EAC is the full project figure.

distractors.text: Estimate to Complete is the schedule forecast for the remaining project duration, while Estimate at Completion is the final planned calendar finish date for the whole effort
distractors.trap: Both are cost forecasts, not schedule or date forecasts, so this wrongly confuses cost figures with time.

explanation: Estimate to Complete forecasts only the cost of work still remaining; Estimate at Completion forecasts the total final cost, equal to actual cost to date plus ETC.

prompts: A risk that was identified and given a planned response actually occurs. Which pool of money is the project manager expected to draw on first?
A risk the team had already identified and planned a response for actually materializes on the project. Which pool of funds should the project manager tap first?
An identified risk with a documented response strategy occurs during execution. From which reserve is the project manager expected to draw first, without escalating?

correct.text: The contingency reserve, because it was set aside specifically for identified risks that already have response strategies
correct.why: Identified risks within the baseline are funded by contingency reserve, which the PM can use without escalating.

correct.text: Draw on the contingency reserve, since known risks inside the baseline are funded from it without escalation
correct.why: Contingency covers identified, planned-for risks.

correct.text: The contingency reserve is used first, as it funds the identified risks the team already planned responses for
correct.why: Known risks map to contingency, not management reserve.

distractors.text: The management reserve, because any risk that actually materializes counts as an unforeseen event outside the plan
distractors.trap: An identified risk is not unforeseen; management reserve is held for unknown-unknowns outside the baseline.

distractors.text: The cost baseline work budget, by reallocating funds from other in-flight work packages that are currently running under budget
distractors.trap: Reallocating from other work packages distorts performance measurement; the planned funding for known risks is contingency reserve.

distractors.text: No reserve at all, since the project manager must first request a formal change to the approved budget baseline before drawing any funds.
distractors.trap: Using contingency for a known risk does not require a baseline change; it was already inside the baseline for exactly this.

distractors.text: The management reserve first, and then the contingency reserve if the management reserve has been fully drawn down by the impact
distractors.trap: The order is backwards; contingency funds identified risks first, and management reserve covers unknown-unknowns.

distractors.text: The profit margin built into the contract, since a realized risk is treated as a delivery cost rather than a reserve draw
distractors.trap: Eroding margin to cover a planned risk ignores the contingency reserve set aside precisely for this event.

distractors.text: A fresh emergency fund requested from the sponsor, because a realized risk calls for new money beyond the original budget
distractors.trap: No new funding is needed; the identified risk was already provisioned for through the contingency reserve.

distractors.text: The remaining contingency of an unrelated project in the same portfolio, reallocated by the program manager on request
distractors.trap: Reserves are not pooled across projects on demand; this risk is funded by the project's own contingency reserve.

explanation: An identified risk with a planned response is funded from the contingency reserve, which sits inside the cost baseline; management reserve is for unknown-unknowns.

prompts: A project manager inherits a troubled project: morale is low, the schedule and budget are unreliable, and stakeholders distrust the status reports. What should the project manager do first to begin recovery?

A new program lead takes over a failing product launch where the team is demoralized, the reported metrics keep slipping, and executives no longer believe the dashboards. What is the best first move toward recovery?

An IT project manager is assigned to a stalled ERP rollout with burned-out staff, contradictory cost figures, and a steering committee that has lost faith in progress updates. How should the manager begin turning it around?

correct.text: Stabilize the situation and run an honest, independent assessment of the true scope, schedule, cost, and risks before committing to any recovery plan
correct.why: Recovery starts with stabilizing and establishing the real baseline so decisions rest on facts, not optimistic reports.

correct.text: Pause new commitments, establish the real baseline through a fact-based review, and only then propose a credible recovery approach
correct.why: A credible reset requires the true picture first; committing before you know reality repeats the original mistake.

correct.text: Conduct a candid reassessment of actual status across scope, cost, schedule, and risk so any reset is built on facts rather than optimistic reporting
correct.why: The genuine current state must be known before a recovery plan can be trusted.

distractors.text: Immediately recommit to the original deadline and authorize sustained overtime and weekend work in an effort to claw back the schedule that has already slipped
distractors.trap: Committing to the old deadline before assessing reality repeats the optimism that caused the trouble and ignores root causes.

distractors.text: Recommend cancelling the project to the sponsor because the unreliable baselines make a successful recovery unlikely
distractors.trap: A kill decision may be valid, but it should follow an honest assessment at a defined gate, not precede any analysis.

distractors.text: Replace the entire project team to signal a fresh start and restore stakeholder confidence before reviewing the facts
distractors.trap: Wholesale replacement destroys knowledge and trust without addressing the actual scope, schedule, and risk problems.

distractors.text: Launch a highly visible communication campaign promising stakeholders that the numbers will soon improve, hoping renewed confidence restores momentum before you have verified any of the underlying project data
distractors.trap: Promising results you have not validated deepens distrust when the real picture emerges and does nothing to fix the fundamentals.

distractors.text: Renegotiate the contract and formally request additional budget and time from the sponsor right away, so the project has breathing room even though the true extent of the problems is still unknown
distractors.trap: Asking for more resources before you know what is actually wrong sizes the ask on guesses and can waste the sponsor's goodwill.

distractors.text: Bring in additional contractors and expand the team quickly to accelerate delivery, on the assumption that more capacity will overcome whatever caused the schedule and budget to slip
distractors.trap: Adding people to a troubled project without a diagnosis usually adds coordination cost and slows it further rather than recovering it.

distractors.text: Rebuild trust first by rewriting all of the status reports in a more positive and reassuring tone so stakeholders feel confident while the underlying work continues exactly as it has been
distractors.trap: Cosmetic reporting hides the problem instead of surfacing it and erodes credibility further once reality catches up.

explanation: Turning around a troubled project begins with stabilizing it and running an honest, independent assessment of true scope, schedule, cost, and risk, so any recovery plan is grounded in facts rather than the optimism that caused the trouble.

prompts: Match the level: a team needs a standard that gates entry into a Sprint, a standard that gates completion of every increment, and conditions specific to one requirement. Which option lists Definition of Ready, Definition of Done, and Acceptance Criteria in that order?

A development team wants three things clearly separated: a gate for starting a Sprint, a bar every increment must meet to be called finished, and the specific conditions that make one particular feature acceptable. Which option orders Definition of Ready, Definition of Done, and Acceptance Criteria correctly?

During a coaching session, a group asks you to label the entry gate for their iteration, the shared completion standard for all their work, and the checklist tied to a single backlog item. Which mapping to Definition of Ready, Definition of Done, and Acceptance Criteria is right?

correct.text: Definition of Ready gates entry into the Sprint, Definition of Done gates completion of every increment, and Acceptance Criteria are the conditions specific to one requirement
correct.why: Each operates at a distinct level: entry gate, universal completion bar, and per-item conditions.

correct.text: The Definition of Ready is the entry gate, the Definition of Done is the universal completion bar for all increments, and Acceptance Criteria apply to a single item
correct.why: The three constructs sit at entry, completion, and per-requirement levels respectively.

correct.text: Entry is gated by the Definition of Ready, completion of each increment is gated by the Definition of Done, and per-requirement conditions are captured in the Acceptance Criteria
correct.why: Ready governs starting, Done governs finishing all work, and Acceptance Criteria govern one requirement.

distractors.text: Acceptance Criteria gate entry into the Sprint, the Definition of Ready gates completion of every increment, and the Definition of Done specifies the conditions for one individual requirement
distractors.trap: Scrambled mapping: entry is the Definition of Ready and the completion bar is the Definition of Done, so none of these assignments is correct.

distractors.text: The Definition of Done gates entry into the Sprint, the Acceptance Criteria gate completion of all increments, and the Definition of Ready specifies the conditions for a single requirement
distractors.trap: Scrambled mapping: Done is a completion bar, not an entry gate, and Ready is the entry gate rather than a per-requirement condition.

distractors.text: The Definition of Ready gates entry into the Sprint, the Acceptance Criteria gate completion of every increment, and the Definition of Done specifies the conditions for one individual requirement
distractors.trap: The Acceptance Criteria and Definition of Done are swapped: Done is the universal completion bar and Acceptance Criteria are per-requirement conditions.

distractors.text: The Definition of Done gates entry into the Sprint, the Definition of Ready gates completion of every increment, and the Acceptance Criteria specify the conditions for one individual requirement
distractors.trap: Ready and Done are swapped: Ready is the entry gate and Done is the completion bar for every increment.

distractors.text: Acceptance Criteria gate entry into the Sprint, the Definition of Done gates completion of every increment, and the Definition of Ready specifies the conditions for a single requirement
distractors.trap: Entry is gated by the Definition of Ready and the per-requirement conditions are the Acceptance Criteria, so both of those are misplaced here.

distractors.text: The Definition of Ready gates entry into the Sprint, and a combined Definition of Done then serves both as the completion bar for every increment and as the specific conditions that make one individual requirement acceptable
distractors.trap: This folds Acceptance Criteria into the Definition of Done; the per-requirement conditions are a distinct construct from the universal completion bar.

distractors.text: Acceptance Criteria define the conditions specific to one requirement, while the Definition of Ready and the Definition of Done together form a single shared gate that governs both entry into the Sprint and completion of every increment
distractors.trap: Ready and Done are distinct gates operating at entry and at completion, not one shared gate.

explanation: The Definition of Ready gates entry into the Sprint, the Definition of Done is the universal bar for completing every increment, and Acceptance Criteria are the specific conditions for a single requirement — three constructs at three distinct levels.

prompts: A support team handles a continuous stream of incoming tickets of varying urgency, with no natural fixed-length planning cycle. Which approach fits best and why?
A network operations center receives an unending flow of alerts and service requests at irregular intervals, with no natural planning cadence. Which method suits this work and why?
A facilities help desk fields a steady but unpredictable stream of maintenance requests of mixed priority, never settling into fixed planning windows. Which approach fits best and why?

correct.text: Kanban, because it manages continuous flow by visualizing the work and limiting work in progress rather than committing to fixed-length iterations
correct.why: Kanban suits steady, unpredictable arrival of work without forcing it into timeboxes.

correct.text: Adopt Kanban so the steady stream of tickets is pulled across a visualized board under explicit work-in-progress limits instead of forced timeboxes
correct.why: Pull-based flow with WIP limits matches unplanned, variable work far better than fixed Sprints.

correct.text: Use Kanban, which fits the unpredictable arrival of work by controlling flow and WIP rather than planning fixed iterations in advance
correct.why: Continuous, uneven demand is best handled by flow management rather than iteration commitments.

distractors.text: Scrum, because its fixed-length Sprints give the team a steady cadence for planning the incoming work
distractors.trap: Fixed Sprint cadence suits planned iterations, not a continuous stream of unplanned tickets with no natural cycle.

distractors.text: A predictive plan, because it fixes scope and schedule up front for the team to track against
distractors.trap: Highly variable incoming work cannot be fully scoped in advance, so a predictive baseline fits poorly.

distractors.text: Scrum, because its backlog refinement and Sprint planning give the team a structured way to prioritize the incoming tickets and agree how much to commit to in each iteration
distractors.trap: Structured Sprint planning fits stable iterations, not a continuous stream where urgent tickets arrive between planning events.

distractors.text: A predictive waterfall approach that plans the ticket work in sequential phases against a scope baseline, because a defined baseline lets the team forecast delivery dates and report progress against the plan
distractors.trap: Sequencing unpredictable arrivals against a fixed baseline invites constant change and re-planning rather than firm dates.

distractors.text: Scrum with two-week Sprints, where incoming tickets are added to the product backlog and drawn into the next Sprint during planning so the team works from a prioritized, agreed batch
distractors.trap: Holding urgent tickets for the next Sprint boundary delays time-sensitive work that flow-based Kanban would pull straight away.

distractors.text: Scrumban combined with a predictive schedule, because pairing a flow board with up-front Sprint planning and a baseline gives the team both a visual queue and a milestone plan to track against
distractors.trap: Adding a predictive baseline to flow work introduces planning overhead that the unpredictable ticket stream keeps invalidating.

distractors.text: Extreme Programming, because its engineering practices like pair programming and continuous integration keep the team responsive as new tickets keep arriving
distractors.trap: XP addresses code quality and engineering discipline, not the flow and WIP management a continuous ticket stream needs.

explanation: A continuous, variable stream of work with no natural planning cadence fits Kanban, which visualizes the work and limits work in progress to manage flow rather than committing it to fixed-length iterations.

prompts: A team must choose between releasing the smallest usable version that real customers can use and get value from, or building a throwaway model only to test an idea. Which describes a minimum viable product versus a prototype?

A startup debates whether to ship the smallest working version that early adopters can genuinely use, or to build a quick disposable model just to test the concept internally. Which one is the minimum viable product and which is the prototype?

A product team must decide between releasing a lean first version that delivers real value to paying users versus assembling a rough throwaway build only to demonstrate an idea to investors. How do an MVP and a prototype differ here?

correct.text: An MVP is a usable first release that delivers real value to customers, while a prototype is built mainly to explore, test, or demonstrate an idea
correct.why: An MVP delivers real value; a prototype is a learning device, often discarded.

correct.text: The MVP is the smallest shippable release customers can actually use and gain value from, whereas a prototype is a learning model that is often discarded after testing
correct.why: One reaches customers with value; the other exists to validate a concept.

correct.text: An MVP reaches real users and delivers value with the fewest necessary features, but a prototype exists to validate or demonstrate a concept rather than to be used in production
correct.why: The MVP is minimal yet valuable and real; the prototype is exploratory.

distractors.text: An MVP is a disposable throwaway model built purely for internal experimentation, while a prototype is the first fully revenue-generating release that paying customers actually adopt and rely on
distractors.trap: Reversed: the MVP is the usable release and the prototype is the exploratory model.

distractors.text: Both an MVP and a prototype are purely internal experiments that get discarded after testing, and neither of them is intended to reach real paying customers as an actual released product
distractors.trap: An MVP is a real release that reaches customers, unlike a typical prototype.

distractors.text: An MVP includes the full set of planned features in its complete final form, while a prototype is the release that deliberately carries just the fewest features needed to deliver value
distractors.trap: The MVP deliberately contains the fewest features that still deliver value, not the full set.

distractors.text: An MVP is a fully polished, feature-complete product ready for a mass-market launch, while a prototype is simply an earlier internal build of that same finished product shown to executives
distractors.trap: An MVP is intentionally minimal rather than feature-complete, and a prototype is exploratory, not just an earlier build of the release.

distractors.text: A prototype is the smallest usable release that generates customer value, while an MVP is a detailed technical specification document produced before any code is written
distractors.trap: This reverses the terms and miscasts the MVP as a document; the MVP is a working release, not a spec.

distractors.text: Both terms mean exactly the same thing, a first commercial release, and teams simply use the two words interchangeably depending on whether the audience they are addressing is technical or business focused.
distractors.trap: They are distinct: an MVP delivers value to customers while a prototype is a learning artifact, so they are not interchangeable.

distractors.text: An MVP is a high-fidelity mockup used mainly for sales demonstrations, whereas a prototype is the long-term production system that real customers depend on for their daily work
distractors.trap: This swaps the concepts; the production release customers depend on is the MVP's role, not the prototype's.

explanation: A minimum viable product is the smallest usable release that delivers real value to customers, whereas a prototype is an exploratory model built to test or demonstrate an idea and is often discarded.

prompts: A team sizes backlog items with a unit-less relative scale rather than predicting exact hours. Which unit are they using, and how does it differ from estimating in hours?
An Agile team estimates backlog items using a relative, unit-less scale instead of forecasting exact clock time. What is this unit, and how does it contrast with estimating in hours?

During refinement, developers compare the relative size of stories using dimensionless numbers rather than predicting how many hours each will take. Which unit is this, and how is it different from hours?

correct.text: Story points, which are a relative and unit-less measure of size, whereas hours are a direct measure of elapsed time
correct.why: Story points compare relative size; hours measure time directly.

correct.text: They are using story points to compare the relative size of items, unlike hours, which quantify the actual time the work will take
correct.why: Points are comparative and dimensionless; hours are a concrete time measure.

correct.text: Story points express how large an item is relative to others without units, while hours estimate the concrete duration the work will require
correct.why: The distinction is relative sizing versus direct time measurement.

distractors.text: Hours, because story points are simply the team's estimated hours renamed, so a story worth five points always means five hours of hands-on effort for whichever developer picks it up
distractors.trap: Synonym trap: story points are a relative measure, not a renamed hour count.

distractors.text: Velocity, because each individual backlog item is sized directly in velocity units that the team then sums up to decide how much work will fit into the coming iteration
distractors.trap: Velocity is the team total per iteration, not the unit used to size a single item.

distractors.text: Throughput, because each story counts as exactly one unit of throughput, which is the relative dimensionless measure the team relies on to compare how large the backlog items are
distractors.trap: Throughput counts completed items over time; it is not a relative sizing unit for one story.

distractors.text: Ideal days, because story points are really just a rebranded count of the uninterrupted working days a single developer would need to finish the item on their own
distractors.trap: Ideal days are a time-based measure; story points are deliberately abstract and relative, not a disguised day count.

distractors.text: Function points, because the team is measuring the technical complexity of the software's functions rather than comparing the relative size of the backlog items they will build
distractors.trap: Function points measure delivered software size by a formal method, not the team's relative sizing of backlog items.

distractors.text: Cycle time, because each story is sized by how many days it historically spends moving from start to finish across the board
distractors.trap: Cycle time is a flow metric measured after the fact, not a relative estimate assigned before the work begins.

distractors.text: Capacity, because story points directly represent the number of available team-hours that have been allocated to each backlog item for the coming iteration
distractors.trap: Capacity is available time for a period; story points size the work itself and are not an allocation of hours.

explanation: Sizing backlog items on a dimensionless relative scale means the team is using story points, which compare relative size, in contrast to hours, which are a direct measure of elapsed time.

prompts: A team plans at two horizons: one looks across several iterations toward a deployable release, the other details only the next short cycle. Which describes release planning versus iteration planning?

A team maintains two planning layers: one spanning several iterations toward a shippable increment, the other detailing only the coming short cycle. Which describes release planning versus iteration planning?

An Agile release train plans at two levels, a longer view aimed at a deployable version across many iterations and a narrow view covering just the next timebox. How do release planning and iteration planning differ?

correct.text: Release planning looks across multiple iterations toward a deployable release, while iteration planning details the work for just the next short cycle
correct.why: Release planning is the multi-iteration horizon; iteration planning is the single-cycle horizon.

correct.text: Release planning sets the multi-iteration horizon toward a shippable release, whereas iteration planning plans only the upcoming single cycle in detail
correct.why: The two differ by planning horizon: many iterations versus one.

correct.text: The release plan spans several iterations aiming at a deployable increment, while the iteration plan scopes the specific work of the next short timebox
correct.why: Release planning is broad and forward-looking; iteration planning is narrow and immediate.

distractors.text: Iteration planning spans many iterations toward a deployable release, while release planning covers just the next short cycle and the committed work inside it
distractors.trap: Reversed: release planning spans many iterations and iteration planning covers the next cycle.

distractors.text: Both cover much the same horizon and content, so a team can treat them as one combined plan made in a single session
distractors.trap: They differ in horizon: multiple iterations versus a single upcoming cycle.

distractors.text: Release planning lists the individual daily tasks for tomorrow's stand-up, while iteration planning articulates the long-term product vision and overall business case
distractors.trap: Daily tasks and product vision belong to other levels, not these two planning horizons.

distractors.text: Release planning is a one-off event that fixes the longer roadmap up front, while iteration planning is an informal daily conversation about who works on which task today
distractors.trap: Release planning is revisited across iterations rather than a one-time lock, and the daily task conversation is the stand-up, not iteration planning.

distractors.text: Iteration planning defines the long-range product vision and business case, while release planning breaks the next two-week cycle into individual developer tasks and estimates
distractors.trap: This reverses the horizons; the next-cycle task breakdown is iteration planning, not release planning.

distractors.text: Release planning is run by the product owner and iteration planning by the scrum master, and that ownership split is what distinguishes the two levels
distractors.trap: The distinction is planning horizon, not which role owns each session.

distractors.text: Release planning schedules just the daily stand-up agenda, whereas iteration planning maps out the full portfolio of releases spread across all of the coming fiscal years
distractors.trap: This inflates iteration planning to portfolio scope and shrinks release planning to a stand-up, mislabeling both levels.

explanation: Release planning looks across multiple iterations toward a deployable release, while iteration planning details only the work of the next short cycle — the two are distinguished by their planning horizon.

prompts: A team uses a checklist of characteristics such as independent, negotiable, valuable, estimable, small, and testable to judge whether a user story is well formed. Which model are they applying, and what does it assess?
A team scores each user story against traits like independent, negotiable, valuable, estimable, small, and testable to decide if it is well written. Which model is this, and what does it evaluate?

Before accepting stories into refinement, a product owner checks whether each is independent, negotiable, valuable, estimable, small, and testable. Which acronym describes this check, and what does it assess?

correct.text: INVEST, which assesses whether the story itself is well shaped and ready for delivery
correct.why: INVEST evaluates the quality of the story; it is not the proof of completion.

correct.text: They are applying INVEST, a checklist that judges the quality and readiness of the story's structure rather than proving it is finished
correct.why: INVEST is about how well the story is written, not whether the work is done.

correct.text: INVEST, which evaluates how well a user story is formed by checking that it is independent, negotiable, valuable, estimable, small, and testable
correct.why: The acronym names the six qualities of a well-shaped story.

distractors.text: Acceptance criteria, which prove the story is complete
distractors.trap: Acceptance criteria define when a story is done; INVEST judges whether the story is well formed.

distractors.text: Definition of Done, which sets the universal completion bar
distractors.trap: The Definition of Done is the completion standard for all work, not a check on a single story shape.

distractors.text: MoSCoW, which ranks each story by necessity into must-have, should-have, could-have, and won't-have categories to decide how well the story has been written for the release
distractors.trap: MoSCoW prioritizes by necessity; it does not assess whether a story is well structured.

distractors.text: The Definition of Ready, which is the formal gate confirming a story has already met every acceptance criterion and can therefore be marked complete at the end of the Sprint
distractors.trap: Definition of Ready governs entry, not completion, and it does not assess the six structural qualities INVEST checks.

distractors.text: SMART goals, because the same specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, and time-bound criteria are exactly what determine whether an individual user story has been well structured
distractors.trap: SMART is an objective-setting model; INVEST, not SMART, is the checklist for judging story quality.

distractors.text: MoSCoW prioritization, which sorts the backlog into must-have, should-have, could-have, and won't-have buckets to determine the technical quality of each written story
distractors.trap: MoSCoW ranks importance and says nothing about how well a story is structured or shaped.

distractors.text: Planning poker, because the relative estimate the team assigns during sizing is itself the measure of whether a story has been written well enough to build
distractors.trap: Planning poker produces an estimate; it does not evaluate the six qualities that make a story well formed.

explanation: A checklist of independent, negotiable, valuable, estimable, small, and testable is INVEST, which assesses whether a user story is well shaped and ready for delivery rather than whether the work is complete.

prompts: The Cost Performance Index divides Earned Value by one denominator and the Schedule Performance Index divides it by another. Which denominators are correct?
In earned value analysis, the Cost Performance Index and the Schedule Performance Index each divide Earned Value by a different denominator. Which denominators are correct for each index?
A project analyst computes CPI and SPI, each as Earned Value over a distinct base. Which pairing of denominators is right?

correct.text: CPI divides Earned Value by Actual Cost, and SPI divides Earned Value by Planned Value
correct.why: CPI equals EV over AC for cost efficiency; SPI equals EV over PV for schedule efficiency.

correct.text: The Cost Performance Index is Earned Value over Actual Cost, while the Schedule Performance Index is Earned Value over Planned Value
correct.why: Cost efficiency uses AC as the denominator; schedule efficiency uses PV.

correct.text: CPI uses Actual Cost as its denominator for cost efficiency, and SPI uses Planned Value as its denominator for schedule efficiency
correct.why: Each index divides EV by the base that matches its dimension.

distractors.text: CPI divides Earned Value by Planned Value while SPI divides Earned Value by Actual Cost, matching each index to the opposite base from the correct one
distractors.trap: Swapped denominators - Actual Cost belongs to the cost index and Planned Value belongs to the schedule index.

distractors.text: CPI divides by Budget at Completion; SPI divides by Planned Value
distractors.trap: BAC is the total budget baseline, not the period denominator for CPI; CPI uses Actual Cost.

distractors.text: CPI divides by Actual Cost; SPI divides by Budget at Completion
distractors.trap: SPI measures progress against the time-phased Planned Value, not against the full Budget at Completion.

distractors.text: CPI divides Earned Value by the Estimate at Completion while SPI divides Earned Value by the Estimate to Complete, because both indices are meant to forecast the remaining work rather than measure current efficiency
distractors.trap: EAC and ETC are forecasts, not the denominators of the performance indices, which measure efficiency to date.

distractors.text: Both indices share the same denominator of Actual Cost, since the amount of money truly spent is the only reliable figure available for judging cost and schedule performance alike
distractors.trap: SPI must use Planned Value to reflect schedule; using Actual Cost for both erases the schedule dimension entirely.

distractors.text: CPI divides Actual Cost by Earned Value and SPI divides Planned Value by Earned Value, inverting both ratios so that any result above one automatically signals a problem on the project
distractors.trap: The ratios are inverted; correct indices place Earned Value in the numerator so that values below one signal trouble.

distractors.text: CPI divides by the Schedule Variance and SPI divides by the Cost Variance, cross-linking the two dimensions so that each index reflects the other's impact on overall performance
distractors.trap: Variances are differences, not the denominators of the indices, and cross-linking the dimensions is not how CPI and SPI are defined.

explanation: The Cost Performance Index is Earned Value divided by Actual Cost and the Schedule Performance Index is Earned Value divided by Planned Value, each dividing EV by the base that matches its dimension.

prompts: A project hit a one-time setup problem that inflated costs early, but the team is confident the rest of the work will run at the originally planned rate. Which EAC approach fits this situation?
Early in a project a single non-recurring calibration error spiked costs, but the team is confident the remaining work will proceed at the original planned rate. Which EAC method is appropriate?

A construction project absorbed a one-off permitting delay that raised early costs, yet the crew expects the rest of the work to track the baseline exactly. Which Estimate at Completion approach fits?

correct.text: Treat the overrun as atypical and forecast the remaining work at the budgeted rate, then add it to the costs already spent
correct.why: When a variance is a one-off that will not recur, the atypical EAC uses actual cost to date plus the remaining budgeted work (BAC minus EV).

correct.text: Because the variance is a one-off, use the atypical EAC of actual cost to date plus the remaining budgeted work, which is BAC minus EV
correct.why: The setup overrun is real but non-recurring, so only the money already spent carries the inefficiency forward.

correct.text: Forecast the rest of the project at the planned rate since the problem will not recur, adding that remaining budget to what has already been spent
correct.why: A non-representative variance calls for the atypical forecast rather than extrapolating the overrun.

distractors.text: Forecast the remaining work by dividing the budgeted cost of what is left by the cumulative Cost Performance Index, then add the actual cost already spent.
distractors.trap: Applies the ongoing-inefficiency formula to remaining work, which is wrong for a one-time non-recurring overrun.

distractors.text: Forecast using both the cost and schedule performance indices together in a combined projection.
distractors.trap: The combined CPI-and-SPI forecast fits ongoing schedule pressure, not an isolated one-time event.

distractors.text: Keep the Estimate at Completion equal to the original Budget at Completion for the project.
distractors.trap: The money already overspent on setup is real and must be carried forward; EAC cannot ignore the sunk overrun.

distractors.text: Project the setup inefficiency across the remainder of the project and compute the forecast as the Budget at Completion divided by the cumulative Cost Performance Index recorded to date.
distractors.trap: This is the typical EAC formula, which wrongly projects a one-time, non-recurring overrun across all remaining work.

distractors.text: Rebaseline the project's Budget at Completion to fold in the early setup overrun and then set the Estimate at Completion equal to that revised baseline figure.
