distractors.trap: Rebaselining to absorb the overrun sidesteps forecasting the true cost at completion of the work.

distractors.text: Recalculate the Estimate at Completion by applying just the cumulative schedule performance index to the project's total budget at completion baseline figure.
distractors.trap: Using schedule efficiency to forecast cost does not match a one-time, isolated cost overrun.

distractors.text: Treat the money already spent on the setup problem as a sunk cost and then report just the remaining budgeted work by itself as the Estimate at Completion.
distractors.trap: EAC must include actual cost to date; dropping the real spending understates the total and misstates the forecast.

explanation: Because the setup overrun is a one-time, non-recurring event, the atypical EAC applies: actual cost to date plus the remaining work at its budgeted rate (AC plus BAC minus EV).

prompts: The cost baseline and the total project budget are related but not the same. What does the project budget include that the cost baseline does not?
The cost baseline and the total authorized project budget are closely related yet distinct. Which component does the full project budget contain that the cost baseline does not?
A project manager reconciles the time-phased cost baseline against the total approved budget and finds a gap. What does the project budget include beyond the cost baseline?

correct.text: Management reserve, because the project budget equals the cost baseline plus management reserve
correct.why: The cost baseline holds the time-phased work budget plus contingency reserve; adding management reserve gives the full project budget.

correct.text: The project budget adds management reserve, which sits above the cost baseline and is not part of it
correct.why: Management reserve is held outside the baseline for unknown-unknowns and is only added to reach the total budget.

correct.text: The cost baseline holds the time-phased work budget plus contingency, and adding management reserve on top produces the full project budget
correct.why: Contingency is inside the baseline; management reserve is the piece the baseline excludes.

distractors.text: Contingency reserve - the baseline excludes it but the budget adds it back
distractors.trap: Contingency reserve is already inside the cost baseline; it is management reserve that the baseline excludes.

distractors.text: The actual costs incurred on the project to date combined with the earned value of all the work that has been completed so far against the plan
distractors.trap: Those are performance measurements, not budget components; the budget is built from estimates and reserves, not actuals.

distractors.text: Nothing - the cost baseline and the project budget are identical figures
distractors.trap: They differ by management reserve; the baseline is deliberately set below the total authorized budget.

distractors.text: The contingency reserve for known risks, which lives outside the cost baseline and is added when the total authorized project budget is finally assembled at approval
distractors.trap: Contingency for known risks is already part of the cost baseline; only management reserve sits above it.

distractors.text: The profit margin and corporate overhead allocation, because the project budget must build in the organization's expected return above the raw cost baseline of the planned work
distractors.trap: Profit and overhead are commercial or accounting concerns, not the management reserve that separates baseline from budget.

distractors.text: The detailed activity cost estimates for each work package, given that the cost baseline records summary control-account totals while the full budget itemizes each individual activity
distractors.trap: Activity estimates are aggregated into the cost baseline itself; they are not an extra component the budget adds on top.

distractors.text: The sunk costs from prior project phases plus a general inflation adjustment, which the cost baseline deliberately leaves out but the total authorized project budget restores in full
distractors.trap: Prior sunk costs and inflation are not the defined difference; management reserve is what the baseline excludes and the budget adds.

explanation: The project budget equals the cost baseline plus management reserve; the baseline already contains the time-phased work budget and contingency reserve, so management reserve is the component it excludes.

prompts: A team keeps two ordered lists: one holds every desired item for the product over time, the other holds only the items committed for the current cycle. What distinguishes the Sprint Backlog from the Product Backlog?

During onboarding, a new developer asks why the board tracked in the current iteration shows far fewer items than the long master list the product owner maintains. How would you explain the relationship between the Sprint Backlog and the Product Backlog?

A stakeholder is confused because the list of work planned for this two-week iteration looks like only a small fraction of the full wish list for the product. Which statement correctly separates the Sprint Backlog from the Product Backlog?

An auditor reviewing an agile team's artifacts wants to know which list represents a near-term commitment and which represents the evolving whole of everything the product might ever include. How do the Sprint Backlog and Product Backlog differ?

correct.text: The Sprint Backlog is the subset of work the team has committed to for a single Sprint, while the Product Backlog is the broader ordered list of everything desired for the product
correct.why: The Sprint Backlog is a near-term commitment drawn from the larger, evolving Product Backlog.

correct.text: The Product Backlog holds all wished-for product work over time, and the Sprint Backlog is the slice pulled from it for the current iteration
correct.why: The Sprint Backlog is a Sprint-sized selection of the wider Product Backlog.

correct.text: The Sprint Backlog reflects one cycle's commitment, whereas the Product Backlog is the continuously refined master list of all outstanding product work
correct.why: One is the current commitment; the other is the ongoing full backlog it is drawn from.

distractors.text: The Sprint Backlog is owned and prioritized by the Product Owner, while the Product Backlog is instead maintained and ordered by the Developers throughout the course of the project
distractors.trap: Reversed ownership: the Product Owner orders the Product Backlog and the Developers own the Sprint Backlog.

distractors.text: The Product Backlog is baselined at project start, while the Sprint Backlog is the list that is continuously re-prioritized over time
distractors.trap: Backwards: the Product Backlog is the one continuously refined, not the Sprint Backlog, and it is not frozen at the start.

distractors.text: The Sprint Backlog is organized around release milestones, while the Product Backlog is organized around individual tasks
distractors.trap: Neither is defined by releases versus tasks; the distinction is the commitment horizon, not item type.

distractors.text: The Sprint Backlog holds the same set of items as the Product Backlog, just presented on the team's physical board where stakeholders in the room can follow the daily progress as it unfolds
distractors.trap: The Sprint Backlog is a committed subset selected for one Sprint, not a display copy of the whole Product Backlog.

distractors.text: The Product Backlog holds the deeper technical and architectural enabler work, while the Sprint Backlog holds the customer-facing stories that were selected to be demonstrated to stakeholders at the upcoming review
distractors.trap: Backlogs are not split by technical versus customer-facing content; the difference is the commitment horizon.

distractors.text: The Sprint Backlog is maintained by the Scrum Master as a coordination aid for the team, while the Product Backlog is the artifact that external stakeholders review to follow the project's progress
distractors.trap: The Sprint Backlog belongs to the Developers and is transparent; it is not a Scrum Master coordination tool.

distractors.text: The Product Backlog captures the longer-term roadmap items still awaiting estimation, while the Sprint Backlog captures the items the team has already estimated and sized for delivery work
distractors.trap: Estimation status defines neither; the Product Backlog holds all desired work and the Sprint Backlog is one Sprint's subset.

explanation: The Sprint Backlog is a Sprint-sized commitment selected from the Product Backlog, which is the broader, continuously refined ordered list of all desired product work.

prompts: At the end of a Sprint a team holds two meetings: one demonstrates the working increment to stakeholders for feedback, the other reflects on how the team worked together. Which describes the iteration review versus the retrospective?

A newly formed agile team wants to know why they hold one closing session with customers present to show the product and a separate session with only the team to talk about collaboration. How do the review and the retrospective differ?

A manager sitting in on Sprint-end ceremonies notices one meeting focuses on the increment and stakeholder feedback while the next focuses on team dynamics and improvement actions. Which correctly distinguishes the review from the retrospective?

Two end-of-iteration events are on the calendar: one gathers product feedback from those outside the team, the other surfaces process improvements from within the team. What separates the iteration review from the retrospective?

correct.text: The review demonstrates completed work and gathers product feedback, while the retrospective reflects on the team process to identify improvements
correct.why: Review inspects the product; retrospective inspects the way of working.

correct.text: The review inspects the increment with stakeholders to adapt the product, whereas the retrospective inspects how the team worked to adapt its process
correct.why: One adapts the product, the other adapts the team's practices.

correct.text: In the review the team shows working output and collects feedback from stakeholders, while in the retrospective the team privately examines its collaboration and agrees on changes
correct.why: Product feedback happens in the review; process change happens in the retrospective.

distractors.text: The review reflects on the team's process, while the retrospective demonstrates the product to stakeholders
distractors.trap: Reversed: the review is about the product and the retrospective about the process.

distractors.text: The retrospective sets the scope for the next iteration, while the review is where impediments get removed
distractors.trap: Scope selection happens in planning and impediment removal is ongoing, not these events.

distractors.text: Both the review and the retrospective are meetings whose main purpose is to demonstrate the completed increment to external stakeholders and collect their product feedback for the upcoming iteration
distractors.trap: Only the review demonstrates the increment; the retrospective is an internal process reflection.

distractors.text: The review is a formal sign-off gate at which stakeholders inspect and approve each backlog item before it can be considered done, and the retrospective is where the team decides which approved items to keep in the release
distractors.trap: Done is governed by the Definition of Done, and the retrospective inspects process, not item status.

distractors.text: The retrospective is the session where the team estimates and commits to the upcoming backlog items, and the review is where the product owner reprioritizes and re-sequences the product backlog for the remaining cycles
distractors.trap: Estimating, committing, and reprioritizing belong to refinement and planning, not these two events.

distractors.text: The review is an internal quality audit the team runs on itself against its own coding standards, while the retrospective is the public demonstration where external customers and sponsors give feedback on the new features
distractors.trap: Inverts both: the review is the public product inspection and the retrospective the internal reflection.

distractors.text: The review and the retrospective are two names for the same closing ceremony, with the review label used when stakeholders attend and the retrospective label used when the team meets on its own
distractors.trap: They are two distinct events with different purposes, not one ceremony renamed by attendance.

explanation: The Sprint Review inspects and adapts the product with stakeholders, while the retrospective inspects and adapts the team's process and way of working.

prompts: A Kanban team finds that items sit half-finished for a long time because everyone starts new work before finishing current work. Which intervention most directly shortens cycle time?

A support team using a pull board notices tickets linger in progress for days because members open new tickets before closing the ones they hold. Which change most directly reduces how long each ticket takes to finish?

On a marketing Kanban board, cards pile up in the 'doing' column because contributors keep pulling fresh cards while older ones stall. Which action would most directly cut the average time a card spends in flight?

A maintenance team's flow metrics show rising cycle time driven by too many simultaneously open work items. Which intervention most directly shortens the time it takes items to move from start to done?

correct.text: Lower the work in progress limits so fewer items are open at once and each is finished before a new one is pulled
correct.why: By Little's Law, reducing work in progress at steady throughput lowers average cycle time and forces items to finish before new ones start.

correct.text: Tighten the WIP limits so the team must complete what it has started before beginning anything new
correct.why: Capping concurrent work reduces the in-progress inventory that stretches cycle time.

correct.text: Reduce how many items may sit in progress simultaneously so effort concentrates on finishing rather than starting
correct.why: Fewer open items at constant throughput means shorter average cycle time.

distractors.text: Raise the work in progress limits so the team can pull more items into progress and keep everyone fully occupied in parallel
distractors.trap: More open work increases cycle time; it does not shorten it.

distractors.text: Add more frequent status meetings to review and report in detail on the progress of the open work items each day.
distractors.trap: Reporting on stalled work does not address the cause, which is too much work in progress.

distractors.text: Switch to fixed-length Sprints and commit the team to a larger number of story points each iteration
distractors.trap: Committing to more work does not reduce the in-progress backlog that is stretching cycle time.

distractors.text: Give each team member a dedicated swimlane on the board to manage and prioritize their own personal queue of items for the coming week
distractors.trap: Personal swimlanes formalize and encourage more parallel work rather than limiting it, which keeps cycle time high instead of lowering it the way capping concurrent work would.

distractors.text: Break each large item into many smaller sub-tasks and open them together at the start of the item so progress shows on each piece at once
distractors.trap: Opening all of the sub-tasks simultaneously multiplies the work in progress, which worsens the exact overload that is already inflating the team's cycle time.

distractors.text: Hire additional contractors to take on the extra half-finished items and advance more of the open work in parallel
distractors.trap: Adding people to run more concurrent work does not reduce WIP; the bottleneck is too many items open at once, not headcount.

distractors.text: Introduce a daily rule that each team member makes at least some visible progress on all of their open items before the end of the day
distractors.trap: Spreading effort thinly across open items keeps everything in progress at once, which sustains high WIP and does nothing to shorten cycle time; the fix is finishing items, not touching them all.

explanation: Lowering WIP limits reduces the number of concurrently open items, which by Little's Law shortens average cycle time and forces work to finish before new work starts.

prompts: A product owner wants to organize work from largest to smallest: a broad body of related work, a specific deliverable capability within it, and a small user-centered slice of need. Which ordering of epic, feature, and user story is correct from largest to smallest?

While structuring a new backlog, a team must nest a big theme of related work, the concrete capabilities inside it, and the tiny user-focused increments beneath those. From largest to smallest, how should epic, feature, and user story be ordered?

A coach asks a group to arrange three backlog levels correctly: the widest container of related work, the deliverable capability within it, and the smallest valuable slice for a user. What is the correct largest-to-smallest order of epic, feature, and user story?

Setting up a requirements hierarchy, a program manager needs the broadest grouping at the top, the concrete capability in the middle, and the smallest user-centered piece at the bottom. Which largest-to-smallest ordering of epic, feature, and user story is correct?

correct.text: Epic first, then feature, then user story, moving from the broadest body of work down to the smallest slice
correct.why: An epic decomposes into features, which decompose into user stories.

correct.text: The epic is largest, the feature sits beneath it as a deliverable capability, and the user story is the smallest user-centered increment
correct.why: The hierarchy runs epic to feature to story from big to small.

correct.text: Start with the epic, decompose it into features, and decompose each feature into user stories
correct.why: Decomposition proceeds epic to feature to story.

distractors.text: Order them user story first, then feature, and finally epic, on the mistaken view that the smallest user-facing slice belongs at the very top
distractors.trap: This reverses the hierarchy; the epic is the largest, not the smallest.

distractors.text: Feature, then epic, then user story
distractors.trap: An epic is larger than a feature, so a feature cannot sit above an epic.

distractors.text: Epic, then user story, then feature
distractors.trap: Features sit between epics and stories, so a story cannot sit above a feature.

distractors.text: Theme at the top as the broadest container, then the epic beneath it, then the feature, treating the user story as merely the acceptance criteria attached under each feature
distractors.trap: A user story is a backlog item in its own right, not the acceptance criteria of a feature, so this misplaces the story entirely.

distractors.text: Feature sits at the top because it represents the marketable, sellable capability that customers pay for, then the epic sits beneath it as a large story contained within that feature, and finally the user story is the smallest individual task at the bottom
distractors.trap: An epic is broader than a feature and is not a large story nested inside one, so ranking the feature above the epic inverts the correct decomposition order.

distractors.text: Epic and feature are actually the very same level of work, just labeled differently by different agile frameworks and tools, so the only real ordering that matters is placing that single grouping level above the individual user story beneath it
distractors.trap: Epic and feature are distinct levels of decomposition rather than interchangeable synonyms, so collapsing them into one level loses the correct three-tier largest-to-smallest ordering.

distractors.text: User story at the top because it represents the overall vision the customer cares about most, then the feature that supports that vision, and finally the epic as the smallest concrete unit of code the developers actually implement
distractors.trap: This inverts the hierarchy entirely; the user story is the smallest user-centered slice and the epic is the largest body of work, not the reverse.

explanation: From largest to smallest the order is epic, then feature, then user story: an epic decomposes into features, and features decompose into user stories.

prompts: A team faces a risky technical unknown and schedules a short timeboxed effort to research and prototype before committing to build. What is this effort called, and how does it differ from a user story?

Before committing to build a feature that depends on an unfamiliar third-party API, a team sets aside a brief timebox purely to experiment and learn. What is this called, and how does it differ from a user story?

Unsure whether a new database technology can meet performance needs, a team allocates a fixed, short investigation to prototype and gather evidence before planning the real work. What is this effort, and how does it contrast with a user story?

A team cannot estimate a feature because a key design question is unresolved, so they schedule a short timeboxed exploration to reduce that uncertainty first. What is this effort called, and how does it differ from a user story?

correct.text: A spike, which produces information to reduce uncertainty, whereas a user story delivers customer-facing value
correct.why: A spike is a learning timebox; a user story delivers usable value.

correct.text: A spike, a timeboxed investigation whose output is knowledge, in contrast to a user story whose output is working, valuable functionality
correct.why: The spike buys information; the story delivers value.

correct.text: It is a spike aimed at answering a technical or product question, unlike a user story that is meant to ship a slice of usable capability
correct.why: A spike resolves uncertainty rather than delivering shippable value.

distractors.text: A user story, because any research or investigation the team performs is ultimately just another form of user story that delivers value like every other backlog item.
distractors.trap: Research to reduce uncertainty is a spike, not a value-delivering user story.

distractors.text: A hardening sprint, because it stabilizes the release
distractors.trap: A hardening sprint stabilizes and integrates near release; it is not a short research timebox to reduce an unknown.

distractors.text: A retrospective, because the team learns from it
distractors.trap: A retrospective reflects on process; a spike investigates a technical or product unknown.

distractors.text: A proof-of-concept release, because the prototype the team builds during the investigation timebox is then packaged and shipped out to a small set of real customers in production in order to gather genuine usage data before the team makes a full commitment
distractors.trap: A spike's throwaway prototype exists purely for learning and is not shipped to customers; releasing something to gather real usage data would be a different, value-delivering activity rather than a spike.

distractors.text: A refinement session, because the whole purpose of the timebox is to sit together and break the uncertain work down into smaller, well-understood backlog items that the team can then estimate and plan with far more confidence
distractors.trap: Refinement clarifies and splits existing backlog items but does not perform the hands-on research, experimentation, and prototyping that a spike uses to resolve a genuine technical or product unknown.

distractors.text: A change request, because investigating a new technical direction formally alters the planned scope and must be logged and approved before the team can proceed
distractors.trap: Exploring an unknown within a timebox is a spike, not a scope change requiring formal change control before any learning can occur.

distractors.text: A prototype user story with acceptance criteria, because as long as the team writes the exploration up in the familiar story format with a clear definition of done, it counts as a normal value-delivering backlog item like any other
distractors.trap: Wrapping a research effort in story formatting does not make it a value-delivering user story; a spike's purpose is to produce knowledge to reduce uncertainty, not shippable customer value.

explanation: A spike is a short, timeboxed investigation that produces information to reduce uncertainty, whereas a user story delivers a slice of customer-facing, usable value.

prompts: Requirements for a new product are highly uncertain and expected to change often as customers react to early versions. Which development approach fits, and why?
A startup is building a product in an unproven market where user needs are fuzzy and likely to shift as each early release gets feedback. Which development approach fits, and why?

A team is asked to deliver something whose requirements no one can fully pin down up front and that will almost certainly evolve as stakeholders see working versions. Which approach suits this, and why?

For an innovative feature whose scope will keep changing as the market responds, leadership asks whether to lock requirements first or elaborate them over time. Which development approach fits, and why?

correct.text: An adaptive approach, because it expects requirements to evolve and plans progressively through iteration and feedback
correct.why: Adaptive approaches suit volatile requirements by elaborating scope over time.

correct.text: An adaptive, iterative approach, since it embraces changing requirements and refines scope release by release using feedback
correct.why: Adaptive delivery absorbs change by progressively elaborating scope.

correct.text: An adaptive approach that plans in short increments, because it is designed to incorporate learning as customers react to each version
correct.why: Iterative feedback loops handle volatility better than a fixed up-front plan.

distractors.text: A predictive approach, because all requirements must be fixed and baselined before any work begins
distractors.trap: Highly volatile requirements resist a fully defined up-front baseline, so predictive fits poorly.

distractors.text: A predictive approach, because change is impossible once a project starts
distractors.trap: Change is expected here; an adaptive approach is designed to absorb it.

distractors.text: An adaptive approach, but one that skips planning altogether and simply reacts to each new customer request as it arrives, since any detailed plan would soon be overtaken by change
distractors.trap: Adaptive delivery still plans, just progressively rather than up front; portraying it as planless reaction misstates how it absorbs evolving requirements.

distractors.text: A predictive approach with a comprehensive change control board, because logging and formally approving each of the many expected requirement changes through governance will keep the fixed baseline under control
distractors.trap: When change is constant and pervasive, routing every shift through heavy change control adds overhead rather than fitting the volatility that adaptive delivery handles naturally.

distractors.text: A fully predictive waterfall with an extended, detailed analysis phase up front, because investing more time in exhaustive requirements gathering now will eliminate the uncertainty before construction starts
distractors.trap: More up-front analysis cannot remove uncertainty that only resolves as customers react to real versions, so a locked waterfall plan still fits poorly here.

distractors.text: An adaptive approach only for the user interface while the rest of the product is fully baselined and frozen, because volatility is always confined to what the customer can see on screen
distractors.trap: Requirement volatility here is broad and not limited to the interface, so freezing everything else reintroduces the rigidity adaptive delivery is meant to avoid.

distractors.text: A predictive approach that simply builds a very large management reserve and contingency buffer into the fixed baseline up front, because setting aside enough money and time in advance will absorb whatever the changing requirements eventually turn out to be
distractors.trap: Reserves cushion cost and schedule impact but cannot make a fixed, up-front baseline fit requirements that fundamentally keep changing; the situation calls for adaptive progressive elaboration, not a padded predictive plan.

explanation: An adaptive approach fits volatile, uncertain requirements because it plans progressively and incorporates feedback each iteration rather than fixing all scope up front.

prompts: A team schedules a dedicated timebox late in a release to fix defects, integrate, and stabilize before shipping. Which practice is this, and how does it relate to the Definition of Done?

As a release date nears, a team sets aside a full iteration purely to close out bugs, run integration, and harden the build before go-live. What is this practice called, and how does it relate to the Definition of Done?

A program reserves a late timebox in which no new features are added and the team only stabilizes, integrates, and fixes defects prior to shipping. Which practice is this, and how does it connect to the Definition of Done?

Because quality work kept getting deferred, a team plans a special end-of-release iteration devoted to integration testing and defect resolution. What is this called, and what does it imply about the Definition of Done?

correct.text: A hardening sprint, which concentrates stabilization in a later timebox, whereas a strong Definition of Done aims to build release-quality into every increment
correct.why: Hardening sprints defer quality work; a robust Definition of Done bakes it in continuously.

correct.text: It is a hardening sprint that pushes stabilization to the end, in contrast to a rigorous Definition of Done that keeps each increment releasable as it is built
correct.why: The practice defers quality; a strong DoD prevents that deferral.

correct.text: A hardening sprint used to catch up on deferred quality work, which a comprehensive Definition of Done would largely make unnecessary by embedding quality in every Sprint
correct.why: A strong DoD reduces the need for a separate hardening phase.

distractors.text: A retrospective, used to stabilize and integrate the increment before it ships
distractors.trap: A retrospective inspects and improves process; it does not perform defect fixing and integration work.

distractors.text: A spike, understood as the dedicated end-of-release timebox the team uses to stabilize, integrate, and resolve the remaining defects in the build before it is finally shipped
distractors.trap: A spike is a short investigation to reduce uncertainty, not a stabilization timebox.

distractors.text: Backlog refinement, carried out to integrate and harden the increment for release
distractors.trap: Refinement prepares future backlog items; it does not stabilize a release.

distractors.text: A release planning event, whose main purpose is to lock in the final ship date with the sponsor and reassign the available testers, with the remaining integration and defect-resolution work then scheduled and distributed across the following quarter of delivery
distractors.trap: Release planning sets dates, scope, and assignments but does not perform the concentrated hands-on stabilization, integration, and defect-fixing work that defines a hardening sprint.

distractors.text: A regression test cycle run by a separate downstream QA group after the delivery team has moved on, which some teams treat as covering quality in place of a Definition of Done applied within each increment as it is built
distractors.trap: Handing quality to a downstream QA group does not replace the Definition of Done; the DoD is meant to keep quality built inside each increment rather than deferring it to a separate team later.

distractors.text: A phase-gate review in which the increment is stabilized and hardened after a formal governance board signs off on it, meaning the Definition of Done is understood to apply chiefly at that one final gate rather than to the individual Sprints along the way
distractors.trap: The Definition of Done applies to every increment produced in every Sprint, not just a single final governance gate, so treating it as a one-time end-of-release checkpoint misrepresents its purpose.

distractors.text: A continuous integration pipeline, in which automating the build and test steps late in the release performs the stabilization work, with a strong Definition of Done seen as less necessary once the tooling is in place
distractors.trap: Automation supports quality but does not replace the Definition of Done; a hardening sprint is a deferred stabilization timebox, and a robust DoD is what keeps each increment releasable rather than something the pipeline makes obsolete.

explanation: A hardening sprint concentrates stabilization, integration, and defect fixing into a late timebox, whereas a strong Definition of Done builds release-quality into every increment and reduces the need for it.

prompts: Planned Value, Earned Value, and Actual Cost are the three EVM baselines of measurement. Which statement matches each to its meaning?
A project manager preparing an earned value report must correctly label the three core measures. Which statement correctly matches Planned Value, Earned Value, and Actual Cost to their meanings?

During a cost performance review, a stakeholder asks the team to define the three EVM quantities in plain terms. Which statement correctly maps Planned Value, Earned Value, and Actual Cost to what each represents?
An exam candidate must pair each earned value term with its definition. Which statement correctly describes Planned Value, Earned Value, and Actual Cost?

correct.text: Planned Value is the budget for scheduled work, Earned Value is the budget for completed work, and Actual Cost is what was actually spent
correct.why: PV is what you planned to have done, EV is the budgeted worth of what you did do, and AC is the real spend on it.

correct.text: Planned Value is the authorized budget for work scheduled, Earned Value is the budgeted value of work actually done, and Actual Cost is the real money spent
correct.why: PV plans, EV credits completed work at budget, AC records real spend.

correct.text: PV represents the budgeted cost of scheduled work, EV represents the budgeted cost of the work performed, and AC represents the true expenditure incurred
correct.why: Each term ties budget-to-schedule, budget-to-work-done, and actual spend respectively.

distractors.text: Planned Value is what was spent, Earned Value is the schedule, Actual Cost is the budget for completed work
distractors.trap: This scrambles all three; Actual Cost is real spend, not the budgeted value of completed work.

distractors.text: Earned Value is the authorized budget for scheduled work and Planned Value is the value of completed work
distractors.trap: Reversed - PV is scheduled work budget; EV is the budgeted value of completed work.

distractors.text: Actual Cost represents the budgeted value of the completed work, while Earned Value represents the actual cash that has been spent to perform that work to date
distractors.trap: Swapped - Earned Value is budgeted value of work done; Actual Cost is the cash spent.

distractors.text: Planned Value is the total fixed contract price that was agreed with the customer at signing, Earned Value is the cumulative profit margin the project has realized to date, and Actual Cost is the remaining portion of the budget that is still available to be spent
distractors.trap: None of these definitions map to EVM; PV is budgeted scheduled work, EV is the budgeted value of work performed, and AC is money actually spent, not contract price, profit margin, or remaining budget.

distractors.text: Earned Value is the estimated cost of all work remaining, Planned Value is the reserve set aside for risks, and Actual Cost is the forecast final cost of the project at completion
distractors.trap: These describe estimate-to-complete, reserves, and estimate-at-completion, not the three core EVM measures of scheduled budget, completed-work budget, and actual spend.

distractors.text: Actual Cost is the budget baseline approved at the start, Planned Value is the variance between plan and reality, and Earned Value is the percentage of the schedule elapsed so far
distractors.trap: AC is real expenditure not the baseline, PV is scheduled-work budget not a variance, and EV is budgeted value of work done not elapsed schedule percentage.

distractors.text: Planned Value is the number of hours the team logged against the plan, Earned Value is the customer's perceived quality of the finished features, and Actual Cost is the labor rate multiplied by the total headcount assigned to the effort
distractors.trap: EVM measures are expressed in budgeted and actual cost of work, not logged hours, perceived quality, or a headcount rate; PV is budgeted scheduled work, EV is budgeted completed work, and AC is money actually spent.

explanation: Planned Value is the budgeted cost of scheduled work, Earned Value is the budgeted cost of completed work, and Actual Cost is the money actually spent.

prompts: The To-Complete Performance Index for a struggling project comes out above 1.0. What is this telling the project manager?
A cost report shows a project's To-Complete Performance Index has risen above 1.0 as it fell behind on budget. What does this value tell the project manager about the remaining work?
After a budget overrun, the analyst calculates a To-Complete Performance Index greater than 1.0 against the original budget target. How should the project manager interpret this?
A dashboard flags that the To-Complete Performance Index needed to hit the budget-at-completion is now above 1.0. What does that indicate to the project manager?

correct.text: The remaining work must be performed more efficiently than the work done so far in order to still hit the target budget
correct.why: TCPI above 1 means the future cost efficiency required exceeds 1.0, so the team must outperform its past pace to recover.

correct.text: To meet the budget, the team must achieve better cost efficiency on the work left than it has managed up to now
correct.why: A TCPI over 1 demands above-baseline efficiency for the rest of the project.

correct.text: Hitting the budget now requires the rest of the project to run leaner than its performance to date, since the required efficiency exceeds one
correct.why: The index quantifies a required efficiency greater than the team's historical rate.

distractors.text: The project has already finished under budget with room to spare
distractors.trap: A TCPI above 1 signals a harder road ahead, not a comfortable finish; it is a required-efficiency target, not a result.

distractors.text: The remaining work can comfortably be performed less efficiently than the work already completed and the team will still manage to come in on the target budget.
distractors.trap: Less efficiency is allowed only when TCPI is below 1; above 1 demands more efficiency.

distractors.text: The cost baseline has been exceeded and the project must be cancelled
distractors.trap: TCPI quantifies the efficiency needed to recover; it does not by itself mandate cancellation.

distractors.text: The schedule is behind and the team should crash the remaining activities by adding resources to the critical path so the milestone dates can still be recovered on time
distractors.trap: TCPI is a cost-efficiency index, not a schedule metric, so it says nothing about crashing the critical path or recovering milestone dates.

distractors.text: The cost performance to date has been running exactly on plan against the baseline, so no corrective action of any kind is required and the team can simply continue working at precisely the same level of efficiency it has already demonstrated so far
distractors.trap: A TCPI value above 1 specifically means the team's past efficiency is not enough going forward; continuing at the same rate would overrun the budget, so corrective action is in fact needed.

distractors.text: The estimate at completion is now guaranteed to equal the original budget at completion, confirming the funding set aside will be spent exactly as authorized with no variance
distractors.trap: TCPI states the efficiency required to meet the target, not a guarantee that the forecast will land on budget; a value above 1 warns the target is at risk.

distractors.text: The team should immediately rebaseline the project to a higher budget, because a To-Complete Performance Index above 1.0 is the formal signal that the original target is no longer achievable and must be officially replaced with a more realistic one
distractors.trap: A TCPI above 1 quantifies the tougher efficiency now required, not an automatic mandate to rebaseline; rebaselining is a governance decision, not something the index alone dictates.

explanation: A TCPI above 1.0 means the remaining work must be done more cost-efficiently than the work completed so far in order to still meet the target budget.

prompts: Among rough order of magnitude, budgetary, and definitive estimates, which is the most accurate and when is it produced?
A cost engineer must tell leadership which of the rough order of magnitude, budgetary, and definitive estimates carries the tightest range and at what point it is prepared. Which is it?

As a project's scope matures, a manager compares rough order of magnitude, budgetary, and definitive estimates to decide which to trust most for final commitments. Which is the most accurate and when is it produced?

Asked to identify the narrowest-range estimate and the stage at which it appears, a planner weighs rough order of magnitude, budgetary, and definitive estimates. Which is the most accurate and when is it produced?

correct.text: The definitive estimate is the most accurate, prepared late once scope is detailed and well understood
correct.why: Accuracy improves as scope matures; the definitive estimate uses the most complete information and has the tightest range.

correct.text: The definitive estimate has the tightest range and is produced later in the project from mature, detailed scope
correct.why: Better information late in planning narrows the estimate's range.

correct.text: Accuracy is highest for the definitive estimate, which is built near execution when the scope is fully elaborated
correct.why: The most complete scope information yields the narrowest, most accurate estimate.

distractors.text: The rough order of magnitude estimate is the most accurate because it is done first
distractors.trap: ROM is the least accurate with the widest range; being early means less information, not more precision.

distractors.text: The budgetary estimate is the most accurate because it sets the approved budget
distractors.trap: A budgetary estimate is broader than a definitive one; setting a budget does not make it the tightest range.

distractors.text: All three carry essentially the same accuracy because each is built from the same underlying scope information and simply presented to stakeholders in a different format
distractors.trap: The three deliberately differ in range because scope information matures over time; a different presentation format does not make their accuracy equal.

distractors.text: The rough order of magnitude estimate is the most accurate because it is deliberately padded with the largest contingency reserve, which guarantees the actual cost will always land inside its range
distractors.trap: A wide range from heavy padding is not accuracy; ROM remains the least precise estimate regardless of how much reserve is added.

distractors.text: The budgetary estimate is the most accurate because it is produced by the most senior cost experts using the organization's best historical analogous data from comparable past projects
distractors.trap: Analogous data and senior estimators still cannot match the tight range a definitive estimate gets from detailed, current scope; the budgetary estimate stays broader.

distractors.text: The definitive estimate is actually the least accurate because it is rushed at the end of the project when there is no longer time to gather good information about the work
distractors.trap: The definitive estimate is prepared from the most complete scope information and is the most accurate, not a rushed, low-information figure.

distractors.text: The three estimates are equally accurate but simply serve different audiences, with the rough order of magnitude prepared for executives, the budgetary prepared for finance, and the definitive prepared for the delivery team on the ground
distractors.trap: The three differ in accuracy and range because of how mature the scope information is, not merely in their intended audience; the definitive estimate is genuinely the tightest and most accurate.

explanation: The definitive estimate is the most accurate with the tightest range because it is prepared later from detailed, well-understood scope.

prompts: In earned value terms, which option correctly pairs discrete effort, apportioned effort, and level of effort with how its progress is measured?

A manufacturing PM is setting up an EVM baseline and must classify three kinds of work: assembling a testable unit, inspection that scales with that assembly, and running the project office. Which statement correctly matches discrete, apportioned, and level of effort to how each earns value?
During a construction project's cost planning, the team debates how discrete effort, apportioned effort, and level of effort each register progress. Which option describes all three correctly?

correct.text: Discrete effort has its own measurable output, apportioned effort moves in proportion to related discrete work, and level of effort is time-based support work with no distinct deliverable
correct.why: These are the three EVM work types: discrete is directly measurable, apportioned tracks a parent activity, and level of effort earns value with the passage of time.

correct.text: Discrete effort is measured by its own completed deliverable, apportioned effort earns value in step with the discrete activity it supports, and level of effort accrues value simply as time passes
correct.why: Same three definitions restated: measurable output, proportional tracking of a parent, and calendar-based earning.

correct.text: A discrete task is tracked by its direct output, an apportioned task rises and falls with the parent discrete work it is tied to, and a level-of-effort task is ongoing support measured against the calendar
correct.why: Discrete is standalone and measurable, apportioned is proportional to a parent, and level of effort is purely time-driven.

distractors.text: Discrete effort is proportional to other related work, apportioned effort is measured purely by the passage of time on the calendar, and level of effort is the category that has its own distinct and independently measurable deliverable
distractors.trap: Scrambled - it is apportioned effort that is proportional and level of effort that is time-based with no distinct output.

distractors.text: All three categories earn value purely through the passage of time, each treated as general support for the overall project rather than as work that produces any distinct, measurable output of its own on the schedule
distractors.trap: Only level of effort is purely time-based; discrete and apportioned effort are tied to actual work output.

distractors.text: Level of effort provides the most precise earned value of the three, with its steady calendar basis producing the cleanest and most reliable progress signal available across all of the work types
distractors.trap: Time-based earning does not make level of effort precise; discrete effort with measurable output gives the truest progress signal.

distractors.text: Apportioned effort has its own independently measurable deliverable, discrete effort is really just the time-based support work of the project, and level of effort moves strictly in proportion to a parent activity it is linked to
distractors.trap: Every role is misassigned; discrete is the measurable one, apportioned is proportional, and level of effort is the time-based support.

distractors.text: Discrete effort and apportioned effort are both purely time-based support work that earn value with the calendar, while level of effort is the single category of the three that produces a directly measurable, standalone output
distractors.trap: This flips the picture entirely; discrete produces the measurable output and level of effort is the time-based support.

distractors.text: Level of effort should be reclassified into discrete work packages, giving each project activity its own distinct, independently verifiable, and separately schedulable deliverable to earn value
distractors.trap: Level of effort legitimately earns value over time; forcing it into discrete packages misrepresents genuine support work.

explanation: Discrete effort has a distinct measurable output, apportioned effort earns value in proportion to a related discrete activity, and level of effort earns value with the passage of time and has no standalone deliverable.

prompts: Control account, work package, and planning package describe levels of the WBS used in earned value management. Which option orders them correctly from highest to lowest?

A PMO analyst is documenting how a control account, a planning package, and a work package nest within the WBS for cost integration. Which option lists them from the highest management point down to the most detailed level?
While rolling up cost data, a PM must explain the hierarchy of the control account, the planning package, and the work package to a new team member. Which ordering from highest to lowest is correct?

correct.text: The control account is the highest integration point, a planning package sits below it for work not yet detailed, and a work package is the lowest level with detailed activities
correct.why: A control account integrates several elements for measurement, a planning package is a placeholder beneath it, and the work package holds the detailed schedulable work.

correct.text: At the top is the control account where cost and schedule are integrated, beneath it a planning package holds work still to be elaborated, and at the bottom the work package carries the defined tasks
correct.why: Highest to lowest is control account, then planning package, then work package.

correct.text: The control account is the top management and measurement point, the planning package is a placeholder for future work beneath it, and the work package holds the detailed schedulable activities
correct.why: Control account integrates, planning package is the undetailed placeholder, work package is the lowest detailed level.

distractors.text: The work package is the highest point in the hierarchy, the planning package sits directly below it as the middle tier, and the control account is the lowest and most detailed level holding the activities
distractors.trap: Inverted - the control account is the higher management point, not the lowest detailed level.

distractors.text: The planning package is the highest integration point where cost and schedule come together, and the control account is simply the undetailed placeholder for future work that sits beneath it in the structure
distractors.trap: Swapped roles - the control account is the integration point and the planning package is the placeholder.

distractors.text: All three of these are simply the same level of the WBS, just named differently by different teams, tools, and organizations, so the ordering from highest to lowest does not actually matter at all
distractors.trap: They are distinct levels with distinct roles, not synonyms for one tier of the WBS.

distractors.text: The planning package is the highest point where cost and schedule are integrated for measurement, the work package is an undetailed placeholder beneath it, and the control account holds the finished detailed activities
distractors.trap: All three roles are shuffled; the control account is the integration point, the planning package is the placeholder, and the work package holds detail.

distractors.text: The work package integrates several control accounts together for measurement, while planning packages are the smallest units of fully detailed, schedulable work sitting at the very bottom of the entire structure
distractors.trap: It is the control account that integrates and the work package that is the lowest detailed unit, not the reverse.

distractors.text: The control account and the work package are essentially interchangeable terms for the same tier, and the planning package is simply the summary rollup that sits above both of them at the top of the hierarchy
distractors.trap: They are three distinct levels; the planning package is a lower placeholder, not a rollup above the others.

explanation: From highest to lowest: the control account integrates work for measurement, the planning package is a placeholder for future undetailed work beneath it, and the work package is the lowest level holding detailed schedulable activities.

prompts: On a RACI chart, several people are responsible for a task but only one name can be marked accountable. What is the difference being captured?
A PM building a responsibility assignment matrix notices three developers marked Responsible for a module while a single tech lead is marked Accountable. What distinction does this reflect?
In a marketing project's RACI, a team can share the Responsible role but the Accountable role is limited to one person. Which statement explains why?

correct.text: Responsibility concerns who actually performs the work, while accountability concerns who is answerable for the final outcome
correct.why: Many can do the work, but accountability for the result rests with a single answerable person.

correct.text: The responsible people are those doing the hands-on work, whereas the single accountable person is the one answerable for whether the result is delivered correctly
correct.why: Responsibility is the doing; accountability is answering for the outcome and is held by one person.

correct.text: Responsibility can be shared among everyone performing the task, but accountability is assigned to exactly one person who owns and answers for the final result
correct.why: Responsibility is divisible across doers; accountability is singular and answerable.

distractors.text: Responsibility means being answerable for the outcome, while accountability means doing the hands-on work of the task
distractors.trap: Reversed: doing the work is responsibility, and being answerable is accountability.

distractors.text: They are effectively identical roles, so it is perfectly valid for several different people to all be marked accountable together for one and the same single task.
distractors.trap: Synonym trap: accountability is singular and answerable, while responsibility can be shared.

distractors.text: Accountability applies only to managers, while responsibility applies only to individual team members below them
distractors.trap: Either role can be held at any level; the distinction is answerability versus performance, not rank.

distractors.text: Accountability can be divided among several people who each answer for their own slice of the outcome, while responsibility is the single named person who signs off on the completed deliverable
distractors.trap: This inverts both roles; accountability is singular sign-off and responsibility is the shareable doing of the work.

distractors.text: Responsibility is decided by the project sponsor and accountability is decided by the functional manager, so the two letters simply record which leader approved that particular assignment
distractors.trap: The letters describe doing versus answering for the work, not which leader authorized the assignment.

distractors.text: Both letters mark who reviews the work after it is finished, with responsibility used for internal reviewers and accountability reserved for the external customer's final sign-off
distractors.trap: Neither letter is about post-work review; responsibility is performing the work and accountability is ownership of the outcome.

explanation: Responsibility identifies who performs the work and can be shared, while accountability identifies the single person answerable for the outcome.

prompts: A project manager can write down the configuration steps in a procedure, but a seasoned engineer's intuition for spotting trouble resists being fully documented. Which pairing is correct?

A knowledge-management lead notes that installation instructions are easy to publish, yet a veteran technician's feel for a failing machine cannot be captured in a manual. Which statement pairs explicit and tacit knowledge correctly?
During lessons-learned, a PM contrasts the written database schema with a senior analyst's hard-to-explain instinct for bad data. Which option matches explicit and tacit knowledge to these correctly?

correct.text: Explicit knowledge can be documented and shared directly, while tacit knowledge resides in personal experience and is hard to formalize
correct.why: Explicit knowledge is codifiable and transferable in writing; tacit knowledge is personal, experiential, and difficult to capture.

correct.text: Explicit knowledge is the codifiable kind you can write into a procedure and hand off, whereas tacit knowledge lives in lived experience and is difficult to put into words
correct.why: Explicit is written and transferable; tacit is experiential and resists articulation.

correct.text: You can capture explicit knowledge in manuals, checklists, and databases, but tacit knowledge such as intuition and skill stays with the individual and resists full documentation
correct.why: Explicit is recordable; tacit is personal know-how that is hard to formalize.

distractors.text: Explicit knowledge resides in personal experience and resists being documented, while tacit knowledge is the kind that is easily written down, published, and shared across the whole team.
distractors.trap: Reversed: the codifiable type is explicit, and the experiential, hard-to-capture type is tacit.

distractors.text: Both explicit and tacit knowledge can be fully and completely captured in a single procedure document with equal ease and the same level of detail.
distractors.trap: Tacit knowledge specifically resists full documentation, which is why it is contrasted with explicit knowledge.

distractors.text: Explicit and tacit knowledge are really the same thing and differ just in the file format or software system used to store them, not in whether the knowledge itself can be articulated.
distractors.trap: Synonym trap: the difference is whether the knowledge can be articulated, not how it is stored.

distractors.text: Tacit knowledge is the codifiable kind stored in the lessons-learned register, while explicit knowledge is the intuitive expertise that is hard to transfer between team members.
distractors.trap: This swaps the two; explicit is the codifiable, transferable kind and tacit is the intuitive, hard-to-transfer kind.

distractors.text: Explicit knowledge belongs to senior staff and tacit knowledge to junior staff, making the real difference between them one of seniority and rank rather than whether it can be written down.
distractors.trap: The distinction is about articulability, not the seniority of the person who happens to hold the knowledge.

distractors.text: Both explicit and tacit knowledge transfer readily through a single training document, and a well-written procedure substitutes for hands-on mentoring, coaching, and lived experience.
distractors.trap: Tacit knowledge cannot be fully transferred in writing, which is exactly why mentoring and experience remain necessary.

explanation: Explicit knowledge is codifiable and can be documented and shared directly, while tacit knowledge lives in personal experience and is difficult to formalize.

prompts: Defects caught by the team before delivery versus defects found by the customer after delivery are categorized respectively as which two cost of quality types?
A software team fixes bugs it discovers during internal testing, and separately handles bugs users report after release. In cost of quality terms, these two categories are named what, in that order?
A factory scraps flawed parts found on its own line before shipping and later pays for warranty claims on parts customers received. Which cost of quality pair names these two situations in order?

correct.text: The team's pre-delivery defects are internal failure costs, and the customer's post-delivery defects are external failure costs
correct.why: Failures found before delivery are internal failure costs; those found by the customer after delivery are external failure costs.

correct.text: Defects caught in-house before shipping fall under internal failure costs, while defects the customer discovers after delivery fall under external failure costs
correct.why: Internal failure is pre-delivery; external failure is post-delivery.

correct.text: Reworking a defect found inside the company is an internal failure cost, whereas handling a defect the customer reports after release is an external failure cost
correct.why: The dividing line is whether the customer received the defect: before is internal, after is external.

distractors.text: The team's pre-delivery defects are external failure costs and the customer's post-delivery defects are internal failure costs, since anything caught inside the company is treated as external
distractors.trap: This reverses the order; pre-delivery failures are internal and post-delivery failures are external.

distractors.text: These are best classified as prevention costs and appraisal costs, the money spent up front on training and inspection to stop defects from ever occurring in the first place
distractors.trap: Both of these are conformance costs spent to avoid failures, not the cost of failures that have already occurred.

distractors.text: The correct pairing is appraisal costs for the in-house defects and warranty reserves for the customer-reported ones, treating inspection spending and set-aside funding as the two buckets
distractors.trap: Appraisal is inspection spending and a warranty reserve is a funding set-aside; neither names the failure categories asked about.

distractors.text: Both pre-delivery and post-delivery defects simply roll up into a single category called cost of nonconformance, so there is no meaningful distinction based on where the defect was discovered
distractors.trap: Cost of nonconformance is split precisely into internal and external failure by where the defect is found.

distractors.text: In-house defects are prevention costs because catching them early prevents escapes, and customer-found defects are appraisal costs because the customer effectively performs the final inspection
distractors.trap: Prevention and appraisal are conformance costs; defects that actually occur are failure costs, internal or external.

distractors.text: The team's defects are conformance costs and the customer's defects are opportunity costs, reflecting the lost future sales that tend to follow any defect that escapes to the end user
distractors.trap: These are internal and external failure costs; conformance and opportunity cost are different categories entirely.

distractors.text: Pre-delivery defects are external failure costs and post-delivery defects are appraisal costs, because inspection is assumed to continue into the customer's environment after delivery
distractors.trap: Pre-delivery failures are internal, post-delivery failures are external, and appraisal is inspection cost, not a failure category.

explanation: Defects found before delivery are internal failure costs, and defects found by the customer after delivery are external failure costs; both are costs of nonconformance.

prompts: Sensitivity analysis and Monte Carlo simulation are both quantitative techniques. Which statement correctly distinguishes them?

A risk analyst wants to know both which cost driver most affects the budget and what the overall probability of finishing under budget is. Which statement correctly separates what sensitivity analysis does from what Monte Carlo simulation does?

During quantitative risk analysis, a PM must explain the difference between a tornado-diagram technique and a many-iteration simulation. Which option distinguishes sensitivity analysis from Monte Carlo simulation correctly?

correct.text: Sensitivity analysis ranks which inputs move the outcome most, often on a tornado diagram, while Monte Carlo runs many iterations to model the combined probability distribution
correct.why: Sensitivity ranks which drivers matter most; Monte Carlo gives the full range and probability of combined results.

correct.text: Sensitivity analysis isolates the key drivers, while Monte Carlo simulation runs thousands of iterations to produce the full probability distribution
correct.why: One identifies the influential variables; the other models the distribution of overall outcomes.

correct.text: Sensitivity analysis isolates the variables with the greatest influence, often on a tornado diagram, whereas Monte Carlo repeatedly samples all the inputs to model the full range of possible outcomes
correct.why: Sensitivity highlights key drivers; Monte Carlo produces the outcome distribution across iterations.

distractors.text: Sensitivity analysis estimates the full probability distribution of total project outcomes across many thousands of iterations, while Monte Carlo simulation simply identifies the single most important variable that is driving the overall result
distractors.trap: Reversed - Monte Carlo produces the distribution and sensitivity analysis ranks the influential variables.

distractors.text: Sensitivity analysis varies many inputs together on each run to reveal their combined effect, while Monte Carlo simulation adjusts one variable at a time to isolate how much that single input drives the result
distractors.trap: Swapped - sensitivity analysis changes one input at a time, while Monte Carlo samples all inputs together.

distractors.text: Sensitivity analysis samples each input from a probability distribution, while Monte Carlo simulation tests a few fixed best-case and worst-case values to see which assumption changes the outcome the most
distractors.trap: Swapped - Monte Carlo draws inputs from probability distributions, while fixed best/worst-case testing is closer to sensitivity analysis.

distractors.text: Sensitivity analysis runs thousands of random iterations across the inputs to build a full probability distribution of the combined outcome, while Monte Carlo simulation simply ranks which single variable has the greatest influence and displays it on a tornado diagram
distractors.trap: This swaps them completely; Monte Carlo runs the iterations and sensitivity analysis produces the tornado diagram of drivers.

distractors.text: Sensitivity analysis is used for cost estimates while Monte Carlo simulation is used for schedule networks, with each technique tied to a separate knowledge area rather than to a different analytical purpose
distractors.trap: Both techniques apply to cost and schedule alike; they differ by analytical purpose, not by knowledge area.

distractors.text: Sensitivity analysis relies on thousands of randomly simulated trials to converge on an answer, while Monte Carlo simulation reaches its result in a single deterministic calculation pass over the base estimates
distractors.trap: Reversed - Monte Carlo is the many-trial simulation, while sensitivity analysis does not run randomized iterations.

explanation: Sensitivity analysis identifies which individual variables most affect the outcome (often via a tornado diagram), while Monte Carlo simulation runs many iterations to estimate the probability distribution of combined outcomes.

prompts: A team debates whether to continue a feature they have already invested heavily in. Which cost concept tells them that the money already spent should NOT influence the go-forward decision?

A hospital IT group has poured months of budget into a custom scheduling module and argues about whether to keep going. Which cost concept says the funds already consumed should not sway the decision to proceed?
A construction sponsor points to the large sum already paid for a delayed design and insists the project must continue to justify it. Which cost concept explains why that reasoning is flawed?

correct.text: Sunk cost - money already spent and unrecoverable should be excluded when deciding what to do next
correct.why: Sunk costs cannot be recovered, so a rational forward-looking decision weighs only future costs and benefits.

correct.text: Because the earlier investment can never be recovered, only future costs and benefits should drive the choice
correct.why: Rational choices ignore unrecoverable outlays and focus on incremental future value.

correct.text: Treat the already-spent funds as irrelevant and evaluate the decision purely on expected future value
correct.why: The label matters less than the principle: prior spend is a sunk cost and should not anchor the decision.

distractors.text: Opportunity cost - the value of the path the team already committed to must first be recovered before any further budget is approved
distractors.trap: Opportunity cost is the value of the best alternative forgone, not a reason to honor past spending.

distractors.text: Operating cost - the money spent so far will simply recur in every future period and therefore must be counted again in the go-forward decision
distractors.trap: Operating cost is the ongoing cost of running a delivered result, unrelated to ignoring prior investment.

distractors.text: Contingency reserve - the amount already spent should be reclassified as a planned reserve drawdown taken against the overall project budget
distractors.trap: Reclassifying spent money as reserve does not change that it is sunk; the concept being tested is sunk cost.

distractors.text: Return on investment - the team should keep funding the feature so that the original outlay eventually earns back a positive and measurable return
distractors.trap: Chasing ROI on unrecoverable spend is the sunk-cost fallacy; past outlay does not obligate future funding.

distractors.text: Escalation of commitment is fully justified here because abandoning the work now would waste absolutely everything the team has already invested so far
distractors.trap: This names the very bias to avoid; the fact that money is already gone is not a reason to keep spending.

distractors.text: Cost of quality - the money invested to date should be protected by driving the feature all the way through to full completion and release
distractors.trap: Cost of quality concerns conformance and defect prevention, not whether to honor prior investment.

distractors.text: Present value - the funds already committed should be discounted back to today and then carried forward as an input into the go-forward analysis
distractors.trap: Discounting applies to future cash flows; already-spent money has no present value to carry forward.

explanation: Sunk costs are unrecoverable and irrelevant to a forward-looking decision, which should weigh only future costs and benefits.

prompts: The performance measurement baseline and the cost baseline are related but not identical. What does the performance measurement baseline include that the cost baseline does not?
A program manager is explaining why the performance measurement baseline is broader than the cost baseline. Which element does the PMB integrate that the cost baseline leaves out?
During a review, a stakeholder asks how the performance measurement baseline differs from the cost baseline. What does the PMB add beyond planned expenditures?

correct.text: It integrates scope and schedule together with cost, whereas the cost baseline covers planned spending alone
correct.why: The PMB is the combined scope, schedule, and cost baseline used to gauge overall performance.

correct.text: The PMB combines the scope and schedule baselines with the cost baseline into one integrated measure
correct.why: Performance is judged against all three dimensions, not cost by itself.

correct.text: Beyond planned spending, it also incorporates the approved scope and schedule baselines
correct.why: The cost baseline is only the cost dimension; the PMB unifies all three.

distractors.text: It adds the management reserve, while the cost baseline leaves that buffer out of its total
distractors.trap: Management reserve sits outside the PMB too; the real difference is the integration of scope and schedule.

distractors.text: It covers just the cost dimension, while the cost baseline is the one that pulls scope, schedule, and cost together into a single measure
distractors.trap: Reversed - the PMB is the integrated baseline and the cost baseline is the narrower cost-only view.

distractors.text: Nothing of substance - the two are really interchangeable labels for the same underlying baseline document
distractors.trap: The PMB is broader than the cost baseline; they are not synonyms.

distractors.text: It folds in both the contingency reserve and the management reserve, which the cost baseline keeps separate from its totals
distractors.trap: Contingency reserve is already inside the cost baseline, and management reserve sits outside the PMB entirely.

distractors.text: It layers the project risk register and the quality metrics on top of the planned cost distribution spread over time
distractors.trap: The PMB integrates scope and schedule, not the risk register or quality metrics.

distractors.text: It expresses the periodic funding limits and cash-flow ceilings that the cost baseline itself does not capture
distractors.trap: Funding limits come from funding limit reconciliation, not from what distinguishes the PMB from the cost baseline.

distractors.text: It captures the actual costs incurred by the team to date, whereas the cost baseline holds the original approved spending plan
distractors.trap: Both are planned baselines; actuals are tracked separately and are not part of the PMB definition.

explanation: The performance measurement baseline integrates the scope, schedule, and cost baselines, while the cost baseline is only the cost dimension.

prompts: A quality team must decide whether to examine the deliverable itself or examine whether the team followed its defined processes. Which statement correctly pairs the technique to its focus?
A PMO lead must clarify for a new manager which technique checks whether the team followed its processes and which checks the finished deliverable. Which pairing is right?
On a manufacturing project, leadership wants to know whether to review process conformance or the physical product. Which statement correctly matches each technique to what it examines?

correct.text: An audit examines process compliance and control, while an inspection examines the product or deliverable itself
correct.why: Audits target conformance of processes; inspections target the conformance of the actual work product.

correct.text: Inspections look at the deliverable to verify it meets requirements, while audits verify the processes were followed
correct.why: The product is inspected; the process is audited.

correct.text: Use an audit to check that defined procedures were followed and an inspection to check the work product
correct.why: Same distinction: process conformance versus product conformance.

distractors.text: An audit examines the finished deliverable in detail, while an inspection examines process compliance and organizational control conformance
distractors.trap: Reversed: the inspection looks at the product, and the audit looks at process and control conformance.

distractors.text: Both an audit and an inspection focus on process conformance, checking that the team followed its defined procedures rather than examining the deliverable produced
distractors.trap: An inspection specifically examines the product or deliverable, not the process.

distractors.text: An audit measures the finished deliverable against its acceptance criteria, while an inspection reviews whether the team followed its defined quality procedures
distractors.trap: Reversed: measuring the deliverable against acceptance criteria is inspection, and process conformance is the audit.

distractors.text: An inspection reviews the supplier's financial and accounting records, while an audit measures the finished deliverable against its acceptance criteria
distractors.trap: Inspection is not a financial review, and measuring the deliverable against acceptance criteria is inspection, not audit.

distractors.text: An audit is a peer walkthrough of the work product, while an inspection is a management review of the project's schedule and overall health
distractors.trap: These conflate the terms with unrelated review types; the audit checks process conformance and the inspection examines the product.

distractors.text: An audit validates the finished product against its requirements, while an inspection samples the process to surface possible improvement ideas
distractors.trap: Reversed roles again: the product is inspected and the process is audited.

distractors.text: An audit confirms that the sponsor has formally accepted the deliverable, while an inspection checks that the team followed its defined processes
distractors.trap: Neither term means sponsor acceptance; the audit examines process and the inspection examines the product.

explanation: An audit examines whether processes and controls were followed; an inspection examines the actual product or deliverable.

prompts: A team debates two ideas: the conditions that must be met before a whole phase can be closed or allowed to proceed, versus the shared checklist confirming a single backlog increment is complete. Which pairing is correct?
An agile release train lead contrasts the gate that lets an entire phase close with the checklist that says one backlog increment is finished. Which pairing is correct?
A hybrid project defines when a whole stage may transition and, separately, when a single increment counts as complete. Which statement matches each term to its scope?

correct.text: Exit criteria govern readiness to complete or transition a broader body of work, while definition of done confirms a specific increment is complete
correct.why: Exit criteria operate at the phase or stage boundary, whereas definition of done applies to an individual increment.

correct.text: Definition of done applies to a single increment, while exit criteria decide whether a whole phase may close or proceed
correct.why: One is increment-level completion, the other is phase-level readiness.

correct.text: Exit criteria sit at the phase boundary, and definition of done sits at the level of one backlog item
correct.why: They operate at different scopes: stage transition versus increment completion.

distractors.text: Exit criteria are what confirm a single increment is complete, while the definition of done is what governs whole-phase transitions and stage-gate readiness
